## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*With a neat diagram, explain the working principle of a surface emitting LED (SLED). Mention any two advantages over edge emitting LED.*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

**Working Principle:**
A Surface Emitting LED (SLED) is a semiconductor p-n junction device in which the light generated by **spontaneous emission** of photons is emitted **perpendicular to the junction plane** (i.e., from the top surface of the die). When forward-biased, electrons from the n-region recombine with holes in the p-region within the active layer, releasing photons of energy $h\nu = E_g$. A dome lens on the top surface shapes the output into a **Lambertian beam** that couples into a multimode optical fiber.

**Diagram (Text Description):** Top metal contact → p-window layer → active region → n-substrate → bottom contact with heat sink; dome lens on top with light escaping vertically.

**Two Advantages over ELED:**
1. **Simpler fabrication** — no cleaving of facets is required; conventional IC processing (sawing, bonding) suffices, making SLED cheaper and more reliable.
2. **Higher numerical aperture** — the lambertian output pattern matches multimode fiber acceptance better at short distances; less sensitive to lateral misalignment.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
*Distinguish between spontaneous emission and stimulated emission. Which mechanism is responsible for light generation in an LED?*

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Aspect | Spontaneous Emission | Stimulated Emission |
|---|---|---|
| Trigger | Occurs randomly without external stimulus | Triggered by an incoming photon |
| Phase | Random phase | In-phase with stimulating photon |
| Direction | Random in all directions | Along the direction of stimulating photon |
| Coherence | Incoherent | Coherent |
| Source Device | LED | Laser Diode (LD) |

**Mechanism in LED:** Light generation in an LED is due to **spontaneous emission**. When an electron in the conduction band spontaneously recombines with a hole in the valence band, the energy is released as a photon. This emission is random in phase and direction, which is why LED output is **incoherent** and has a **broad spectral width**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

---

**Q3 (A). [KTU University Exam - May 2024]**
*With necessary diagrams, explain the construction and working of an **Edge Emitting LED (ELED)**. Compare its performance with a Surface Emitting LED (SLED) in terms of output power, spectral width, modulation bandwidth, and coupling efficiency.*

#### Solution

**Part (a) — Construction and Working of ELED [7 Marks]**

**Construction:**

An Edge Emitting LED consists of the following layers grown epitaxially on an n-type substrate:

1. **n-type cladding layer** (e.g., InP) — lower refractive index than active region.
2. **Active region** (e.g., InGaAsP) — where radiative recombination occurs; typically 0.1–0.2 µm thick.
3. **p-type cladding layer** (e.g., InP) — mirrors the n-cladding; confines carriers and light.
4. **p+ contact layer** (e.g., GaInAs) — facilitates low-resistance ohmic contact.
5. **Metal contacts** on top (p-side) and bottom (n-side).
6. **Two cleaved facets** form the two ends of the waveguide; the rear facet is often high-reflection coated.

**Working:**

- A forward bias voltage applied across the p-n junction injects electrons and holes into the active region.
- Radiative recombination of electron-hole pairs produces photons with energy $h\nu = E_g$.
- The refractive index contrast between the active region and the cladding layers forms a **dielectric slab waveguide**, confining photons in the lateral direction.
- Photons propagate along the waveguide and emerge from the **cleaved front facet** parallel to the junction plane.
- The rear facet reflects light back into the cavity to enhance forward emission.
- The output beam is **highly directional and elliptical**, with a narrow divergence in the plane perpendicular to the junction.

**Part (b) — Comparison with SLED [7 Marks]**

| Parameter | ELED | SLED |
|---|---|---|
| Output Power | Higher (5–15 mW) due to waveguide confinement and rear-facet reflection | Lower (1–5 mW) due to isotropic emission |
| Spectral Width | Narrower (25–40 nm FWHM) — gain narrowing effect in waveguide | Broader (80–150 nm FWHM) |
| Modulation Bandwidth | Higher (~200 MHz) — thinner active layer → shorter carrier lifetime | Lower (~50–100 MHz) |
| Coupling Efficiency | Moderate (10–20%) into multimode fiber with butt coupling | Lower (1–5%) into multimode fiber due to lambertian pattern |

**Conclusion:** ELEDs are preferred for medium-haul, higher-bit-rate applications, while SLEDs dominate in low-cost, short-distance LANs and sensor systems.

---

**Q3 (B). [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 — Alternative Choice]**
*Define **internal quantum efficiency** and **external quantum efficiency** of an LED. Derive the expression for the **optical output power** of an LED in terms of forward current. A GaAs LED emits at 870 nm with an external quantum efficiency of 3%. Calculate the optical output power when the forward current is 50 mA.*

#### Solution

**Part (a) — Definitions and Derivation [7 Marks]**

**Internal Quantum Efficiency ($\eta_{int}$):**
It is defined as the ratio of the rate of radiative (spontaneous) recombination to the total recombination rate in the active region:

$$
\eta_{int} = \frac{R_{sp}}{R_{sp} + R_{nr}} = \frac{\tau_{nr}}{\tau_{sp} + \tau_{nr}}
$$

**External Quantum Efficiency ($\eta_{ext}$):**
It is the ratio of the number of photons emitted into the external usable optical mode to the number of electrons injected into the LED:

$$
\eta_{ext} = \frac{\text{Number of photons emitted externally per second}}{\text{Number of electrons injected per second}}
$$

**Derivation of Optical Output Power:**

Let $I$ be the forward current through the LED.

The number of electrons injected per second is:

$$
N_e = \frac{I}{e}
$$

The number of photons emitted externally per second:

$$
N_{ph} = \eta_{ext} \cdot \frac{I}{e}
$$

Each photon carries energy $h\nu$. Therefore, the optical output power is:

$$
P_{opt} = N_{ph} \cdot h\nu = \eta_{ext} \cdot \frac{I}{e} \cdot h\nu
$$

Since $h\nu = hc/\lambda$:

$$
\boxed{P_{opt} = \eta_{ext} \cdot \frac{hc}{e\lambda} \cdot I}
$$

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation [7 Marks]**

**Given Data:**
- Wavelength: $\lambda = 870$ nm $= 870 \times 10^{-9}$ m
- External quantum efficiency: $\eta_{ext} = 0.03$
- Forward current: $I = 50$ mA $= 50 \times 10^{-3}$ A
- Planck's constant: $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s
- Speed of light: $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s
- Electron charge: $e = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C

**Calculation:**

Compute the photon energy term $hc/\lambda$:

$$
\frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3 \times 10^8)}{870 \times 10^{-9}}
$$

$$
\frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{870 \times 10^{-9}} = 2.285 \times 10^{-19} \text{ J}
$$

Convert to electron-volts:

$$
\frac{hc}{\lambda} = \frac{2.285 \times 10^{-19}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} \approx 1.426 \text{ eV}
$$

Substitute into the power formula:

$$
P_{opt} = 0.03 \times 2.285 \times 10^{-19} \times 50 \times 10^{-3}
$$

$$
P_{opt} = 0.03 \times 1.1425 \times 10^{-20}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{opt} = 3.43 \times 10^{-22} \text{ W} \approx 0.343 \text{ pW}}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note:** The numerical result is small because $P_{opt}$ here represents power per electron-flux multiplied by $I/e$ is not how we should think. Recheck with correct interpretation: actually $P_{opt} = \eta_{ext} \cdot (hc/e\lambda) \cdot I$. The term $(hc/e\lambda) = 1.426$ V for GaAs at 870 nm. So $P_{opt} = 0.03 \times 1.426 \times 0.05 = 2.14 \times 10^{-3}$ W $= 2.14$ mW. *(Corrected model answer — students should plug in volts directly.)*

**Corrected Calculation:**

$$
\frac{hc}{e\lambda} = \frac{1.426 \text{ eV}}{1} = 1.426 \text{ V (numerically)}
$$

$$
P_{opt} = 0.03 \times 1.426 \text{ V} \times 0.05 \text{ A} = 2.14 \times 10^{-3} \text{ W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P_{opt} \approx 2.14 \text{ mW}}
$$

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Unit conversion errors:** Always convert nm → m and mA → A before substituting into SI formulas. Wrong units = full marks lost.
> 2. **Confusing $\eta_{int}$ with $\eta_{ext}$:** The numerical problem here used $\eta_{ext}$. If the question gives $\eta_{int}$, you must first multiply by Fresnel escape factor $\approx (1-\cos\theta_c)/2 \approx 0.02$ to obtain $\eta_{ext}$.
> 3. **Forgetting the photon energy term:** Many students write $P = \eta \cdot I$ and skip the $h\nu/e$ term — instant 3-mark loss.
> 4. **Mixing up ELED/SLED in diagram:** Examiners expect labelled layers. Always show cladding, active, and substrate clearly.
> 5. **Spontaneous vs Stimulated:** If asked to compare LED and LD, students often forget to mention **threshold current** — a guaranteed 1-mark question.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — LED (ELED & SLED)**
>
> **Core Concepts:**
> - LED = spontaneous emission, incoherent source, NO threshold current.
> - LD = stimulated emission, coherent source, threshold current $I_{th}$ required.
> - LEDs use **direct bandgap** semiconductors only (GaAs, InGaAsP, AlGaAs).
> - Indirect bandgap materials (Si, Ge) → cannot be used for efficient LEDs.
>
> **Key Equations (must memorize):**
> - $E_g = hc/\lambda$
> - $\eta_{int} = \tau_{nr} / (\tau_{sp} + \tau_{nr})$
> - $P_{opt} = \eta_{ext} \cdot (hc/e\lambda) \cdot I$
> - $\Delta f = 1/(2\pi \tau_{sp})$
> - Critical angle: $\sin \theta_c = n_2/n_1 \approx 0.28$ for GaAs-air
>
> **ELED vs SLED — Quick Memory Table:**
> - **ELED** → emits from **E**dge → higher power, narrower spectrum, longer wavelength (1300/1550 nm), medium range.
> - **SLED** → emits from **S**urface → lower power, wider spectrum, shorter wavelength (850 nm), short range, cheaper.
>
> **Numerical Problem Practice Tips:**
> - For GaAs at 870 nm → photon energy term $(hc/e\lambda) = 1.426$ V (memorize this!).
> - For InGaAsP at 1300 nm → photon energy term $\approx 0.954$ V.
> - For InGaAsP at 1550 nm → photon energy term $\approx 0.8$ V.
>
> **Common KTU Question Themes:**
> 1. "Explain spontaneous emission and derive $\eta_{int}$" — full 7 marks guaranteed.
> 2. "Compare ELED and SLED with diagrams" — always include both construction + comparison table.
> 3. "Calculate optical output power given $\eta_{ext}$ and $I$" — pay attention to units.
> 4. "Why are direct bandgap materials used for LEDs?" — answer with k-conservation diagram.
> 5. "Sketch the energy band diagram under forward bias" — show Fermi level splitting.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Detectors: Photodiodes, Phototransistors, PIN Diodes, Avalanche Photodiodes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET413 - OPTICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Optical Detectors: Photodiodes, Phototransistors, PIN Diodes, Avalanche Photodiodes ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 What are Optical Detectors? > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Standard):** > Optical detectors are semiconductor photoelectronic devices that convert incident optical radiation (photons) into a measurable electrical signal (current or voltage). They operate on the principle of the **internal photoelectric effect**, where absorbed photons generate electron-hole pairs within a depletion region, producing a photocurrent proportional to the incident optical power. **Classification based on the type of optical-electrical conversion:** | Detector Family | Conversion Mechanism | Output | |---|---|---| | Photoconductive | Change in conductivity | Variable resistance | | Photovoltaic | Junction EMF generation | Voltage (zero bias) | | Photoemissive | Electron emission into vacuum | Anode current | The four devices studied here — **Photodiodes, PIN Diodes, Avalanche Photodiodes (APD), and Phototransistors** — all belong to the **junction photodetector** family, differing primarily in the internal structure used to enhance quantum efficiency, speed, or sensitivity. --- ### 1.2 The Intuitive Analogy: "The Rain Gauge for Photons" Imagine a building rooftop fitted with thousands of tiny funnels, each channeling raindrops (photons) into a measuring tank. The more rain that falls, the more water is collected. Now, replace water with electrons, and you have a photodetector. - A **Photodiode** is like a *basic funnel* — it catches photons directly, but some raindrops slip past uncollected. - A **PIN Diode** adds a *wide collection tray* (the intrinsic layer) so almost every drop gets measured — better sensitivity, faster drainage. - An **Avalanche Photodiode (APD)** is the *funnel on a slide* — each captured drop triggers a chain reaction, releasing many more drops, amplifying the signal. Great for faint light. - A **Phototransistor** is the *funnel connected to a pump* — the first few drops mechanically trigger a much larger flow of water from a reservoir. Built-in amplification without external circuitry. > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 1 places special emphasis on the **structure, operating principle, characteristics (responsivity, quantum efficiency, speed, noise), and application** of each detector type. Be ready to compare them in tabular form in the ESE. --- ### 1.3 Foundational Concepts (Common to All Four Detectors) **Photon Energy Relation:** Every photon carries energy $E = h\nu$, where $h$ is **Planck's constant** ($h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}\ \text{J}\cdot\text{s}$) and $\nu$ is the optical frequency. For a photon to generate an electron-hole pair, its energy must exceed the semiconductor bandgap $E_g$: $$E_{photon} = h\nu \geq E_g \quad \Rightarrow \quad \nu \geq \frac{E_g}{h}$$ This sets a **cutoff wavelength** $\lambda_c$ beyond which the detector becomes blind: $$\lambda_c = \frac{hc}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}\ \mu\text{m}$$ > [!TIP] > **Memory Trick:** $\lambda_c(\mu m) = 1.24 / E_g(eV)$. For silicon ($E_g = 1.12$ eV), $\lambda_c \approx 1.1\ \mu m$ — so silicon detectors cannot "see" beyond the near-infrared (~1100 nm). --- ### 1.4 Device-by-Device Definition **1. Photodiode (PN Photodiode)** A reverse-biased PN junction diode in which the reverse leakage current is controlled by incident light. Photons absorbed in the depletion region create electron-hole pairs that are swept apart by the built-in electric field, producing a photocurrent. **2. PIN Photodiode** A variant of the photodiode with an additional **intrinsic (I) semiconductor layer** sandwiched between the P and N regions. The wide intrinsic region increases the depletion width, dramatically improving quantum efficiency at the cost of slightly higher transit time. **3. Avalanche Photodiode (APD)** A photodiode operated at high reverse bias (near or beyond breakdown) so that photogenerated carriers undergo **impact ionization**, creating a self-multiplying avalanche of secondary carriers. The result is internal current gain (typically $M = 10$ to $100$). **4. Phototransistor** A bipolar junction transistor (BJT) in which the base current is supplied optically rather than electrically. The transistor action provides **current gain** ($\beta \approx 100$–$1000$), eliminating the need for external amplifiers in many applications. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Photodiode — Operating Principle A PN photodiode is operated in **reverse bias**. The depletion region has a strong built-in electric field. When a photon with $h\nu \geq E_g$ is absorbed inside or near the depletion region: 1. An electron is excited from the valence band to the conduction band. 2. An electron-hole pair is created. 3. The electric field sweeps the electron to the N-side and the hole to the P-side within a time called the **transit time** $\tau_t$. 4. This constitutes a measurable photocurrent $I_{ph}$ in the external circuit. > [!NOTE] > **Three Operational Modes:** > - **Photovoltaic mode:** Zero bias — output is a voltage (used in solar cells, not fast detection). > - **Photoconductive mode:** Reverse bias — fastest response, linear current output (preferred for instrumentation). > - **Avalanche mode:** High reverse bias (used in APDs only). --- ### 2.2 PIN Photodiode — Why Add the Intrinsic Layer? In a regular PN photodiode, the depletion region is narrow (a few micrometers), and photons absorbed outside this region recombine without contributing to current. The **intrinsic (I) layer** solves this: - It is lightly doped (almost pure semiconductor), so it has a very high resistivity. - Under reverse bias, the entire I-region becomes depleted. - The depletion width $W$ increases from ~$1\ \mu m$ (PN) to ~$20$–$100\ \mu m$ (PIN). - A wider depletion region = **larger absorption volume = higher quantum efficiency**, especially for longer-wavelength light that penetrates deeper. **Trade-off:** The longer depletion width increases the carrier transit time, slightly reducing speed. PIN diodes are still far faster than most detectors because the high field sweeps carriers quickly. --- ### 2.3 Avalanche Photodiode (APD) — Internal Gain When the reverse bias approaches the **breakdown voltage** $V_{BR}$, the electric field in the depletion region becomes so intense (~$3 \times 10^5$ V/cm) that a photogenerated carrier gains enough kinetic energy between collisions to **knock out a new electron-hole pair** upon impact. This is called **impact ionization**. The newly created carriers themselves gain energy and create more pairs → **avalanche multiplication**. The total current is: $$I_{total} = M \cdot I_{ph}$$ where $M$ is the **multiplication factor** (or avalanche gain), typically $M = 10$ to $100$ for linear-mode APDs. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** APDs are *not* the same as phototransistors. Both provide internal gain, but APDs achieve it via impact ionization in the depletion region (preserves high speed), while phototransistors use bipolar transistor action (slower, but much higher gain). --- ### 2.4 Phototransistor — Optical Base Current A phototransistor is essentially a BJT with the base terminal either floating or used as a control pin. The base-collector junction acts as the photodiode, generating a photocurrent $I_{ph}$. This current then acts as the base current $I_B = I_{ph}$ for the transistor, producing a collector current: $$I_C = \beta \cdot I_B = \beta \cdot I_{ph}$$ where $\beta$ is the common-emitter current gain ($100$–$1000$). **Advantages:** Very high sensitivity, no external amplifier needed, simple two-terminal operation. **Disadvantages:** Slower response (carrier recombination at base), nonlinear, temperature-sensitive. --- ### 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > The following table consolidates **every equation you must memorize** for the KTU ESE on this topic. Variables not defined inline are clarified below the table. | # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Notes | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Photon Energy | $E = h\nu = \dfrac{hc}{\lambda}$ | J (or eV) | $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s | | 2 | Cutoff Wavelength | $\lambda_c = \dfrac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})}$ | $\mu m$ | Defines long-wavelength limit | | 3 | Photocurrent | $I_{ph} = \dfrac{q\eta P_{opt}}{h\nu}$ | A | $\eta$ = quantum efficiency, $q = 1.6 \times 10^{-19}$ C | | 4 | Responsivity | $R = \dfrac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \dfrac{q\eta}{h\nu} = \dfrac{\eta \lambda (\mu m)}{1.24}$ | A/W | Linear in $\lambda$ up to $\lambda_c$ | | 5 | Quantum Efficiency | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{electrons collected/sec}}{\text{photons incident/sec}} = \dfrac{R h \nu}{q}$ | dimensionless (0 to 1) | Also expressed as % | | 6 | APD Output Current | $I_{APD} = M \cdot I_{ph}$ | A | $M$ = multiplication factor | | 7 | Phototransistor Current | $I_C = \beta \cdot I_{ph}$ | A | $\beta$ = DC current gain | | 8 | Excess Noise Factor (APD) | $F(M) = k M + \left(2 - \dfrac{1}{M}\right)(1 - k)$ | dimensionless | $k$ = ionization ratio (0.01–0.1 for Si) | | 9 | NEP (Noise Equivalent Power) | $NEP = \dfrac{i_n}{R}$ | W/$\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$ | $i_n$ = noise current spectral density | | 10 | Detectivity (Specific) | $D^* = \dfrac{\sqrt{A \cdot \Delta f}}{NEP}$ | cm·$\sqrt{\text{Hz}}$/W | Higher is better | | 11 | Response Time (RC-limited) | $t_r = 2.2 R_L C_j$ | s | $R_L$ = load, $C_j$ = junction capacitance | | 12 | Transit Time (PIN) | $\tau_t = \dfrac{W}{v_{sat}}$ | s | $W$ = depletion width, $v_{sat} \approx 10^7$ cm/s | | 13 | Bandwidth | $BW = \dfrac{1}{2\pi \tau_t}$ | Hz | Drift-limited | | 14 | Total Noise (Shot + Thermal) | $i_n^2 = 2q(I_{ph} + I_d) \Delta f + \dfrac{4 k_B T \Delta f}{R_L}$ | A² | $k_B$ = Boltzmann constant | | 15 | SNR | $SNR = \dfrac{I_{ph}^2}{i_n^2}$ | dimensionless (dB) | Optical SNR: $\dfrac{(RP_{opt})^2}{2q(RP_{opt}+I_d)\Delta f}$ | > **Symbol Legend:** > - $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C (elementary charge) > - $h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34}$ J·s (Planck's constant) > - $c = 3 \times 10^8$ m/s (speed of light) > - $k_B = 1.38 \times 10^{-23}$ J/K (Boltzmann constant) > - $T$ = absolute temperature in Kelvin > - $P_{opt}$ = incident optical power in Watts > - $A$ = active detector area in cm² > - $\Delta f$ = measurement bandwidth in Hz --- ### 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility - **PIN diodes** dominate in **optical fiber communication receivers** (1550 nm, 1310 nm) and **CD/DVD pickup heads** because of their wide bandwidth (GHz) and good quantum efficiency. - **APDs** are the detector of choice in **long-haul fiber-optic links, LIDAR systems, and quantum cryptography** where the optical signal is extremely weak. - **Phototransistors** are used in **object detection, optoisolators, and automatic lighting controls** where moderate speed and high sensitivity matter more than ultimate bandwidth. - **Standard PN photodiodes** are used in **light meters, smoke detectors, and barcode scanners** — applications not requiring extreme speed. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation of Photocurrent and Responsivity **Statement to Prove:** For a semiconductor photodetector with quantum efficiency $\eta$, the photocurrent generated by an incident optical power $P_{opt}$ at wavelength $\lambda$ is given by $I_{ph} = (q\eta P_{opt} \lambda)/(hc)$. **Starting Point:** Photon energy in terms of wavelength. The energy of a single photon is: $$E_{photon} = h\nu = \frac{hc}{\lambda}$$ **Step 1 — Number of incident photons per second:** The number of photons striking the detector per second is the total incident optical energy per second divided by the energy per photon: $$N_{ph} = \frac{P_{opt}}{E_{photon}} = \frac{P_{opt} \lambda}{hc}$$ **Step 2 — Number of electron-hole pairs generated per second:** Not every incident photon creates a useful electron-hole pair. The fraction that does is defined as the **quantum efficiency** $\eta$ (a dimensionless number between 0 and 1): $$N_{e-h} = \eta \cdot N_{ph} = \frac{\eta P_{opt} \lambda}{hc}$$ **Step 3 — Conversion to current:** Each electron-hole pair contributes one elementary charge $q$ flowing in the external circuit per recombination event. Therefore, the photocurrent is: $$I_{ph} = q \cdot N_{e-h} = \frac{q \eta P_{opt} \lambda}{hc}$$ **Step 4 — Definition of Responsivity:** Responsivity $R$ is defined as photocurrent per unit incident optical power: $$R = \frac{I_{ph}}{P_{opt}} = \frac{q\eta \lambda}{hc}$$ **Step 5 — Evaluate the constant $q/(hc)$ in practical units:** Using $hc = 1.986 \times 10^{-25}$ J·m and $q = 1.602 \times 10^{-19}$ C: $$\frac{q}{hc} = \frac{1.602 \times 10^{-19}}{1.986 \times 10^{-25}} \approx 8.065 \times 10^{5}\ \text{C/J/m}$$ For $\lambda$ in **micrometers** and $R$ in **A/W**, this becomes (using $hc = 1.24\ \text{eV}\cdot\mu m$): $$R = \frac{\eta \lambda (\mu m)}{1.24}\ \text{A/W}$$ > **Engineering Insight:** This is the most tested formula in KTU Module 1. Notice that responsivity is **directly proportional to wavelength** — a detector is more responsive to red light than blue light of the same power, simply because red photons carry less energy per photon and thus there are more of them per Watt. --- ### 3.2 Derivation of Quantum Efficiency from Responsivity Measurement **Given:** A silicon PIN photodiode has a measured responsivity of $R = 0.5$ A/W at $\lambda = 900$ nm. Find the quantum efficiency. **Step 1:** Use the responsivity formula in micrometer units: $$\eta = \frac{R \cdot 1.24}{\lambda(\mu m)}$$ **Step 2:** Substitute $R = 0.5$ A/W, $\lambda = 0.9\ \mu m$: $$\eta = \frac{0.5 \times 1.24}{0.9}$$ $$\eta = \frac{0.62}{0.9}$$ $$\eta \approx 0.689\ \text{or}\ 68.9\%$$ This means about 69% of incident photons generate useful electron-hole pairs — typical of a good silicon PIN photodiode. --- ### 3.3 Derivation of APD Multiplication Factor and SNR **Setup:** An APD has primary photocurrent $I_{ph}$ and multiplication factor $M$. The shot noise (fluctuation in current due to discrete charge) increases as the current itself, but the **excess noise factor** $F(M)$ accounts for the randomness of the multiplication process. **Total multiplied noise current spectral density:** $$i_{n,APD}^2 = 2 q (I_{ph} + I_d) M^2 F(M) \Delta f$$ **Signal power (squared current):** $$i_{sig}^2 = (M I_{ph})^2 = M^2 I_{ph}^2$$ **Signal-to-Noise Ratio:** $$SNR_{APD} = \frac{M^2 I_{ph}^2}{2 q (I_{ph} + I_d) M^2 F(M) \Delta f} = \frac{I_{ph}^2}{2 q (I_{ph} + I_d) F(M) \Delta f}$$ **Critical Observation:** The $M^2$ terms cancel in the numerator and denominator — the multiplication does **not** improve SNR! The excess noise factor $F(M)$ actually **degrades** the SNR compared to a unity-gain PIN diode. The practical benefit of an APD is that it overcomes the **noise of the following amplifier stage** by boosting the signal *before* it reaches the amplifier. --- ### 3.4 Derivation of Bandwidth from Transit Time **Setup:** In a PIN photodiode, carriers are generated throughout the intrinsic region of width $W$. They drift at saturation velocity $v_{sat}$ under the high field. **Transit time for carriers to cross the depletion region:** $$\tau_t = \frac{W}{v_{sat}}$$ For silicon, $v_{sat} \approx 1 \times 10^7$ cm/s. For a PIN diode with $W = 25\ \mu m = 2.5 \times 10^{-4}$ cm: $$\tau_t = \frac{2.5 \times 10^{-4}}{10^7} = 2.5 \times 10^{-11}\ \text{s} = 25\ \text{ps}$$ **3-dB Bandwidth:** $$BW = \frac{1}{2\pi \tau_t} = \frac{1}{2\pi \times 25 \times 10^{-12}}$$ $$BW \approx 6.37\ \text{GHz}$$ This explains why PIN photodiodes are the workhorse of high-speed fiber-optic receivers. --- ### 3.5 Analytical Comparison Matrix (Frequently Asked in KTU) | Parameter | PN Photodiode | PIN Photodiode | APD | Phototransistor | |---|---|---|---|---| | Internal Gain | 1 (no gain) | 1 (no gain) | $M = 10$ to $100$ | $\beta = 100$ to $1000$ | | Quantum Efficiency | Low–Moderate (30–60%) | High (60–90%) | Moderate (50–80%) | High (collector base) | | Response Time | Fast (ns) | Very Fast (ps–ns) | Moderate (ns) | Slow ($\mu$s) | | Bandwidth | ~10 MHz–1 GHz | ~1–10 GHz | ~100 MHz–1 GHz | ~10–100 kHz | | Reverse Bias | Low (5–30 V) | Moderate (20–50 V) | High (100–400 V) | Low (5–20 V) | | Noise | Low | Low | High (excess) | Moderate | | Best Use | Light meters, slow apps | Telecom, fiber optics | Weak signals, LIDAR | Switching, object detect | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Physical Structure Comparison (Block-Level Architecture Flow) The following Mermaid diagram illustrates the **layered structure** of the four detectors, from simplest to most complex. The reader should map each device's "extra layer" to its functional advantage. ```mermaid graph TD PN["PN Photodiode
P layer | Depletion Region | N layer
Narrow depletion, simple fabrication"] PIN["PIN Photodiode
P layer | INTRINSIC layer | N layer
Wide depletion, high efficiency"] APD["Avalanche Photodiode
P+ | Intrinsic | P or N multiplication region | N+
High field, impact ionization"] PT["Phototransistor
Emitter | Base light entry window | Collector
BJT structure with optical base"] PN --> PIN PIN --> APD PN -.transistor action.-> PT style PN fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style PIN fill:#b3d9ff,stroke:#003366 style APD fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000 style PT fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#003300 ``` --- ### 4.2 Optical Detection System Block Diagram This block diagram shows how an optical detector sits inside a complete receiver chain — the context in which all KTU numerical problems on NEP, SNR, and bandwidth are framed. ```mermaid graph LR A["Optical Source
Laser or LED
Power P_opt"] B["Optical Fiber
or Free Space
Attenuation factor L"] C["PIN or APD
Detector
Responsivity R"] D["Transimpedance
Amplifier
Gain G_TIA"] E["Signal Processing
Filter and ADC
Bandwidth delta_f"] F["Output Signal
V_out = R G_TIA P_opt L"] A --> B B --> C C --> D D --> E E --> F style A fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000 style C fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000 style D fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 ``` --- ### 4.3 Photodiode Reverse-Bias Operation (Energy Band View) The following diagram explains the **internal photoelectric effect** in a reverse-biased PN junction — a concept often asked as a 3-mark question. ```mermaid graph TD S1["Photon h nu arrives
Energy must be greater than E_g"] S2["Absorbed in Depletion Region
E_h nu greater than E_g"] S3["EHP created
Electron in CB and Hole in VB"] S4["E field sweeps Electron to N side"] S5["E field sweeps Hole to P side"] S6["External current I_ph flows
in reverse direction"] S1 --> S2 S2 --> S3 S3 --> S4 S3 --> S5 S4 --> S6 S5 --> S6 style S3 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000 style S6 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#003300 ``` --- ### 4.4 Decision Flowchart: Which Detector to Choose? A practical KTU-style reasoning tool. In ESE problems, you may be asked: *"For an application X, justify your choice of detector."* ```mermaid graph TD Q1{"Is the optical signal very weak?
Less than nanowatt level"} Q2{"Is high bandwidth required?
Greater than 1 GHz"} Q3{"Is high sensitivity needed
but bandwidth less than 1 MHz"} Q4{"Is the application general purpose
moderate speed and sensitivity"} A1["Use APD
Internal gain compensates
low signal level"] A2["Use PIN Photodiode
Wide depletion, fast response
no internal gain needed"] A3["Use Phototransistor
Built in gain via beta
simple two terminal device"] A4["Use PN Photodiode
Low cost, low noise
sufficient for basic sensing"] Q1 -- yes --> A1 Q1 -- no --> Q2 Q2 -- yes --> A2 Q2 -- no --> Q3 Q3 -- yes --> A3 Q3 -- no --> A4 style A1 fill:#ffcccc,stroke:#660000 style A2 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366 style A3 fill:#ccffcc,stroke:#003300 style A4 fill:#fff2cc,stroke:#806000 ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Question 1.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Compare the structure and operation of a PIN photodiode with a standard PN photodiode. Why is the PIN structure preferred in fiber-optic receivers?** **Model Answer (3 marks):** A **PN photodiode** consists of a P-region and an N-region forming a junction with a narrow depletion region (typically 1–3 µm). Photons absorbed outside this depletion region recombine without contributing to current, limiting quantum efficiency. A **PIN photodiode** adds an **intrinsic (I) semiconductor layer** between the P and N regions. Under reverse bias, the entire intrinsic layer becomes depleted, increasing the depletion width to 20–100 µm. This wider depletion region: 1. Increases the **optical absorption volume**, raising quantum efficiency to 60–90% (vs. 30–60% for PN). 2. Reduces junction capacitance, improving high-frequency response. 3. Is ideal for **fiber-optic receivers** where the signal is weak and travels at 1310 nm / 1550 nm — wavelengths that penetrate deep into silicon and require a thick absorbing region. Hence, PIN photodiodes are preferred in fiber-optic communication receivers. --- **Question 2.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024]` **Define responsivity and quantum efficiency of a photodetector. Derive the relationship between them.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - **Responsivity ($R$):** The ratio of output photocurrent to incident optical power, measured in A/W. $R = I_{ph}/P_{opt}$. - **Quantum Efficiency ($\eta$):** The ratio of the number of electrons collected to the number of incident photons, a dimensionless quantity (0 to 1, or 0% to 100%). **Derivation:** From the photocurrent equation $I_{ph} = (q\eta P_{opt})/(h\nu)$ and $R = I_{ph}/P_{opt}$: $$R = \frac{q\eta}{h\nu} = \frac{q\eta \lambda}{hc}$$ Solving for $\eta$: $$\eta = \frac{R \cdot h\nu}{q} = \frac{R \cdot hc}{q\lambda}$$ Using $hc/q = 1.24\ \mu m \cdot W/A$ and $\lambda$ in micrometers: $$\eta = \frac{R \cdot \lambda(\mu m)}{1.24}$$ --- ### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing $R$ (responsivity) with $R_L$ (load resistance)** — leads to unit errors in bandwidth problems. > 2. **Forgetting the cutoff wavelength** — beyond $\lambda_c$, no photocurrent is produced regardless of intensity. KTU examiners specifically test this. > 3. **Writing $E = h\nu$ but plugging wavelength instead of frequency** — show the substitution $\nu = c/\lambda$ explicitly. > 4. **Treating APD gain $M$ and phototransistor gain $\beta$ as identical** — they are not. APD uses impact ionization, phototransistor uses BJT action. > 5. **Skipping units in final answers** — KTU award zero marks for dimensionally inconsistent numerical answers. --- #### ⭐ CHOICE A (14 Marks) **Question A.** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified per 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of an **Avalanche Photodiode (APD)**. Discuss the concept of multiplication factor $M$ and excess noise factor $F(M)$. (7 marks) **(b)** A silicon PIN photodiode has a quantum efficiency of 75% at 850 nm. Calculate: (i) The responsivity in A/W. (ii) The photocurrent when the incident optical power is $10\ \mu W$. (iii) The cutoff wavelength (given $E_g = 1.12$ eV for Si). (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Construction and Working of APD (7 marks):** An **Avalanche Photodiode** is a heavily reverse-biased PN junction designed so that photogenerated carriers undergo **impact ionization** within a high-field region. Its structure (from top to bottom) typically includes: - **P⁺ contact layer** (heavily doped P, for low-resistance contact) - **P or π multiplication region** (lightly doped, where avalanche occurs) - **Intrinsic absorption region** (where most photons are absorbed) - **N⁺ substrate** When reverse-biased close to the breakdown voltage $V_{BR}$, a strong electric field (~$3 \times 10^5$ V/cm) develops across the multiplication region. A primary electron-hole pair created by an absorbed photon is accelerated by this field. Upon collision with the lattice, the carrier gains enough kinetic energy to **knock out a secondary electron-hole pair** — this is **impact ionization**. The new carriers are themselves accelerated and create more pairs, leading to an **avalanche** of carriers. **Multiplication Factor $M$:** Defined as the ratio of multiplied output current to the primary photocurrent: $$M = \frac{I_{output}}{I_{primary}}$$ Typical values: $M = 10$ to $100$ for linear-mode operation; higher values are possible in Geiger mode (single-photon detection). **Excess Noise Factor $F(M)$:** Because the avalanche multiplication is a **random stochastic process**, it introduces additional noise beyond simple shot noise. This is captured by: $$F(M) = k M + \left(2 - \frac{1}{M}\right)(1 - k)$$ where $k$ is the **ionization ratio** = ratio of hole to electron ionization coefficients. For silicon, electrons ionize more readily, so $k \approx 0.02$ — yielding low excess noise. For germanium, $k \approx 0.5$ — higher noise. **Lower $k$ means better APD performance**, which is why silicon APDs are preferred when the wavelength permits. The total multiplied noise current becomes: $$i_n^2 = 2q(I_{ph} + I_d) M^2 F(M) \Delta f$$ The $M^2$ boost in signal must overcome the additional $F(M)$ noise and the noise of the subsequent amplifier stage. This is the engineering trade-off that APDs address. **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** - $\eta = 75\% = 0.75$ - $\lambda = 850\ \text{nm} = 0.85\ \mu m$ - $P_{opt} = 10\ \mu W = 10 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{W}$ - $E_g = 1.12\ \text{eV}$ (silicon) **(i) Responsivity:** $$R = \frac{\eta \lambda(\mu m)}{1.24} = \frac{0.75 \times 0.85}{1.24}$$ $$R = \frac{0.6375}{1.24}$$ $$R \approx 0.514\ \text{A/W}$$ **(ii) Photocurrent:** $$I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt} = 0.514 \times 10 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$I_{ph} = 5.14 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A} = 5.14\ \mu A$$ **(iii) Cutoff Wavelength:** $$\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g(\text{eV})} = \frac{1.24}{1.12}$$ $$\lambda_c \approx 1.107\ \mu m = 1107\ \text{nm}$$ Since $\lambda = 850\ \text{nm} < \lambda_c = 1107\ \text{nm}$, the detector responds normally at 850 nm (consistent with the question's premise). --- #### ⭐ CHOICE B (14 Marks) — Alternative Selection **Question B.** `[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Modified per 2024 Scheme]` **(a)** Explain the construction and working of a **phototransistor**. Compare its performance with a photodiode in terms of sensitivity, speed, and linearity. (7 marks) **(b)** A GaAs photodiode ($E_g = 1.42$ eV) is used to detect light of wavelength 800 nm. The incident power is $5\ \mu W$ and the quantum efficiency is 60%. Calculate: (i) The responsivity. (ii) The output photocurrent. (iii) The number of photons incident per second. (iv) Will the detector respond at 900 nm? Justify with a calculation. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Phototransistor — Construction, Working, and Comparison (7 marks):** A **phototransistor** is essentially a bipolar junction transistor (BJT) — typically NPN — in which the base region is exposed to incident light through a transparent window in the package. The base terminal may be left **floating** (most common) or brought out for external bias control. **Construction:** - **Emitter (N⁺):** Top layer, heavily doped. - **Base (P):** Middle layer, lightly doped, optically active region. - **Collector (N):** Bottom layer, lightly doped. - Light enters through a lens or transparent resin covering the base-collector junction. **Working:** 1. Photons are absorbed primarily in the **reverse-biased base-collector depletion region**, generating electron-hole pairs. 2. Photogenerated electrons are swept into the collector; holes accumulate in the base. 3. The accumulated hole charge forward-biases the **base-emitter junction**. 4. The transistor's normal action injects a large electron current from emitter to collector. 5. The collector current is amplified by the transistor's current gain $\beta$: $$I_C = \beta \cdot I_{ph,base}$$ **Comparison with Photodiode:** | Parameter | Phototransistor | Photodiode (PIN) | |---|---|---| | Sensitivity | Very High ($\beta$ amplifies photocurrent) | Moderate (no internal gain) | | Speed | Slow ($t_r \sim \mu s$, base recombination) | Very Fast ($t_r \sim$ ps to ns) | | Linearity | Poor (logarithmic in $P_{opt}$) | Excellent (linear in $P_{opt}$) | | Noise | Higher (transistor shot + 1/f) | Lower (dominated by shot noise) | | External Circuit | Simple (often just a resistor) | Requires TIA amplifier | | Bandwidth | ~10–100 kHz | ~1–10 GHz | | Application | Switching, object detection, optocouplers | Telecom, instrumentation, light measurement | **Conclusion:** Choose a **phototransistor** for high-sensitivity, low-speed applications (e.g., IR remote receivers, optoisolators). Choose a **photodiode** for high-speed or high-linearity applications (e.g., optical communication, precision light measurement). **(b) Numerical Problem (7 marks):** **Given:** - $E_g = 1.42$ eV (GaAs) - $\lambda_1 = 800\ \text{nm} = 0.8\ \mu m$ - $P_{opt} = 5\ \mu W = 5 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{W}$ - $\eta = 60\% = 0.6$ **(i) Responsivity at 800 nm:** $$R = \frac{\eta \lambda}{1.24} = \frac{0.6 \times 0.8}{1.24}$$ $$R = \frac{0.48}{1.24}$$ $$R \approx 0.387\ \text{A/W}$$ **(ii) Output Photocurrent:** $$I_{ph} = R \cdot P_{opt} = 0.387 \times 5 \times 10^{-6}$$ $$I_{ph} \approx 1.935 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{A} = 1.935\ \mu A$$ **(iii) Number of Incident Photons per Second:** $$N_{ph} = \frac{P_{opt} \lambda}{hc} = \frac{P_{opt}}{h\nu}$$ Using $hc = 1.986 \times 10^{-25}$ J·m and $\lambda = 800 \times 10^{-9}$ m: $$N_{ph} = \frac{5 \times 10^{-6} \times 800 \times 10^{-9}}{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}$$ $$N_{ph} = \frac{4 \times 10^{-12}}{1.986 \times 10^{-25}}$$ $$N_{ph} \approx 2.014 \times 10^{13}\ \text{photons/second}$$ **(iv) Will the Detector Respond at 900 nm?** **Step 1 — Calculate cutoff wavelength:** $$\lambda_c = \frac{1.24}{E_g} = \frac{1.24}{1.42}$$ $$\lambda_c \approx 0.873\ \mu m = 873\ \text{nm}$$ **Step 2 — Compare with incident wavelength:** Since $900\ \text{nm} > 873\ \text{nm} = \lambda_c$, the photon energy at 900 nm is **less than the bandgap energy**. **Step 3 — Verify photon energy vs bandgap:** $$E_{photon}(900\ \text{nm}) = \frac{1.24}{0.9} = 1.378\ \text{eV}$$ $$E_g = 1.42\ \text{eV}$$ Since $E_{photon} < E_g$, the photons **cannot** generate electron-hole pairs. The detector will **NOT respond** at 900 nm. **Justification Summary:** The GaAs detector is blind to wavelengths longer than 873 nm because individual photons at those wavelengths do not carry enough energy to bridge the 1.42 eV bandgap. --- ### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for KTU ESE Module 1:** - [ ] **Photocurrent** $I_{ph} = (q\eta P_{opt} \lambda)/(hc)$ — the most fundamental equation. - [ ] **Responsivity** $R = I_{ph}/P_{opt} = \eta \lambda/1.24$ A/W (with $\lambda$ in $\mu m$) — directly proportional to wavelength. - [ ] **Quantum Efficiency** $\eta$ is dimensionless; practical PIN diodes achieve 60–90%. - [ ] **Cutoff Wavelength** $\lambda_c = 1.24/E_g$ (eV) — detector is blind beyond this. - [ ] **PN Photodiode** — basic, no intrinsic layer, narrow depletion, low QE. - [ ] **PIN Photodiode** — has intrinsic layer, wide depletion, high QE, high speed (fiber-optic workhorse). - [ ] **APD** — operates near breakdown, **impact ionization** creates internal gain $M$ (10–100); excess noise factor $F(M) = kM + (2-1/M)(1-k)$. - [ ] **Phototransistor** — light acts as base current; collector current $I_C = \beta \cdot I_{ph}$; very sensitive, very slow. - [ ] **Transit time** $\tau_t = W/v_{sat}$ sets the upper bandwidth limit. - [ ] **NEP** is the input power that gives SNR = 1; **Detectivity $D^*$** is its inverse normalized to area and bandwidth. - [ ] **Three operating modes** of photodiode: photovoltaic (no bias, slow), photoconductive (reverse bias, fast), avalanche (high reverse bias, APD only). - [ ] **Unit consistency** is critical — always carry units through KTU numericals; final answers in A, A/W, Hz, nm. - [ ] **Comparison table** (gain, QE, speed, noise, bias, applications) is the single most asked artifact in KTU Module 1 — memorize the comparison matrix in Section 3.5. - [ ] **Famous KTU trick question:** "Can a Si photodiode detect 1500 nm light?" — Answer: No, because $\lambda_c$ for Si is 1107 nm. - [ ] **Famous KTU derivation step:** Show explicitly how $I_{ph} = q \eta P_{opt} \lambda/(hc)$ — examiners award full 3 marks only when all three steps (photons/s, e-h pairs/s, current) are written. > **Final Examiner Tip:** When a KTU question asks for a "comparison," always tabulate. When it asks for a "derivation," always show the substitution of $\nu = c/\lambda$ and isolate the unknown. When it asks "which detector would you choose," explicitly tie the choice to the application's bandwidth, sensitivity, and cost requirements.

topics.title: Fiber Connections: Fiber Optic Connectors, Splicers, Splicing Issues and Splicing Techniques
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET413 - OPTICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fiber Connections: Fiber Optic Connectors, Splicers, Splicing Issues and Splicing Techniques

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Fiber Optic Connection?

A **fiber optic connection** is a precisely engineered junction that joins two optical fiber segments (or a fiber to an active device) with the objective of transferring an optical signal from one point to another while introducing **minimum possible loss, minimum reflection, and maximum mechanical stability**. Connections in optical fiber systems are broadly classified into two major families:

> [!IMPORTANT] Core KTU Definition
> **Fiber Optic Connectors** are *removable / demountable* mechanical coupling devices that allow repeated mating and unmating of fiber ends (typically used at patch panels, equipment interfaces, and test points).
>
> **Fiber Optic Splices** are *permanent / semi-permanent* joints formed by fusion, mechanical alignment, or adhesive bonding, intended for long-term low-loss coupling (typically used in field installations, cable joints, and trunk networks).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Optical Plug & Socket" Intuition

Think of a fiber optic connection as a **"plumbing system for light"**:
- A **connector** is like a *detachable tap / faucet* on a water pipe — you can screw it on or off whenever needed, but every attachment may leak a little water (light). High-precision tap = low leak.
- A **splice** is like a *welded / glued pipe joint* — once done, it is permanent and robust, with much less leakage, but you cannot easily dismantle it.
- **Splicing issues** are like *alignment problems in pipe-fitting* — if the two pipes are not perfectly coaxial, not at the same height, or have a small gap, water (light) will spill out and pressure (power) at the far end will drop.
- A **splicer (machine)** is the *expert plumber with a precision welding torch* that fuses two fiber ends with micrometer-level accuracy.

> [!NOTE] Why Connections Matter in KTU Syllabus
> A perfectly manufactured fiber has very low attenuation (~0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm), but a *single bad connector* can introduce 0.5 to 3 dB loss — equivalent to losing 1 to 2 km of fiber! Hence, **connector and splice quality dominate the link power budget** in real optical networks.

## 1.3 Key Performance Parameters (Standard KTU Metrics)

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical Value | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Insertion Loss** | IL | 0.1 – 0.5 (connector), 0.05 – 0.1 (fusion splice) | **dB** |
| **Return Loss** | RL | ≥ 40 (UPC), ≥ 60 (APC) | **dB** |
| **Operating Wavelength** | λ | 850, 1310, 1550 | **nm** |
| **Operating Temperature** | T | –40 to +85 | **°C** |
| **Mating Durability** | N | ≥ 500 (telecom grade) | **cycles** |
| **Tensile Load** | F | ≥ 50 (typical) | **N** |

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Board Tip
> Always quote the **wavelength of operation** and the **fiber type (SMF / MMF)** when specifying any connector or splice value. Examiners deduct marks if these are missing.

## 1.4 Classification Overview (for the KTU Mind Map)

```
Fiber Connections
├── Removable (Connectors)
│   ├── Ferrule-based (FC, SC, ST, LC, MU, E-2000)
│   ├── Lens-based (Expanded Beam)
│   └── Specialty (MTRJ, MT, MPO/MTP for multi-fiber)
└── Permanent (Splices)
    ├── Fusion Splice
    ├── Mechanical Splice
    └── Array Splices (Ribbon)
```
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fiber Optic Connectors — Detailed Architecture

A connector is essentially a precision mechanical assembly with the following functional sub-units:

1. **Ferrule** — A rigid cylindrical ceramic (zirconia, $\text{ZrO}_2$) or glass sleeve that holds the fiber in a precisely bored central hole. Standard outer diameter = **2.5 mm** (FC, SC, ST) or **1.25 mm** (LC, MU).
2. **Alignment Sleeve / Alignment Sleeve Collet** — Provides self-centering when two ferrules are mated.
3. **Spring-Loaded Coupling Nut / Latch** — Maintains a controlled end-face contact pressure.
4. **Housing & Boot** — Provides strain relief and protection.
5. **End-Face Geometry** — Polished surface (PC, UPC, or APC) that determines return loss.

### 2.1.1 Major Connector Types (Must-Know for KTU)

| Type | Full Form | Coupling Style | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **FC** | Ferrule Connector | Screw-threaded (knurled nut) | Test equipment, single-mode |
| **SC** | Subscriber Connector | Push-pull rectangular | Datacom, telecom, FTTH |
| **ST** | Straight Tip | Bayonet (twist-lock) | LANs, multimode legacy |
| **LC** | Lucent Connector | RJ-45 latch, 1.25 mm ferrule | High-density SFP, routers |
| **MU** | Miniature Unit | Push-pull, 1.25 mm | Japan NTT, dense panels |
| **E-2000** | Euro 2000 | Push-pull with shutter | High-power, POF |
| **APC** | Angled Physical Contact | 8° angle polish, → high RL | CATV, FTTH, RF-over-fiber |
| **UPC** | Ultra Physical Contact | Domed polish, → low RL | Standard digital telecom |
| **MT / MPO / MTP** | Multi-fiber | Array ferrule (12, 24, 48 fibers) | 40G/100G/400G datacom |

> [!NOTE] APC vs UPC
> The **8° angled end-face** in APC connectors causes back-reflected light to be diverted into the cladding rather than back to the source — dramatically improving return loss to **≥ 60 dB**.

### 2.1.2 End-Face Polishing Standards

| Polish Type | Return Loss | Use |
|---|---|---|
| Flat (PC) | ≥ 30 dB | Legacy |
| **UPC (Ultra PC)** | **≥ 50 dB** | Most digital systems |
| **APC (8° Angled)** | **≥ 60 dB** | Analog, high-power |

## 2.2 Fiber Optic Splicers (Machines)

A **fusion splicer** is a microprocessor-controlled precision machine that performs automatic alignment and arc-discharge fusion of two fiber end-faces. The major functional blocks are:

1. **Fiber Cleaver** — Produces a flat, mirror-finish end-face (cleave angle ≤ 0.5°).
2. **V-Groove Alignment Stage** — Piezo-actuated micro-positioning stage.
3. **Alignment System** — Core alignment (camera + image processing, PAS – Profile Alignment System) or cladding alignment (V-groove only).
4. **Fusion Electrodes** — Tungsten electrodes that generate a controlled electric arc (~ 10 kV, ~ 1 mA, ~ 1–2 s).
5. **Estimated Loss Calculation** — Core alignment splicers use image-based NA estimation to predict splice loss.
6. **Reinforcement Heater** — Heat-shrink protection sleeve (60 mm) applied over the splice.

> [!IMPORTANT] PAS vs IPA Alignment
> **PAS (Profile Alignment System)** is the *gold standard* for low-loss single-mode splicing (uses two cameras from orthogonal directions to image the core). **IPA (Local Injection & Detection)** injects light from one side and detects local power variations to align cores — used in field-portable splicers.

## 2.3 Splicing Techniques — Two Major Families

### 2.3.1 Fusion Splicing
Two cleaved fiber ends are aligned and fused by an electric arc. The fiber core melts, creating a continuous, monolithic glass joint.
- **Loss**: 0.02 – 0.1 dB (excellent)
- **Back-reflection**: ~ –50 dB (very low)
- **Durability**: > 25 years
- **Tools**: Fusion splicer + cleaver + protection sleeve
- **Process Steps**:
  1. Strip protective coating (~30 mm)
  2. Clean fiber with lint-free wipe + IPA
  3. Cleave to obtain flat end-face (≤ 0.5°)
  4. Insert both fibers into splicer
  5. Auto-alignment (PAS / IPA)
  6. Gap setting and pre-fusion arc
  7. Main arc fusion
  8. Tension test
  9. Shrink protection sleeve (heater)

### 2.3.2 Mechanical Splicing
Two cleaved fibers are aligned and held in place by a precision mechanical assembly using index-matching gel.
- **Loss**: 0.1 – 0.5 dB
- **Back-reflection**: ~ –35 dB
- **Durability**: ~ 5–10 years
- **Tools**: Mechanical splice kit (no expensive splicer)
- **Variants**: Elastomeric splice, 3-rod precision splice, rotary mechanical splice.

> [!NOTE] Index-Matching Gel
> A refractive-index-matching gel (n ≈ 1.46, same as silica) is applied at the joint to suppress Fresnel reflections from the air gap.

## 2.4 Splicing Issues (Critical for KTU Questions)

The following physical imperfections degrade splice / connector performance:

### 2.4.1 Loss Mechanisms

1. **Intrinsic (inherent to fiber geometry / optical properties)**
   - Core diameter mismatch
   - Numerical Aperture (NA) mismatch
   - Core ellipticity / non-circularity
   - Refractive Index Profile (RIP) mismatch
2. **Extrinsic (caused by joining technique)**
   - Lateral (transverse) offset
   - Angular (tilt) misalignment
   - End separation (longitudinal gap)
   - End-face quality (cleave angle, roughness)

### 2.4.2 The Three Cardinal Misalignments (Diagram with Mermaid later)

1. **Lateral Offset (d)** — Fibers displaced sideways; the dominant loss mechanism in single-mode fibers because the mode field is very small (~ 9 µm at 1550 nm).
2. **Angular Tilt (θ)** — Fiber axes not collinear.
3. **Longitudinal Separation (s)** — Gap between fiber end-faces; relevant for connectors with PC ferrules and for mechanical splices.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (Master Cheat Sheet)

> [!IMPORTANT] Always quote formulas in dB scale (using $10\log_{10}$), not linear ratio, as per telecom convention.

| # | Parameter | Formula (Linear) | Formula (dB) | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Insertion Loss (IL)** | $IL = P_{in}/P_{out}$ | $IL_{dB} = -10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{out}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | $P_{out} \le P_{in}$ → $IL \ge 0$ dB |
| 2 | **Return Loss (RL)** | $RL = P_{in}/P_{ref}$ | $RL_{dB} = -10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{ref}}{P_{in}}\right)$ | Higher RL = better |
| 3 | **Fresnel Reflection Coefficient** | $R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2$ | $R_{dB} = 10\log_{10}R$ | $n_1$ = fiber, $n_2$ = medium |
| 4 | **Fresnel Loss (air gap)** | $R \approx 0.0357$ | $R_{dB} \approx -14.5$ dB | For silica-to-air boundary (4%) |
| 5 | **Lateral Misalignment Loss** | $\eta_{lat} \approx \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{d^2}{w^2}\right)$ | $L_{lat} \approx -4.34\!\left(\dfrac{d^2}{w^2}\right)$ | $w$ = mode field radius |
| 6 | **Angular Misalignment Loss** | $\eta_{ang} \approx \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(\pi n w \theta)^2}{\lambda^2}\right)$ | $L_{ang} \approx -4.34\!\left(\dfrac{\pi n w \theta}{\lambda}\right)^2$ | $\theta$ in radians |
| 7 | **Gap Loss (Longitudinal)** | $\eta_{gap} \approx \dfrac{1}{1 + (s \lambda / 2\pi n w^2)^2}$ | $L_{gap}$ from curve | $s$ = separation |
| 8 | **NA Mismatch Loss** | $\eta_{NA} = \left(\dfrac{NA_2}{NA_1}\right)^2$ | $L_{NA} = -10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{NA_2}{NA_1}\right)^2$ | When $NA_2 < NA_1$ |
| 9 | **Core Diameter Mismatch Loss** | $\eta_{core} = \left(\dfrac{2a_2}{2a_1}\right)^2$ | $L_{core} = -10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{a_2}{a_1}\right)^2$ | When $a_2 < a_1$ |
| 10 | **Coupling Efficiency** | $\eta = P_{coupled} / P_{source}$ | $\eta_{dB} = 10\log_{10}\eta$ | Always $\le 1$ |
| 11 | **Total Splice Loss** | $L_{total} = \sum_i L_i$ | (dB sum) | Losses add in dB |
| 12 | **Wavelength Dependence** | $w \approx a \left(0.65 + \dfrac{1.619}{V^{3/2}} + \dfrac{2.879}{V^6}\right)$ | — | Marcuse formula for MFD |

> [!NOTE] Boundary Conditions
> - For $d \le 0.2 w$ → lateral loss $< 0.18$ dB
> - For $\theta \le 1°$ → angular loss $\approx 0.04$ dB
> - For $s \le 0.1 w^2/\lambda$ → gap loss $< 0.1$ dB
> - **Fresnel reflection is unavoidable in air-gap connections; use index-matching gel or physical contact (PC) ferrule polish to suppress it.**

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Long-Haul Telecom** — Fusion splices dominate (lowest loss, longest life).
- **FTTH (Fiber-to-the-Home)** — Mechanical splices (field-installable) and pre-terminated connectors (SC/APC) are common.
- **Datacom (Data Centers)** — LC / MTP/MPO connectors at 40G/100G/400G speeds.
- **Sensors and Instrumentation** — Specialty connectors for harsh environments (MIL-spec, hermetic, expanded-beam for dirty/vibrating conditions).
- **CATV / RF-over-Fiber** — APC connectors mandatory to avoid multi-path interference.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Lateral Misalignment Loss (Coupling Efficiency)

### Setup
Two single-mode fibers with equal mode-field radius $w$ are joined with a **lateral (transverse) offset** $d$. We assume Gaussian mode field profiles of the form:

$$
\psi(r) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{r^2}{w^2}\right)
$$

### Step 1 — Coupling Efficiency Definition

The coupling efficiency $\eta$ is the **overlap integral** between the launched Gaussian mode (centered at $x = d/2$) and the receiving Gaussian mode (centered at $x = -d/2$), expressed as a power ratio:

$$
\eta = \dfrac{\left\vert \iint E_1(x,y)\, E_2^*(x,y)\, dx\,dy \right\vert^2}{\iint \vert E_1\vert^2\,dx\,dy\;\iint \vert E_2\vert^2\,dx\,dy}
$$

### Step 2 — Substitute Gaussian Fields

Let the launched field be $E_1 = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-d/2)^2 + y^2}{w^2}\right)$ and the receiving field be $E_2 = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x+d/2)^2 + y^2}{w^2}\right)$.

The numerator becomes:

$$
I_{num} = \left\vert \iint \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x-d/2)^2 + y^2}{w^2}\right)\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(x+d/2)^2 + y^2}{w^2}\right) dx\,dy \right\vert^2
$$

### Step 3 — Combine Exponentials

Adding the exponents in the product:

$$
(x-d/2)^2 + (x+d/2)^2 = 2x^2 + 2(d/2)^2
$$

So the overlap integral reduces to:

$$
I = \iint \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2x^2 + d^2/2 + 2y^2}{w^2}\right) dx\,dy
$$

### Step 4 — Separate Integrals

$$
I = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{d^2}{2w^2}\right) \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2x^2}{w^2}\right) dx \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2y^2}{w^2}\right) dy
$$

### Step 5 — Evaluate Gaussian Integrals

Using $\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} e^{-\alpha x^2} dx = \sqrt{\pi/\alpha}$, with $\alpha = 2/w^2$:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2x^2}{w^2}\right) dx = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi w^2}{2}}
$$

Therefore:

$$
I = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{d^2}{2w^2}\right) \cdot \dfrac{\pi w^2}{2}
$$

### Step 6 — Compute the Denominator

$$
I_{denom} = \iint \vert E_1\vert^2 dx\,dy \;\iint \vert E_2\vert^2 dx\,dy
$$

Each $\iint \vert E_i\vert^2 dx\,dy$ also equals $\pi w^2/2$, so:

$$
I_{denom} = \left(\dfrac{\pi w^2}{2}\right)^2
$$

### Step 7 — Coupling Efficiency

$$
\eta = \dfrac{I^2}{I_{denom}} = \dfrac{\left[\exp(-d^2/2w^2)\cdot \pi w^2/2\right]^2}{(\pi w^2/2)^2} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{d^2}{w^2}\right)
$$

### Step 8 — Convert to dB Loss

$$
L_{lat}^{dB} = -10\log_{10}\eta = -10\log_{10}\left[\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{d^2}{w^2}\right)\right] = \dfrac{10\,d^2}{w^2 \ln 10}
$$

Using $\ln 10 \approx 2.3026$:

$$
\boxed{\,L_{lat}^{dB} \approx 4.343\,\dfrac{d^2}{w^2}\,}
$$

> [!NOTE] Interpretation
> This is the **most-quoted formula** for SMF splice loss. For $w = 5\ \mu m$ (typical at 1310 nm), a $1\ \mu m$ offset already gives $L \approx 0.17$ dB.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Angular Tilt Loss

### Setup
Two fibers of mode-field radius $w$ are joined with a small **angular tilt** $\theta$ between their axes.

### Step 1 — Field Expressions

$$
E_1(x,y) = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{x^2 + y^2}{w^2}\right) \cdot e^{j k \theta x}
$$

The receiving fiber is the same Gaussian, multiplied by the phase-shift factor $e^{-jk\theta x}$ to enforce phase matching at $x = 0$.

### Step 2 — Overlap Integral

$$
I = \iint \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2(x^2+y^2)}{w^2}\right) \cdot e^{j k\theta x} \;dx\,dy
$$

The $y$-integral gives $\sqrt{\pi w^2/2}$. The $x$-integral:

$$
\int_{-\infty}^{\infty} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{2x^2}{w^2}\right) e^{j k\theta x}\, dx = \sqrt{\dfrac{\pi w^2}{2}} \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(k\theta w)^2}{8}\right)
$$

### Step 3 — Combine

$$
I = \dfrac{\pi w^2}{2}\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(k\theta w)^2}{8}\right)
$$

### Step 4 — Coupling Efficiency

$$
\eta = \dfrac{I^2}{(\pi w^2/2)^2} = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(k\theta w)^2}{4}\right)
$$

Substituting $k = 2\pi n / \lambda$:

$$
\eta = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{(\pi n w \theta)^2}{\lambda^2}\right)
$$

### Step 5 — Loss in dB

$$
\boxed{\,L_{ang}^{dB} \approx 4.343\left(\dfrac{\pi n w \theta}{\lambda}\right)^2\,}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Fresnel Reflection Loss

### Setup
A light beam passes from a medium of refractive index $n_1$ (silica fiber, $n_1 = 1.468$ at 1550 nm) into $n_2$ (air, $n_2 = 1.0$) at normal incidence.

### Step 1 — Reflectance at a Single Interface

From Fresnel's equations at normal incidence:

$$
R = \left(\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1 + n_2}\right)^2
$$

### Step 2 — Insert Values

$$
R = \left(\dfrac{1.468 - 1.000}{1.468 + 1.000}\right)^2 = \left(\dfrac{0.468}{2.468}\right)^2 = (0.1896)^2 \approx 0.0359
$$

### Step 3 — Convert to dB

$$
R_{dB} = 10 \log_{10}(0.0359) \approx -14.45\ \text{dB}
$$

### Step 4 — Loss at the Joint (Two Air Gaps)

If a fiber-to-fiber joint has *two* air interfaces (fiber1–air and air–fiber2), the total transmission is:

$$
T = (1 - R)^2 = (0.964)^2 \approx 0.929
$$

$$
L_{Fresnel}^{dB} = -10\log_{10}(0.929) \approx 0.32\ \text{dB}
$$

> [!NOTE] Significance
> Even a perfect physical alignment can lose ~0.32 dB at an air gap. This is why **PC ferrules** (which force direct glass-to-glass contact) and **index-matching gel** are essential.

---

## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (Combined Splice Loss)

**Problem:** Two single-mode fibers are spliced. Fiber 1: $2a_1 = 9\ \mu m$ core, $NA_1 = 0.11$. Fiber 2: $2a_2 = 10\ \mu m$ core, $NA_2 = 0.10$. Mechanical splice has lateral offset $d = 1.2\ \mu m$, tilt $\theta = 0.5°$, and a small air gap. Wavelength $\lambda = 1310$ nm, $n_1 = 1.468$. Compute total loss.

### Step A — Mode Field Radii

Use $w \approx a(0.65 + 1.619/V^{3/2} + 2.879/V^6)$ (Marcuse). For $V \approx 2.4$:
$w_1 \approx 4.6\ \mu m$, $w_2 \approx 5.2\ \mu m$. Average $w \approx 4.9\ \mu m$.

### Step B — Lateral Loss

$$
L_{lat} = 4.343 \times \dfrac{(1.2)^2}{(4.9)^2} = 4.343 \times 0.0600 = 0.260\ \text{dB}
$$

### Step C — Angular Loss

Convert $0.5°$ to radians: $\theta = 0.5 \times \pi/180 = 8.727 \times 10^{-3}$ rad.

$$
L_{ang} = 4.343 \times \left(\dfrac{\pi \times 1.468 \times 4.9 \times 8.727 \times 10^{-3}}{1.31}\right)^2
$$

$$
= 4.343 \times (0.1505)^2 = 4.343 \times 0.02265 = 0.0984\ \text{dB}
$$

### Step D — NA Mismatch

$$
L_{NA} = -10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{0.10}{0.11}\right)^2 = -10\log_{10}(0.826) = 0.083\ \text{dB}
$$

### Step E — Core Mismatch

$$
L_{core} = -10\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{4.5}{4.0}\right)^2 = -10\log_{10}(1.266) = -0.102\ \text{dB}
$$
(Negative means no loss because receiving core is larger.)

### Step F — Fresnel Loss (air gap)

$$
L_{Fresnel} \approx 0.32\ \text{dB}
$$

### Step G — Total

$$
\boxed{\,L_{total} = 0.260 + 0.098 + 0.083 + 0 + 0.32 \approx 0.76\ \text{dB}\,}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Board Tip
> In dB, **losses always add** (regardless of whether they are positive or negative). Always verify your sign convention before summing.

---

## 3.5 Analytical Matrix: Connector vs Splice

| Feature | Connector | Fusion Splice | Mechanical Splice |
|---|---|---|---|
| Removability | Yes | No | No (semi) |
| Insertion Loss (typ.) | 0.2–0.5 dB | 0.02–0.1 dB | 0.1–0.5 dB |
| Return Loss (typ.) | 40–60 dB | > 50 dB | 30–40 dB |
| Equipment Cost | Low (per mating) | High (splicer ₹2–5 lakh) | Very low |
| Skill Required | Moderate | High | Low |
| Field Suitability | Excellent | Good (with portable splicer) | Excellent |
| Reliability | Good | Excellent | Moderate |
| Use Case | Patch panels, equipment | Trunk lines, long-haul | Emergency repair, FTTH |
| Time per joint | < 1 min | 1–2 min | < 1 min |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Diagram of a Fusion Splicer

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Cleaved Fiber 1] --> B[Fiber V-Groove Stage 1]
    C[Cleaved Fiber 2] --> D[Fiber V-Groove Stage 2]
    B --> E{PAS / IPA Alignment Unit}
    D --> E
    E -->|X-Y-Z Micro-Adjustment| F[Gap Setting Actuator]
    F --> G[Pre-Fusion Arc Discharge]
    G --> H[Main Fusion Arc]
    H --> I[Tension Test 200 g]
    I -->|Pass| J[Heater Protection Sleeve 60 mm]
    I -->|Fail| K[Realign and Refuse]
    J --> L[Completed Splice]
    K --> F
    E --> M[LCD Display Estimated Loss dB]
    H --> M
```

## 4.2 Architecture of a Fiber Optic Connector

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Fiber Cable] --> B[Strain Relief Boot]
    B --> C[Aramid Yarn Kevlar Retention]
    C --> D[Connector Housing]
    D --> E[Ferrule 2.5 mm or 1.25 mm]
    E --> F[Precision Bore Hole 125 micron]
    F --> G[Polished End-Face PC or UPC or APC]
    D --> H[Spring]
    H --> I[Coupling Nut or Latch]
    I --> J[Alignment Sleeve]
    J --> K[Mated Connector Pair]
    G --> K
```

## 4.3 Three Cardinal Misalignments in a Fiber Joint

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Ideal
        A1[Fiber 1 Core] --- B1[Fiber 2 Core Aligned]
    end
    subgraph Lateral
        A2[Fiber 1 Core] -. offset d .-> B2[Fiber 2 Core]
    end
    subgraph Angular
        A3[Fiber 1 Core] == tilt theta ==> B3[Fiber 2 Core]
    end
    subgraph Longitudinal
        A4[Fiber 1 Core] -->|gap s| B4[Fiber 2 Core]
    end
    Ideal --- Lateral --- Angular --- Longitudinal
```

## 4.4 Fiber Connection Selection Decision Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([New Fiber Joint Required]) --> Q1{Joint Permanently Needed}
    Q1 -->|Yes| Q2{Field Equipment Available}
    Q1 -->|No| ConnPath[Use Connector]

    Q2 -->|Only Hand Tools| Mech[Mechanical Splice]
    Fusion --> Q3{Performance Critical}
    Q3 -->|Yes| PAS[PAS Core Alignment]
    Q3 -->|No| IPA[IPA Cladding Alignment]
    Mech --> Gel[Index Matching Gel Applied]
    ConnPath --> Q4{Number of Matings}
    Q4 -->|Few| ST[ST or FC]
    Q4 -->|Many| LC[LC or SC]
    Q4 -->|Very Many| MTP[MTP or MPO Array]
```

## 4.5 Loss Mechanism Causality Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    Root[Total Splice / Connector Loss]
    Root --> Int[Intrinsic Losses]
    Root --> Ext[Extrinsic Losses]

    Int --> A3[Core Ellipticity]
    Int --> A4[RIP Mismatch]
    Ext --> B1[Lateral Offset d]
    Ext --> B2[Angular Tilt theta]
    Ext --> B3[Longitudinal Gap s]
    Ext --> B4[End-Face Roughness]
    Ext --> B5[Contamination Dust or Oil]
    A1 --> Sum[Algebraic Sum in dB]

    A3 --> Sum
