# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE] Mark Distribution Reference
> KTU ESE Module-Level (out of 100): Module 2 carries ~ **20 marks** in the End-Semester. Typical mix: 1 Part-A (3 marks) + 1 Part-B (14 marks) per module, *internal choice allowed in Part-B*.

---

## Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Define insertion loss and return loss of a fiber optic connector. Why are these parameters important?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Insertion Loss (IL)** is the ratio of optical power launched into the input port to the power exiting the output port, expressed in dB: $IL_{dB} = -10\log_{10}(P_{out}/P_{in})$. It quantifies the signal attenuation introduced by the connector. (1.5 marks)
- **Return Loss (RL)** is the ratio of input power to the power reflected back to the source: $RL_{dB} = -10\log_{10}(P_{ref}/P_{in})$. It quantifies back-reflection at the joint. (1 mark)
- These parameters are important because they directly affect the **link power budget, signal-to-noise ratio, and bit error rate (BER)** in high-speed digital and analog optical systems. (0.5 marks)

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]
**Differentiate between fusion splicing and mechanical splicing.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Fusion Splice | Mechanical Splice |
|---|---|---|
| **Principle** | Arc-fused molten joint | Mechanical alignment + index gel |
| **Loss** | 0.02–0.1 dB (very low) | 0.1–0.5 dB |
| **Return Loss** | > 50 dB | 30–40 dB |
| **Equipment** | Expensive fusion splicer | Low-cost hand tool |
| **Durability** | 25+ years | 5–10 years |
| **Use** | Trunk / long-haul | FTTH, emergency repair |

(Each correct contrast: 0.5 mark × 6 = 3 marks)

---

## Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> **Internal Choice Instruction** (KTU 2024): *Answer any ONE of the following:*

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 marks]
**Explain the various types of fiber optic connectors in detail. Compare the FC, SC, ST, and LC connectors with neat diagrams.**

**Model Solution:**

**Definition [1 mark]**
A fiber optic connector is a demountable mechanical device that provides a precisely aligned, low-loss optical coupling between two fibers or a fiber and an active device.

**Common Connector Types [4 marks — 1 mark each]**

1. **FC (Ferrule Connector):** Uses a 2.5 mm ceramic ferrule and a screw-threaded coupling nut. Highly vibration-resistant. Common in single-mode test equipment and long-haul telecom.

2. **SC (Subscriber Connector):** Uses a 2.5 mm ferrule with a push-pull rectangular housing. Excellent for high-density panels. Snap-action provides audible click on engagement. Used in FTTH, LAN, datacom.

3. **ST (Straight Tip):** Uses a 2.5 mm ferrule with a bayonet (twist-and-lock) coupling. Common in legacy multimode LANs (Ethernet, token ring).

4. **LC (Lucent Connector):** Uses a 1.25 mm ferrule with an RJ-45-style latching mechanism. Half the size of SC — enables very high panel density. Standard for SFP transceivers in modern datacom.

5. *(Optional brief mention)* **MU** – 1.25 mm push-pull, Japan-domestic. **E-2000** – shuttered, high-power. **APC** – 8° angle polish for high RL.

**Comparative Table [1.5 marks]**

| Parameter | FC | SC | ST | LC |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Ferrule Ø | 2.5 mm | 2.5 mm | 2.5 mm | **1.25 mm** |
| Coupling | Screw | Push-pull | Bayonet | RJ-latch |
| Density | Low | Medium | Low | **Very High** |
| Typical Use | Test, SMF | FTTH, SMF/MMF | Legacy MMF LAN | SFP, datacom |

**Why Size Matters [0.5 mark]**
Smaller ferrules (1.25 mm) reduce panel real estate — critical in 40G/100G/400G datacom where thousands of fibers terminate in a single rack.

#### (b) [7 marks]
**Discuss in detail the different splicing techniques used in optical fiber communication. Compare fusion and mechanical splicing.**

**Model Solution:**

**Splicing — Definition [1 mark]**
A permanent (or semi-permanent) joint formed between two fiber end-faces to provide a low-loss, low-reflection optical path. Two main techniques: **fusion** and **mechanical**.

**Fusion Splicing [2.5 marks]**
- *Process:* (1) Strip coating (2) Clean with IPA (3) Cleave to ≤ 0.5° (4) Insert in splicer V-grooves (5) PAS/IPA auto-alignment (6) Pre-fusion arc (7) Main arc fusion (8) Tension test (9) Heat-shrink protection sleeve.
- *Equipment:* Fusion splicer (₹2–5 lakh), cleaver, protection sleeve.
- *Alignment methods:* PAS (core alignment, lowest loss) and IPA (cladding alignment, fast).
- *Loss:* 0.02–0.1 dB. *RL:* > 50 dB. *Durability:* 25+ years.

**Mechanical Splicing [2.5 marks]**
- *Principle:* Precision-machined alignment sleeve (glass or ceramic capillary) holds the two fibers; index-matching gel (n ≈ 1.46) suppresses Fresnel reflection.
- *Types:* (i) Elastomeric splice (self-centering rubber insert) (ii) 3-rod precision splice (3 glass rods clamp fiber) (iii) Rotary splice.
- *Loss:* 0.1–0.5 dB. *RL:* 30–40 dB. *Durability:* 5–10 years.
- *Advantages:* No expensive equipment; fast (~1 min); ideal for emergency repair and FTTH drop cables.

**Comparison [1 mark]**
(See table in Q2 above — already covered.)

> [!WARNING] Examiner's Pitfall
> Students often write *"mechanical splice uses glue"* — this is **wrong**. Modern mechanical splices do NOT use adhesive; they use a **precision alignment sleeve and index-matching gel** that can be re-entered. Glue-based terminations (epoxy + polish) are a *separate* category called "epoxy-and-polish pigtails" — not "mechanical splices". -0.5 mark.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice

#### (a) [7 marks]
**Explain the various issues that arise during fiber splicing. Derive the expression for splice loss due to lateral misalignment in single-mode fiber.**

**Model Solution:**

**Splicing Issues [3.5 marks]**
The two categories of loss mechanisms are:

1. **Intrinsic (inherent to fiber):**
   - **Core diameter mismatch** — different core sizes between the two fibers.
   - **NA mismatch** — different numerical apertures.
   - **Core ellipticity / non-circularity** — core not perfectly circular.
   - **Refractive-index profile (RIP) mismatch** — graded vs step-index mixing.

2. **Extrinsic (caused by jointing):**
   - **Lateral (transverse) offset** $d$ — fibers displaced sideways.
   - **Angular tilt** $\theta$ — fiber axes not collinear.
   - **Longitudinal gap** $s$ — separation between end-faces.
   - **End-face quality** — cleave angle > 0.5°, roughness, contamination.

**Lateral Misalignment Loss Derivation [3.5 marks]**
*(Same derivation as in Section 3.1 of these notes; summarized here.)*

Assume Gaussian mode field $\psi(r) = \exp(-r^2/w^2)$. Two fibers of equal MFD $w$ with lateral offset $d$:

1. Coupling efficiency = power overlap of the two Gaussians:
   $$\eta = \dfrac{\left\vert \iint E_1 E_2^*\, dA \right\vert^2}{\iint \vert E_1\vert^2 dA \cdot \iint \vert E_2\vert^2 dA}$$

2. Substitute Gaussians and evaluate the integrals:
   $$\eta = \exp\!\left(-\dfrac{d^2}{w^2}\right)$$

3. Convert to dB loss using $-10\log_{10}\eta$:
   $$L_{dB} = -10\log_{10}\!\left[\exp\!\left(-\dfrac{d^2}{w^2}\right)\right] = \dfrac{10 d^2}{w^2 \ln 10}$$

4. Since $\ln 10 \approx 2.3026$:
   $$\boxed{\,L_{dB} \approx 4.343\,\dfrac{d^2}{w^2}\,}$$

5. **Discussion:** For SMF-28 at 1310 nm, $w \approx 4.6\ \mu m$. A 1 µm offset gives $L \approx 0.21$ dB. A 2 µm offset gives $L \approx 0.82$ dB — very sensitive!

#### (b) [7 marks]
**A single-mode fiber operating at 1310 nm has a mode field diameter of 9.2 µm. It is to be spliced to another SMF with a 1.5 µm lateral offset, 0.7° angular tilt, and a small air gap. The fiber has n = 1.468. Estimate the total splice loss.**

**Model Solution:**

**Given:**
- Wavelength $\lambda = 1310$ nm = $1.31\ \mu m$
- Mode field diameter $2w = 9.2\ \mu m$ → $w = 4.6\ \mu m$
- Lateral offset $d = 1.5\ \mu m$
- Angular tilt $\theta = 0.7° = 0.7 \times \pi/180 = 1.222 \times 10^{-2}$ rad
- Refractive index $n = 1.468$
- Air gap (assume Fresnel loss present)

**Step 1 — Lateral Loss [2 marks]**
$$L_{lat} = 4.343 \times \dfrac{(1.5)^2}{(4.6)^2} = 4.343 \times \dfrac{2.25}{21.16} = 4.343 \times 0.1063 = 0.462\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 2 — Angular Loss [2 marks]**
$$L_{ang} = 4.343 \times \left(\dfrac{\pi \times 1.468 \times 4.6 \times 1.222 \times 10^{-2}}{1.31}\right)^2$$
$$= 4.343 \times \left(\dfrac{0.2594}{1.31}\right)^2 = 4.343 \times (0.1980)^2 = 4.343 \times 0.0392 = 0.170\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 3 — Fresnel Loss (air gap) [1.5 marks]**
For two silica–air interfaces (silica n = 1.468):
$$R = \left(\dfrac{0.468}{2.468}\right)^2 = 0.0359$$
$$T = (1-R)^2 = 0.929 \rightarrow L_{Fresnel} = -10\log_{10}(0.929) = 0.32\ \text{dB}$$

**Step 4 — Total Splice Loss [1.5 marks]**
$$L_{total} = 0.462 + 0.170 + 0.32 = \boxed{0.952 \approx 0.95\ \text{dB}}$$

> [!IMPORTANT] KTU Board Tip
> In ESE problems, **always show every intermediate numerical step**. Mark deductions occur primarily for: (i) missing unit conversion (°→rad), (ii) using the wrong formula for the angular loss, (iii) adding losses in linear scale instead of dB, (iv) forgetting to square the offset before dividing by $w^2$.

> [!WARNING] Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losses
> 1. **Forgetting to convert degrees to radians** in angular loss (lose 0.5 mark per part).
> 2. **Using core radius $a$ instead of mode-field radius $w$** in the lateral formula for SMF. SMF has $w \neq a$ — use the MFD/2 value.
> 3. **Adding losses in linear units** — this is wrong; losses must be summed in dB.
> 4. **Confusing "insertion loss" with "return loss"** — these are two distinct parameters.
> 5. **Stating wrong polish-type RL values** — UPC = ≥ 50 dB, **APC = ≥ 60 dB**, NOT the reverse.
> 6. **Calling index-matching gel "glue"** — gel is a *liquid/paste*, not an adhesive. -0.5 mark per occurrence.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### ⚡ Ultra-Fast Revision Checklist

- **Connector** = demountable; **Splice** = permanent. Remember this distinction first.
- **Standard ferrule sizes:** 2.5 mm (FC/SC/ST) vs 1.25 mm (LC/MU).
- **APC = 8° angle polish → RL ≥ 60 dB; UPC = domed polish → RL ≥ 50 dB.**
- **MPO/MTP** = multi-fiber array connectors for 40G/100G/400G datacom.
- **Fusion splice** = electric arc + molten joint; loss 0.02–0.1 dB; PAS alignment is the gold standard.
- **Mechanical splice** = alignment sleeve + index-matching gel; no expensive equipment needed; loss 0.1–0.5 dB.
- **Three cardinal misalignments:** lateral $d$, angular $\theta$, longitudinal $s$.
- **Lateral loss (SMF, Gaussian):** $L_{lat}^{dB} = 4.343 \cdot (d/w)^2$.
- **Angular loss (SMF):** $L_{ang}^{dB} = 4.343 \cdot (\pi n w \theta / \lambda)^2$.
- **Fresnel reflection (silica–air):** $R \approx 3.6\% \approx -14.5$ dB per interface; full joint loss ≈ 0.32 dB.
- **NA mismatch loss:** $L_{NA} = -10\log_{10}(NA_2/NA_1)^2$ (only when $NA_2 < NA_1$).
- **Core mismatch loss:** $L_{core} = -10\log_{10}(a_2/a_1)^2$ (only when $a_2 < a_1$).
- **Marcuse formula** for MFD: $w = a \left(0.65 + 1.619/V^{3/2} + 2.879/V^6\right)$.
- **Always use $w$, not $a$, for SMF mode-field calculations.**
- **Index-matching gel** (n ≈ 1.46) is used in mechanical splices to suppress Fresnel loss.
- **PC ferrule end-face polish** forces direct glass-to-glass contact — eliminates air gap.
- **FTTH installations** typically use SC/APC connectors; long-haul uses FC/UPC + fusion splices.
- **Losses add in dB**, not in linear ratio. Always sum in dB.
- **Typical per-joint budget:** Connector ≤ 0.5 dB; Fusion splice ≤ 0.1 dB; Mechanical splice ≤ 0.5 dB.
- **Contamination (dust, oil) is the #1 field cause of high connector loss** — always clean with IPA + lint-free wipe.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Optical Couplers, Isolators and Circulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET413 - OPTICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optical Couplers, Isolators and Circulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Optical Couplers

**Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
An **optical coupler** is a passive photonic device that splits an incoming optical signal into two or more outputs, or conversely, combines two or more input signals into a single output fiber, with a predefined power-splitting ratio. Couplers are foundational building blocks in fiber-optic networks for power distribution, signal routing, wavelength division multiplexing, and system monitoring.

> [!NOTE]
> Optical couplers are **reciprocal** devices — light traveling in the reverse direction experiences the same coupling behavior as in the forward direction. This distinguishes them fundamentally from isolators and circulators, which are **non-reciprocal**.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Garden-Hose Splitter" Picture:**
Imagine a pressurized water pipe (the input fiber) reaching a T-junction. Depending on the valve setting, the water can be split 50/50 between two outlet hoses, or 70/30, or any other ratio. An optical coupler is essentially the light-equivalent of this plumbing junction — except that instead of mechanical valves, the splitting is controlled by:
- The **physical proximity** of two fiber cores (fused-fiber couplers), or
- **Refractive index waveguides** integrated on a planar substrate (planar waveguide couplers), or
- **Micro-optic lenses and beam splitters** (bulk-optic couplers).

The input light "leaks" sideways into the adjacent fiber through the **evanescent field** that extends just outside the fiber core. The closer the fibers and the longer the interaction region, the more power transfers.

---

### 1.2 Optical Isolators

**Formal Definition:**
An **optical isolator** is a **non-reciprocal** passive device that allows light to pass in one direction (the **forward** direction) with minimal loss, while strongly attenuating light traveling in the opposite (reverse) direction. It protects sensitive optical sources (such as laser diodes) from harmful back-reflections.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The non-reciprocal behavior of the isolator is rooted in the **Faraday Effect** (magneto-optic rotation), discovered by Michael Faraday in 1845. This effect is fundamentally different from natural optical rotation, as it is independent of the direction of light propagation — only the magnetic field direction determines the sense of polarization rotation.

**Intuitive Analogy — The "One-Way Check Valve":**
Think of a check valve in a water pump. Water can flow forward freely, but if it tries to flow backward, a flap slams shut. An optical isolator is the photonic equivalent:
- Forward light → **passes through unimpeded** (low insertion loss, typically **0.5 dB to 1 dB**).
- Backward light → **blocked** (high isolation, typically **30 dB to 50 dB**).

The "flap" in this case is the combined action of a **polarizer**, a **Faraday rotator** (a magneto-optic crystal in a strong magnetic field), and an **analyzer** (a second polarizer oriented at 45°).

---

### 1.3 Optical Circulators

**Formal Definition:**
An **optical circulator** is a non-reciprocal multi-port device that routes light from one port to the next in a fixed cyclic order (e.g., Port 1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 1) while blocking light attempting to travel in the reverse direction between non-adjacent ports. Standard configurations are **3-port** and **4-port** circulators.

> [!NOTE]
> An optical circulator can be conceptually understood as the **integration of two optical isolators** sharing a common Faraday rotator. The 3-port circulator is the most widely used configuration in bidirectional DWDM systems and erbium-doped fiber amplifiers (EDFAs).

**Intuitive Analogy — The "Traffic Roundabout":**
Picture a roundabout where vehicles enter from one road and exit from the next road in clockwise order. No vehicle can "U-turn" or skip to a non-adjacent exit. The optical circulator enforces exactly this rule for photons:
- **Port 1 → Port 2** : light flows forward
- **Port 2 → Port 3** : light flows forward
- **Port 3 → Port 1** : light flows forward (cyclic)
- **All other paths** : blocked (high isolation)

---

### 1.4 Key Performance Metrics (Standard Across All Three Devices)

| Metric | Definition | Typical Magnitude |
|---|---|---|
| **Insertion Loss (IL)** | Power lost when signal passes through the device in the desired direction | **0.1 dB to 1 dB** |
| **Return Loss (RL)** | Ratio of incident power to reflected power | **≥ 50 dB** |
| **Crosstalk / Isolation** | Unwanted power coupled into a non-target port | **≥ 30 dB to 50 dB** (isolators) |
| **Directivity** | Ability to reject backward-propagating light in a coupler | **≥ 50 dB** |
| **Polarization Dependent Loss (PDL)** | Variation in insertion loss with input polarization | **≤ 0.1 dB** |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The decibel (dB) scale is used throughout fiber optics: $\text{Loss (dB)} = -10 \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$. A loss of **3 dB** corresponds to exactly half the optical power.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Optical Couplers — Classification and Working Principle

#### 2.1.1 Classification of Optical Couplers

**(a) Based on the coupling mechanism:**

1. **Fused-Fiber Couplers** (also called *fused biconical tapered couplers*, FBT):
   - Two single-mode fibers are twisted together and heated until they fuse.
   - The fused region is then stretched (tapered), causing the core modes to expand and overlap.
   - Power couples from one fiber to the other through the **evanescent field** in the tapered waist.
   - The coupling ratio is controlled by the **fusion length** and the **taper ratio**.

2. **Planar Waveguide Couplers** (PLC — Planar Lightwave Circuit):
   - Waveguides are lithographically defined on a silicon or silica substrate.
   - Coupling occurs in a **directional coupler region** where two waveguides run parallel at sub-micron separation.
   - Used in mass-produced telecom components with very low PDL and wavelength dependence.

3. **Bulk-Optic Couplers**:
   - Use lenses, prisms, and partially reflecting mirrors.
   - Heavier, larger, but offer broad wavelength operation and are useful in laboratory setups.

**(b) Based on the port configuration:**

| Type | Configuration | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **T-Coupler (2×2)** | 2 inputs, 2 outputs | Power tapping, bidirectional links |
| **Star Coupler (N×N)** | N inputs, N outputs | LAN distribution networks |
| **Tree Coupler (1×N)** | 1 input, N outputs | Power splitting to subscribers (PON) |
| **Wavelength Selective Coupler (WSC)** | Splits by wavelength | WDM demultiplexing |

#### 2.1.2 Why Evanescent Coupling Works — The Physics

In a single-mode fiber, the guided mode's field does not abruptly end at the core-cladding boundary. It **decays exponentially** into the cladding over a distance comparable to the wavelength. This tail is called the **evanescent field**.

When two fiber cores are brought within micrometers of each other, the evanescent field of the first fiber overlaps with the core of the second fiber. This overlap enables **periodic power transfer** between the two waveguides, governed by the **coupled-mode theory**.

The power in the coupled (cross) port as a function of interaction length $L$ is:

$$P_{\text{cross}}(L) = P_{0} \, \sin^{2}(\kappa L)$$

where $\kappa$ is the **coupling coefficient** (units: rad/m), which depends on core separation, refractive index difference, and wavelength.

#### 2.1.3 Critical Performance Parameters of Couplers

- **Coupling Ratio (CR)**: 
$$CR = \frac{P_{\text{cross}}}{P_{\text{cross}} + P_{\text{bar}}} = \sin^{2}(\kappa L)$$

- **Excess Loss (EL)**:
$$EL_{\text{(dB)}} = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{cross}} + P_{\text{bar}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$$

- **Insertion Loss (IL)** for the through port:
$$IL_{\text{bar (dB)}} = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{bar}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right) = 10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{1}{1 - CR}\right) + EL$$

- **Directivity (D)**: 
$$D_{\text{(dB)}} = -10 \log_{10}\left(\frac{P_{\text{reflected back}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right) \geq 50 \text{ dB}$$

- **Splitting Ratio for 1×N star coupler** (uniform distribution): each output receives $1/N$ of the input power, corresponding to an ideal loss of $10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **beat length** $L_{\pi} = \dfrac{\pi}{2\kappa}$ is the interaction length required for **complete** power transfer ($\sin^{2}(\pi/2) = 1$). A 50/50 coupler corresponds to an interaction length of $L_{\pi}/2$.

---

### 2.2 Optical Isolators — The Faraday Effect

#### 2.2.1 The Faraday Effect (Foundation of the Isolator)

When linearly polarized light passes through a transparent material placed in a magnetic field, the plane of polarization rotates by an angle $\theta$ given by:

$$\theta = V \cdot B \cdot L$$

where:
- $\theta$ = rotation angle (radians, but conventionally expressed in degrees)
- $V$ = **Verdet constant** of the material (rad·T⁻¹·m⁻¹)
- $B$ = magnetic flux density (Tesla)
- $L$ = path length through the material (m)

For common magneto-optic materials used in isolators:

| Material | Verdet Constant @ 1550 nm | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Yttrium Iron Garnet (YIG)** | $\approx 20$ rad·T⁻¹·m⁻¹ | High Verdet, used in bulk isolators |
| **Terbium Gallium Garnet (TGG)** | $\approx 40$ rad·T⁻¹·m⁻¹ | Most widely used today |
| **Paramagnetic Glass (FR-5)** | $\approx 32$ rad·T⁻¹·m⁻¹ | Used in compact fiber-pigtailed isolators |

> [!NOTE]
> Faraday rotation is a **non-reciprocal** phenomenon. If a beam is rotated by $+45°$ in the forward direction, a backward beam is rotated by **another** $+45°$ in the same absolute direction (i.e., it becomes $-45°$ relative to the backward propagation). This is the key to isolator operation.

#### 2.2.2 Isolator Construction — Block-by-Block

A standard polarization-dependent isolator consists of three elements:

1. **Input Polarizer (P1)**: Accepts arbitrary input polarization, transmits only the horizontal component.
2. **Faraday Rotator**: A YIG or TGG crystal in a strong permanent magnet (SmCo or NdFeB) producing $\approx 45°$ rotation.
3. **Output Polarizer / Analyzer (P2)**: Oriented at exactly **+45°** relative to P1.

**Forward Path Analysis:**
- Light exits P1 with horizontal polarization.
- Faraday rotator rotates it by **+45°** → it now matches the orientation of P2.
- P2 transmits it with negligible loss.

**Reverse Path Analysis:**
- Any back-reflected light enters P2 and is linearly polarized at +45°.
- The Faraday rotator rotates it by **another +45°** → it becomes **+90°** (vertical) relative to P1.
- P1 is oriented horizontally and **blocks** the vertical component.
- Net result: **high isolation** of the backward signal.

#### 2.2.3 Isolation and Insertion Loss in an Isolator

- **Forward Insertion Loss**: typically **0.5 dB to 1 dB**
- **Reverse Isolation**: typically **30 dB to 50 dB**
- **Return Loss**: typically **≥ 50 dB**
- **Polarization Dependent Loss (PDL)**: typically **≤ 0.15 dB**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Polarization-**independent** isolators are constructed using **birefringent crystals** (e.g., rutile, calcite, YVO₄) that physically separate the two orthogonal polarization states, rotate each one independently by 45°, and recombine them. These are essential in modern telecom systems where input polarization is uncontrolled.

#### 2.2.4 Engineering Utility of Isolators

- **Laser diode protection**: Back-reflections from connectors and fiber ends can shift the laser wavelength, increase relative intensity noise (RIN), or even cause permanent damage. Isolators prevent this.
- **EDFA gain stabilization**: Reflections into the amplifier create unwanted feedback, leading to gain ripple and lasing action.
- **DWDM system integrity**: Prevents cross-channel interference in wavelength-routed networks.

---

### 2.3 Optical Circulators — Multi-Port Non-Reciprocal Routing

#### 2.3.1 Operating Principle

A **3-port optical circulator** is functionally a pair of isolators sharing a common central Faraday rotator. The key internal elements are:
- A **Faraday rotator** producing 45° rotation
- A pair of **birefringent crystals** (walk-off polarizers) at input and output
- A set of **reciprocal waveplates** (e.g., half-wave plates) that compensate for polarization effects
- Permanent magnets providing the field for the rotator

**Path Rules (3-Port Circulator):**
- Port 1 → Port 2 (forward pass)
- Port 2 → Port 3 (forward pass)
- Port 3 → Port 1 (forward pass)
- All other paths (e.g., Port 1 → Port 3, Port 3 → Port 2) are blocked with high isolation.

#### 2.3.2 Key Specifications

| Parameter | Typical Value |
|---|---|
| **Insertion Loss (Port-to-Port)** | 0.6 dB to 1.2 dB |
| **Isolation (Non-Adjacent Port)** | ≥ 40 dB to 50 dB |
| **Crosstalk** | ≤ -50 dB |
| **Return Loss** | ≥ 50 dB |
| **PDL** | ≤ 0.15 dB |
| **Operating Wavelength** | 1310 nm, 1550 nm, C-band (1530–1565 nm), L-band |

#### 2.3.3 4-Port Circulators

A 4-port circulator adds a fourth port in the cyclic sequence: **1 → 2 → 3 → 4 → 1**. These are used in **bidirectional DWDM add-drop multiplexers** where one port is dedicated to add channels and another to drop channels, sharing a single fiber pair.

#### 2.3.4 Engineering Applications

- **Bidirectional transmission over a single fiber** (wavelength-multiplexed upstream and downstream on the same fiber).
- **Optical add-drop multiplexers (OADMs)** in DWDM networks.
- **Fiber-optic gyroscopes** (where 4-port circulators route clockwise and counterclockwise beams through the sensing coil).
- **EDFA pump combiners** and **dispersion compensators** as in-line routing elements.
- **Optical time-domain reflectometry (OTDR)** signal separation.

---

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $CR = \dfrac{P_{\text{cross}}}{P_{\text{cross}} + P_{\text{bar}}} = \sin^{2}(\kappa L)$ | Coupling Ratio of a fused-fiber coupler | Dimensionless, 0 to 1 |
| 2 | $P_{\text{bar}}(L) = P_{0} \cos^{2}(\kappa L)$ | Power remaining in the through (bar) port | Watts |
| 3 | $P_{\text{cross}}(L) = P_{0} \sin^{2}(\kappa L)$ | Power transferred to the cross port | Watts |
| 4 | $L_{\pi} = \dfrac{\pi}{2\kappa}$ | Beat length (full power transfer distance) | meters |
| 5 | $EL_{\text{(dB)}} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{out,total}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | Excess loss of a coupler | dB |
| 6 | $\theta = V B L$ | Faraday rotation angle | radians or degrees |
| 7 | $IL_{\text{(dB)}} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | Insertion loss | dB (positive value = loss) |
| 8 | $\text{Isolation}_{\text{(dB)}} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\dfrac{P_{\text{leak}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right)$ | Reverse isolation of an isolator | dB |
| 9 | $\dfrac{P_{\text{out}}}{P_{\text{in}}} = 10^{-IL_{\text{(dB)}}/10}$ | Power ratio from insertion loss | Dimensionless |
| 10 | $\text{Loss}_{\text{1×N}} = 10 \log_{10}(N)$ | Ideal splitting loss for uniform 1×N coupler | dB |
| 11 | $\Delta\beta = \dfrac{2\pi \Delta n}{\lambda}$ | Difference in propagation constants (modes) | rad/m |
| 12 | $V_{\text{Verdet}} = \dfrac{\alpha}{k T} + \beta \omega^{2}$ | Approximate dispersion of Verdet constant (qualitative) | rad·T⁻¹·m⁻¹ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical KTU Exam Pitfall:** Students frequently confuse **insertion loss** (which is loss in the desired direction) with **isolation** (which is loss in the undesired direction). They are completely different metrics applied to different directions of propagation.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Coupled-Mode Equations for an Optical Coupler

**Starting Point:**
Two single-mode waveguides are placed in close proximity so that the evanescent field of one overlaps the core of the other. Let the complex amplitudes of the modes in waveguide 1 and waveguide 2 be $A_{1}(z)$ and $A_{2}(z)$, respectively, where $z$ is the propagation distance.

**Coupled-Mode Equations (Coupled Mode Theory, Yariv & Yeh formulation):**

$$\frac{dA_{1}}{dz} = i \kappa_{12} A_{2} e^{i \Delta \beta z}$$

$$\frac{dA_{2}}{dz} = i \kappa_{21} A_{1} e^{-i \Delta \beta z}$$

**Step 1 — Simplification for phase-matched (synchronous) waveguides.**

When the two waveguides are **identical** (same effective index), the propagation constants are equal, so $\Delta\beta = 0$. The exponential terms vanish:

$$\frac{dA_{1}}{dz} = i \kappa_{12} A_{2}$$

$$\frac{dA_{2}}{dz} = i \kappa_{21} A_{1}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the principle of power conservation (lossless coupling).**

Coupling is reciprocal, so $\kappa_{12} = \kappa_{21} \equiv \kappa$ (a real number for lossless coupling).

**Step 3 — Solve the system of first-order ODEs.**

Differentiate the first equation with respect to $z$:

$$\frac{d^{2}A_{1}}{dz^{2}} = i \kappa \frac{dA_{2}}{dz}$$

Substitute the second equation:

$$\frac{d^{2}A_{1}}{dz^{2}} = i \kappa \left(i \kappa A_{1}\right) = -\kappa^{2} A_{1}$$

This is a simple harmonic oscillator equation with general solution:

$$A_{1}(z) = C_{1} \cos(\kappa z) + C_{2} \sin(\kappa z)$$

**Step 4 — Apply boundary conditions.**

Assume input power enters only waveguide 1: $A_{1}(0) = A_{0}$ and $A_{2}(0) = 0$.

From the first coupled equation evaluated at $z = 0$:

$$\left.\frac{dA_{1}}{dz}\right|_{z=0} = i \kappa A_{2}(0) = 0$$

This means $C_{2} = 0$. Therefore:

$$A_{1}(z) = A_{0} \cos(\kappa z)$$

From the second coupled equation:

$$A_{2}(z) = \frac{1}{i \kappa}\frac{dA_{1}}{dz} = \frac{1}{i \kappa}\left[-\kappa A_{0} \sin(\kappa z)\right] = i A_{0} \sin(\kappa z)$$

**Step 5 — Convert amplitudes to powers.**

Since $|i A_{0}|^{2} = |A_{0}|^{2}$, the powers are:

$$P_{1}(z) = \vert A_{1}(z)\vert^{2} = P_{0} \cos^{2}(\kappa z)$$

$$P_{2}(z) = \vert A_{2}(z)\vert^{2} = P_{0} \sin^{2}(\kappa z)$$

**Step 6 — Verify power conservation.**

$$P_{1}(z) + P_{2}(z) = P_{0}\left[\cos^{2}(\kappa z) + \sin^{2}(\kappa z)\right] = P_{0} \checkmark$$

**Conclusion:**
The power in the cross port after an interaction length $L$ is:

$$\boxed{\,P_{2}(L) = P_{0} \sin^{2}(\kappa L)\,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> This sinusoidal behavior is the foundation of **all fused-fiber and planar directional couplers**. The coupling ratio is tuned by adjusting $L$ (the pulling length during tapering) until $\kappa L$ produces the desired split.

---

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Coupling Ratio in dB and Insertion Loss Numerical Computation

**Problem Setup:**
A 2×2 fused-fiber coupler has input power $P_{\text{in}} = 10$ mW at the input port (Port 1). After the coupling region, the measured powers are:
- Bar port (Port 2): $P_{\text{bar}} = 3.9$ mW
- Cross port (Port 3): $P_{\text{cross}} = 5.7$ mW
- Total output: $P_{\text{out,total}} = 9.6$ mW

**Step 1 — Compute the coupling ratio (linear).**

$$CR = \frac{P_{\text{cross}}}{P_{\text{cross}} + P_{\text{bar}}} = \frac{5.7}{5.7 + 3.9} = \frac{5.7}{9.6} = 0.59375$$

So the coupler splits **≈ 59.4% : 40.6%**.

**Step 2 — Compute the excess loss in dB.**

$$EL_{\text{(dB)}} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{out,total}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right) = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{9.6}{10}\right)$$

$$EL_{\text{(dB)}} = -10 \log_{10}(0.96) = -10 \times (-0.01773) = 0.1773 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the insertion loss of the cross port in dB.**

$$IL_{\text{cross (dB)}} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{cross}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right) = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{5.7}{10}\right)$$

$$IL_{\text{cross (dB)}} = -10 \log_{10}(0.57) = -10 \times (-0.2441) = 2.441 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 4 — Compute the insertion loss of the bar port in dB.**

$$IL_{\text{bar (dB)}} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{3.9}{10}\right) = -10 \log_{10}(0.39) = 4.090 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 5 — Interpret the results.**

- The **excess loss** of 0.18 dB indicates very good fabrication quality.
- The **cross port IL** of 2.44 dB is close to the ideal $10 \log_{10}(1/0.594) = 2.26$ dB.
- The slight difference (≈ 0.18 dB) corresponds exactly to the **excess loss**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation 3 — Faraday Rotation Angle for a 45° Isolator

**Problem Setup:**
Design an optical isolator using a Terbium Gallium Garnet (TGG) crystal with Verdet constant $V = 40$ rad·T⁻¹·m⁻¹. The available permanent magnet produces a uniform field $B = 0.4$ T. What crystal length $L$ is required to produce the **45°** rotation needed for proper isolator operation?

**Step 1 — Write the Faraday rotation equation.**

$$\theta = V \cdot B \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Convert the desired rotation to radians.**

$$\theta = 45° = \frac{\pi}{4} \text{ rad} \approx 0.7854 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for L.**

$$L = \frac{\theta}{V \cdot B} = \frac{0.7854}{40 \times 0.4} = \frac{0.7854}{16}$$

$$L = 0.04909 \text{ m} = 4.91 \text{ cm}$$

**Step 4 — Verify and interpret.**

A 4.91 cm TGG crystal in a 0.4 T field yields exactly 45° rotation. The strong magnet is essential because the Verdet constant is moderate. Note that for the same rotation using YIG (with $V = 20$ rad·T⁻¹·m⁻¹), one would need **twice the length** (≈ 9.82 cm), which is why TGG has largely replaced YIG in modern isolators.

---

### 3.4 Derivation 4 — Isolation in dB and Power Suppression Factor

**Problem Setup:**
An optical isolator has an input power $P_{\text{in}} = 1$ mW from a back-reflection. The leaked power to the protected port is measured as $P_{\text{leak}} = 1$ μW. What is the isolation in dB? By what factor has the back-reflection been suppressed?

**Step 1 — Compute isolation in dB.**

$$\text{Isolation}_{\text{(dB)}} = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{P_{\text{leak}}}{P_{\text{in}}}\right) = -10 \log_{10}\!\left(\frac{10^{-6}}{10^{-3}}\right)$$

$$= -10 \log_{10}(10^{-3}) = -10 \times (-3) = 30 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the suppression factor.**

$$\text{Suppression} = \frac{P_{\text{in}}}{P_{\text{leak}}} = 10^{\text{Isolation}/10} = 10^{30/10} = 10^{3} = 1000$$

**Step 3 — Physical interpretation.**

The back-reflection has been attenuated by a factor of **1000×**, reducing 1 mW to 1 μW. This is the minimum acceptable isolation for laser protection; high-end systems require **40 dB to 50 dB** isolation, corresponding to suppression factors of $10^{4}$ to $10^{5}$.

---

### 3.5 Derivation 5 — Splitting Loss of an N-Way Star Coupler

**Problem Setup:**
A 1×8 star coupler is used in a passive optical network. Calculate the ideal splitting loss and the total insertion loss assuming a per-port excess loss of 0.3 dB.

**Step 1 — Ideal splitting loss (no excess loss).**

$$\text{Loss}_{\text{1×N, ideal}} = 10 \log_{10}(N) = 10 \log_{10}(8) = 10 \times 0.9031 = 9.031 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 2 — Add the per-port excess loss.**

$$IL_{\text{total}} = 9.031 + 0.3 = 9.331 \text{ dB}$$

**Step 3 — Output power if input is 1 mW.**

$$P_{\text{out}} = P_{\text{in}} \times 10^{-IL_{\text{total}}/10} = 1 \text{ mW} \times 10^{-0.9331}$$

$$P_{\text{out}} = 1 \text{ mW} \times 0.1166 = 0.1166 \text{ mW} = 116.6 \text{ μW per port}$$

This matches the ideal $1/8 = 0.125$ mW reduced by the 0.3 dB excess loss.

---

### 3.6 Analytical Matrix — Quick Comparison of the Three Devices

| Property | Coupler | Isolator | Circulator |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Reciprocity** | Reciprocal | Non-reciprocal | Non-reciprocal |
| **Number of Ports** | 2, 3, 4, … N | 2 | 3 or 4 |
| **Active / Passive** | Passive | Passive | Passive |
| **Underlying Physics** | Evanescent coupling | Faraday effect | Faraday + birefringence |
| **Typical Insertion Loss** | 0.1 – 3 dB | 0.5 – 1 dB | 0.6 – 1.2 dB |
| **Typical Isolation / Directivity** | ≥ 50 dB | 30 – 50 dB | ≥ 40 dB |
| **Primary Application** | Power splitting, WDM | Laser protection | Bidirectional routing |
| **Tunable?** | Limited (factory-set) | No | No |

## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Block Diagram — Functional Architecture of a 2×2 Fused-Fiber Coupler

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[INPUT PORT 1\nOptical Signal]:::inport
    B[FUSED BICONICAL TAPER REGION\nEvanescent Field Coupling\nLength L, Coupling Coefficient kappa]:::couple
    C[BAR PORT 2\nThrough Port]:::outport
    D[CROSS PORT 3\nCoupled Port]:::outport
    E[PORT 4\nIsolated]:::blocked
    F[OUTPUT POWER SPLIT]:::result

    A --> B
    B -->|P_bar = P0 cos squared kappa L| C
    B -->|P_cross = P0 sin squared kappa L| D
    B -.->|Negligible Directivity Loss| E
    C --> F
    D --> F

    classDef inport fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    classDef couple fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    classDef outport fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#1a661a,color:#000
    classDef blocked fill:#f2d7d5,stroke:#7a1f1a,color:#000
    classDef result fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#7a6a00,color:#000
```

**Reading the Diagram:**
- Input light enters the fused taper from **Port 1**.
- The interaction length $L$ and coupling coefficient $\kappa$ determine how much power goes to the bar (through) versus cross (coupled) port.
- Port 4 is theoretically isolated by the device's high directivity (>50 dB).

---

### 4.2 Block Diagram — Internal Architecture of a Polarization-Dependent Optical Isolator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[INPUT FIBER\nUnpolarized Light]:::input
    B[INPUT POLARIZER P1\nVertical Axis]:::pol
    C[FARADAY ROTATOR\nTGG Crystal, B Field 0.4 T, L 4.9 cm\nRotation 45 degrees]:::faraday
    D[OUTPUT POLARIZER P2\n45 degree Relative to P1]:::pol
    E[OUTPUT FIBER\nForward Path Low Loss]:::out

    A --> B
    B -->|Transmits Vertical Component| C
    C -->|45 deg Rotation| D
    D -->|Matches P2 Orientation\nInsertion Loss Approx 0.5 dB| E

    style A fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style B fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style C fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066,color:#000
    style D fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600,color:#000
    style E fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#1a661a,color:#000
```

**Forward Path:** Input → P1 (vertical) → Faraday (+45°) → P2 (45° relative) → Output. Light passes through with minimal loss.

**Reverse Path (not shown in flow but described):**
Any back-reflected light enters P2 (oriented at 45°) → Faraday rotates it by another +45° (so it becomes vertical relative to the **backward** propagation direction) → P1 is horizontal, so it blocks the vertical component → high isolation (30–50 dB).

---

### 4.3 Block Diagram — Routing Logic of a 3-Port Optical Circulator

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CIRC[3 PORT OPTICAL CIRCULATOR]
        direction LR
        P1[PORT 1\nInput A]:::port
        P2[PORT 2\nForward Output]:::port
        P3[PORT 3\nBidirectional Output]:::port
        FR[CENTRAL FARADAY ROTATOR\n45 degrees Plus Birefringent Crystals\nNon Reciprocal Routing]:::rotator
    end

    P1 -->|Allowed Path| P2
    P2 -->|Allowed Path| P3
    P3 -->|Allowed Cyclic Path| P1
    P1 -.->|Blocked\nIsolation Approx 40 dB| P3
    P3 -.->|Blocked| P2
    P2 -.->|Blocked| P1

    style P1 fill:#cce5ff,stroke:#003366,color:#000
    style P2 fill:#d5f5d5,stroke:#1a661a,color:#000
    style P3 fill:#fff9c4,stroke:#7a6a00,color:#000
    style FR fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066,color:#000
```

**Interpretation:**
- The **solid arrows** represent the **allowed (low-loss)** cyclic propagation paths.
- The **dashed arrows** represent the **blocked (high-isolation)** forbidden paths.
- This enforces the unidirectional flow: $1 \to 2 \to 3 \to 1$.

---

### 4.4 Block Diagram — Comparative Functional Flow: Coupler vs. Isolator vs. Circulator

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph COUPLER[OPTICAL COUPLER]
        C_IN[Single Input] --> C_FBT[Fused Taper Region] --> C_OUT[Two or More Outputs\nPower Divided]
    end

    subgraph ISOLATOR[OPTICAL ISOLATOR]
        I_IN[Input] --> I_POL[Polarizer Plus Faraday Plus Analyzer] --> I_OUT[Protected Output]
        I_IN2[Back Reflection] --> I_POL2[Blocked By Analyzer]:::blocked
    end

    subgraph CIRCULATOR[OPTICAL CIRCULATOR]
        CIR_IN[Port 1] --> CIR_FAR[Faraday Plus Birefringent] --> CIR_OUT[Port 2 Output]
        CIR_OUT2[Port 2 Input] --> CIR_FAR2[Same Faraday Plus Birefringent] --> CIR_OUT3[Port 3 Output]
    end

    style C_FBT fill:#ffe0b3,stroke:#cc6600
    style I_POL fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066
    style I_POL2 fill:#f2d7d5,stroke:#7a1f1a
    style CIR_FAR fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066
    style CIR_FAR2 fill:#ffccff,stroke:#660066
```

**Key Insight from the Comparison:**
- A **coupler** is essentially a "splitter" — it divides power with no preference for direction.
- An **isolator** is essentially a "one-way valve" — it suppresses one direction completely.
- A **circulator** is essentially a "traffic roundabout" — it routes each input to a unique, directionally-determined output.

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**Differentiate between a 1×2 optical coupler and a 3-port optical circulator in terms of reciprocity, port count, and underlying physical principle.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Feature | 1×2 Optical Coupler | 3-Port Optical Circulator |
|---|---|---|
| **Reciprocity** | Reciprocal — symmetric in both directions | Non-reciprocal — direction-dependent routing |
| **Port Count** | 1 input, 2 outputs (3 ports total) | 3 ports (1, 2, 3) with cyclic routing |
| **Physical Principle** | Evanescent field coupling / fused taper | Faraday magneto-optic rotation in birefringent crystals |
| **Function** | Power splitting | Unidirectional routing |

The coupler divides input power passively and equally to both outputs (subject to coupling ratio), while the circulator routes Port 1 → Port 2 → Port 3 → Port 1 cyclically, blocking all other paths with high isolation (≥ 40 dB).

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**State and explain the Faraday effect. Why is it the foundation of non-reciprocal optical devices like isolators and circulators?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Faraday effect** (or magneto-optic effect) is the rotation of the plane of polarization of linearly polarized light as it propagates through a transparent medium placed in a magnetic field aligned with the light's direction of propagation.

The rotation angle is given by:
$$\theta = V \cdot B \cdot L$$

where $V$ is the **Verdet constant** of the material, $B$ is the magnetic flux density, and $L$ is the path length.

**Why it is non-reciprocal:**
Unlike natural optical activity, the sense of Faraday rotation is determined by the direction of the **magnetic field**, not by the direction of light propagation. Therefore, light traveling in the reverse direction experiences **additional** rotation in the **same absolute sense**, accumulating twice the rotation between two polarizers. This makes the device behave fundamentally differently in forward vs. reverse directions — the essence of non-reciprocity in isolators and circulators.

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a polarization-dependent optical isolator. Show how forward light is transmitted and backward light is blocked, using the principle of the Faraday effect. State typical values of insertion loss and isolation. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TGG crystal has a Verdet constant of $V = 40$ rad·T⁻¹·m⁻¹. It is placed in a magnetic field of $B = 0.5$ T. Calculate the crystal length $L$ required to produce a Faraday rotation of exactly 45°. If the same isolator is required to operate at 1310 nm instead of 1550 nm, comment on the design changes needed. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Question A):**

**(a) Construction and Working of a Polarization-Dependent Isolator (7 marks):**

**Block Diagram (textual representation for answer sheet):**

```
Input Fiber → Polarizer P1 (0°) → Faraday Rotator (45°) → Analyzer P2 (45°) → Output Fiber
                          ↖ (Reverse path blocked)
```

**Construction:**
A polarization-dependent isolator consists of three main components:
1. **Input Polarizer (P1):** A high-extinction-ratio (>30 dB) linear polarizer, conventionally oriented with its transmission axis at 0° (vertical).
2. **Faraday Rotator:** A magneto-optic crystal (typically TGG or YIG) placed inside a strong permanent magnet (SmCo or NdFeB, $B \approx 0.2$ to 0.5 T), designed to rotate the polarization by **exactly 45°**.
3. **Output Polarizer / Analyzer (P2):** A second linear polarizer oriented with its transmission axis at **+45°** relative to P1.

**Working — Forward Direction:**
- Unpolarized input light enters P1; only the vertical component is transmitted.
- The Faraday rotator rotates this vertical polarization by +45°.
- The rotated polarization now exactly matches the transmission axis of P2, so the light passes through with **minimal loss** (typical insertion loss: **0.5 dB to 1 dB**).

**Working — Reverse Direction (back-reflection):**
- Any back-reflected light enters the isolator from the output side, passing through P2 (which polarizes it at +45°).
- The Faraday rotator rotates it by **another +45°** (in the same absolute sense, because the magnetic field direction has not changed). After this rotation, the light is at +90° relative to P1's axis.
- P1, oriented vertically, completely **blocks** the horizontal component.
- This produces a high **isolation** of **30 dB to 50 dB** for the reverse direction.

**Typical Specifications:**
- Insertion Loss: **0.5 dB to 1 dB**
- Isolation: **30 dB to 50 dB**
- Return Loss: **≥ 50 dB**
- Operating Wavelength: 1310 nm or 1550 nm (telecom bands)

---

**(b) Faraday Rotator Length Calculation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Write the Faraday rotation formula.**

$$\theta = V \cdot B \cdot L$$

**Step 2 — Convert 45° to radians.**

$$\theta = 45° = \frac{\pi}{4} \text{ rad} \approx 0.7854 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the required length L.**

$$L = \frac{\theta}{V \cdot B} = \frac{0.7854}{40 \times 0.5} = \frac{0.7854}{20}$$

$$\boxed{L = 0.03927 \text{ m} \approx 3.93 \text{ cm}}$$

**Step 4 — Wavelength scaling considerations (1310 nm vs. 1550 nm).**

The Verdet constant has a wavelength dependence that approximately follows $V(\lambda) \propto 1/\lambda^{2}$ in the near-IR regime. Therefore:

$$\frac{V_{1310}}{V_{1550}} \approx \left(\frac{1550}{1310}\right)^{2} \approx 1.40$$

So the Verdet constant at 1310 nm is approximately **40% higher** than at 1550 nm:

$$V_{1310} \approx 40 \times 1.40 = 56 \text{ rad·T}^{-1}\text{·m}^{-1}$$

For the same 45° rotation at 1310 nm with the same $B = 0.5$ T:

$$L_{1310} = \frac{0.7854}{56 \times 0.5} = \frac{0.7854}{28} = 0.02805 \text{ m} \approx 2.81 \text{ cm}$$

**Design Change Required:**
The crystal length must be **reduced from 3.93 cm to about 2.81 cm** (a reduction of ≈ 28%) to maintain the 45° rotation. Alternatively, if the crystal length is fixed, the magnetic field must be reduced. The anti-reflection coatings on the crystal facets must also be optimized for the 1310 nm wavelength to minimize Fresnel reflections.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)**

**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** Explain the construction and working of a fused-fiber 2×2 optical coupler. Derive the expression for the cross-port power $P_{2}(L) = P_{0} \sin^{2}(\kappa L)$ using coupled-mode theory. State the condition for a 3-dB (50:50) coupler. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 2×2 fused-fiber coupler is fabricated with a coupling coefficient $\kappa = 0.5$ rad/mm. The desired output is a 90:10 splitting ratio. Calculate the required interaction length $L$. If the input power is 5 mW, calculate the power at each output port. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution (Question B):**

**(a) Construction, Working, and Derivation (7 marks):**

**Construction:**
A fused-fiber 2×2 coupler is fabricated by:
1. Stripping the protective coating from two single-mode fibers over a short length (≈ 2 cm).
2. Twisting the bare fibers together.
3. Heating the twisted region with a flame or electric arc to **≈ 1500°C** until the glass softens and the two fibers fuse.
4. Stretching (tapering) the fused region while monitoring the output power. The stretching reduces the core diameter and brings the cores closer, dramatically enhancing the evanescent field overlap.

**Working Principle:**
In the tapered waist, the cores become so thin that the guided mode is no longer tightly confined — the **evanescent field** extends well into the cladding. The modes of the two fibers overlap, enabling **evanescent coupling**. The longer the interaction region and the smaller the core separation, the greater the power transfer.

**Derivation — Coupled-Mode Theory:**

The coupled-mode equations for the slowly-varying amplitudes $A_{1}(z)$ and $A_{2}(z)$ in two identical, lossless, phase-matched waveguides are:

$$\frac{dA_{1}}{dz} = i \kappa A_{2}$$

$$\frac{dA_{2}}{dz} = i \kappa A_{1}$$

Differentiating the first equation and substituting the second:

$$\frac{d^{2}A_{1}}{dz^{2}} = i \kappa \cdot \frac{dA_{2}}{dz} = i \kappa (i \kappa A_{1}) = -\kappa^{2} A_{1}$$

The general solution is $A_{1}(z) = A_{0}\cos(\kappa z)$ (with boundary condition $A_{2}(0) = 0$).

From the second equation:

$$A_{2}(z) = \frac{1}{i \kappa}\frac{dA_{1}}{dz} = \frac{1}{i \kappa}(-A_{0}\kappa \sin(\kappa z)) = i A_{0}\sin(\kappa z)$$

Therefore, the powers are:

$$P_{1}(z) = \vert A_{0}\vert^{2} \cos^{2}(\kappa z) = P_{0}\cos^{2}(\kappa z)$$

$$P_{2}(z) = \vert i A_{0}\vert^{2} \sin^{2}(\kappa z) = P_{0}\sin^{2}(\kappa z)$$

$$\boxed{P_{2}(L) = P_{0}\sin^{2}(\kappa L)}$$

**Condition for a 3-dB (50:50) coupler:**
For equal power split, $P_{2} = P_{1} = P_{0}/2$, which requires $\sin^{2}(\kappa L) = 0.5$, i.e., $\kappa L = \pi/4$. Therefore, the **3-dB coupling length** is:

$$L_{3\text{dB}} = \frac{\pi}{4\kappa} = \frac{L_{\pi}}{2}$$

where $L_{\pi} = \pi/(2\kappa)$ is the **beat length** (full power transfer distance).

---

**(b) 90:10 Coupler — Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

**Step 1 — Identify the coupling ratio.**

For a 90:10 coupler, 90% of the power is in the bar port and 10% in the cross port:

$$P_{\text{cross}} = 0.10 \, P_{0} \quad \text{and} \quad P_{\text{bar}} = 0.90 \, P_{0}$$

**Step 2 — Apply the cross-port power formula.**

$$P_{2}(L) = P_{0} \sin^{2}(\kappa L) = 0.10 \, P_{0}$$

$$\sin^{2}(\kappa L) = 0.10 \implies \sin(\kappa L) = \sqrt{0.10} = 0.3162$$

$$\kappa L = \arcsin(0.3162) = 0.3217 \text{ rad}$$

**Step 3 — Solve for the interaction length L.**

$$L = \frac{0.3217}{\kappa} = \frac{0.3217}{0.5 \text{ rad/mm}} = 0.6434 \text{ mm}$$

$$\boxed{L \approx 0.643 \text{ mm} = 643 \text{ μm}}$$

**Step 4 — Calculate the power at each output port with $P_{0} = 5$ mW.**

- **Cross port (10%):**
$$P_{\text{cross}} = 0.10 \times 5 \text{ mW} = 0.50 \text{ mW}$$

- **Bar port (90%):**
$$P_{\text{bar}} = 0.90 \times 5 \text{ mW} = 4.50 \text{ mW}$$

- **Verification of power conservation (ideal case):**
$$P_{\text{bar}} + P_{\text{cross}} = 4.50 + 0.50 = 5.00 \text{ mW} = P_{0} \checkmark$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Sign convention error:** Many students forget the minus sign in the dB formula. Insertion loss is a **positive** number representing loss, so the formula is $IL = -10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{out}}/P_{\text{in}})$, which always yields a positive dB value when $P_{\text{out}} < P_{\text{in}}$.
> 2. **Confusing $\sin$ and $\sin^{2}$:** The coupling ratio uses $\sin^{2}(\kappa L)$, not $\sin(\kappa L)$. Forgetting the square gives incorrect power values.
> 3. **Mixing up Coupling Ratio and Excess Loss:** The coupling ratio is the **fractional** power split (e.g., 0.1 for 10%), while excess loss is the **absolute** power lost to scattering/absorption in the coupling region. Both must be calculated and reported.
> 4. **Forgetting the wavelength dependence of the Verdet constant:** When an isolator is re-designed for a new wavelength, the crystal length or magnetic field must be adjusted, because $V(\lambda)$ is not constant.
> 5. **Forgetting the non-reciprocal nature of Faraday rotation:** In reverse propagation, the rotation is **additive** (not subtractive). This is the most commonly missed conceptual point in the KTU exam.

---

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Optical Couplers, Isolators, and Circulators**

#### Optical Couplers
- **Definition:** Passive device that splits or combines optical power between two or more fibers.
- **Main types:** Fused-fiber (FBT), planar waveguide (PLC), bulk-optic.
- **Evanescent coupling:** Power transfers via the exponentially decaying field outside the core.
- **Coupled-mode result:** $P_{\text{cross}} = P_{0} \sin^{2}(\kappa L)$ and $P_{\text{bar}} = P_{0} \cos^{2}(\kappa L)$.
- **Beat length:** $L_{\pi} = \pi / (2\kappa)$ — the length for 100% power transfer.
- **3-dB condition:** $\kappa L = \pi/4$ → 50:50 split.
- **Reciprocal:** Light behaves the same in forward and reverse directions.
- **Key parameters:** Coupling Ratio, Excess Loss, Insertion Loss, Directivity.
- **Typical N-way loss:** $10 \log_{10}(N)$ dB (uniform split).

#### Optical Isolators
- **Definition:** Non-reciprocal 2-port device that passes light in one direction only.
- **Key principle:** Faraday magneto-optic rotation, $\theta = V \cdot B \cdot L$.
- **Construction:** Polarizer (P1) → Faraday Rotator (45°) → Analyzer (P2 at 45°).
- **Forward transmission:** Insertion loss **0.5 dB to 1 dB**.
- **Reverse isolation:** **30 dB to 50 dB**.
- **Critical property:** Faraday rotation is **non-reciprocal** — determined by $\vec{B}$ field, not propagation direction.
- **Common materials:** TGG ($V \approx 40$ rad·T⁻¹·m⁻¹), YIG ($V \approx 20$), FR-5 glass.
- **Polarization-independent variants** use birefringent crystals (YVO₄, rutile) to separate polarizations.
- **Primary use:** Laser diode protection from back-reflections.

#### Optical Circulators
- **Definition:** Non-reciprocal multi-port device routing light in a fixed cyclic order.
- **3-port routing:** 1→2, 2→3, 3→1; all other paths blocked.
- **4-port routing:** 1→2, 2→3, 3→4, 4→1; all other paths blocked.
- **Functionally equivalent to:** Two isolators sharing a common Faraday rotator.
- **Typical insertion loss:** 0.6 dB to 1.2 dB per port pair.
- **Typical isolation:** ≥ 40 dB to 50 dB.
- **Primary uses:** Bidirectional single-fiber transmission, OADMs, fiber gyroscopes, EDFAs.

#### Universal / Cross-Cutting Concepts
- **Reciprocity theorem:** A device is reciprocal if its transfer function is symmetric ($S_{ij} = S_{ji}$). Couplers are reciprocal; isolators and circulators are non-reciprocal.
- **Decibel conversions:** $\text{dB} = -10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{out}}/P_{\text{in}})$; power ratio = $10^{-dB/10}$.
- **Insertion loss ≠ Isolation:** Loss is in the desired direction; isolation is in the undesired direction.
- **Polarization Dependent Loss (PDL):** Variation in insertion loss with input state of polarization; should be ≤ 0.15 dB in well-designed components.
- **Return loss:** Always measured with respect to reflections back to the source; should be ≥ 50 dB.

#### KTU 2024 Frequently Tested Formulas (Memorize These)
1. $P_{\text{cross}} = P_{0} \sin^{2}(\kappa L)$
2. $L_{\pi} = \pi / (2\kappa)$
3. $\theta = V \cdot B \cdot L$
4. $IL_{\text{(dB)}} = -10 \log_{10}(P_{\text{out}} / P_{\text{in}})$
5. $10 \log_{10}(N)$ — splitting loss of 1×N coupler
6. $\text{Suppression factor} = 10^{\text{Isolation}/10}$

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Fiber Optic Sensors: Fiber Optic Instrumentation System for Measurement of Fiber Characteristics - Attenuation Measurement (Cut Back Method), Dispersion Measurement (Time Domain and Frequency Domain Method), Refractive Index Profile Measurements, Optical Time Domain Reflectometers, Fiber Numerical Aperture Measurement
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET413 - OPTICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Fiber Optic Sensors: Fiber Optic Instrumentation System for Measurement of Fiber Characteristics - Attenuation Measurement (Cut Back Method), Dispersion Measurement (Time Domain and Frequency Domain Method), Refractive Index Profile Measurements, Optical Time Domain Reflectometers, Fiber Numerical Aperture Measurement

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Fiber Optic Instrumentation System – Formal Definition

A **Fiber Optic Instrumentation System** is an integrated optical-electronic assembly designed to launch, propagate, detect, and quantitatively analyse light signals within an optical fiber so as to characterize its intrinsic physical and transmission properties. The major fiber characteristics that are measured using such instrumentation include **attenuation (loss)**, **dispersion**, **refractive index profile**, and **numerical aperture (NA)**. The instrumentation typically comprises a stabilized optical source (LED or laser), a fiber launching stage, the fiber under test (FUT), a photodetector, and a signal-processing unit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> "An optical fiber instrumentation system is a precise measurement setup employing controlled optical sources, photodetectors, and the fiber under test to evaluate key performance parameters such as attenuation coefficient, pulse broadening (dispersion), refractive index profile, and numerical aperture, in accordance with **EIA / TIA / IEC standards**."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The Water Hose Model

Imagine a long garden hose connected to a tap. As water flows through the hose, some water is lost due to leaks (representing **attenuation**), and the water pressure pulse that enters may exit smeared or delayed at the far end (representing **dispersion**). To know how much the hose leaks, you could either measure the water at both ends (the **cut-back method**) or send a pulse and listen to the echoes coming back (the **OTDR** principle). The opening through which water enters the hose sets the limit on the angle at which water can be poured in efficiently – this is analogous to the **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of the fiber. Finally, the internal diameter of the hose may not be uniform – this variation is captured by the **Refractive Index Profile** of the fiber.

## 1.3 Attenuation Measurement – The Cut-Back Method

**Definition:** Attenuation in an optical fiber is the loss of optical power as light travels along the fiber, expressed in decibels per kilometre (dB/km). The **Cut-Back Method** is a *destructive* but highly accurate technique recommended by **EIA-455-78 / FOTP-78** standards for measuring the total attenuation (intrinsic + bending losses) of a fiber.

**Intuition:** Just as one would measure how much fuel a long pipe consumes by comparing the fuel in vs. fuel out, the cut-back method compares the optical power emerging from a *full-length* fiber with the power from the *same fiber shortened to ~2 m* (where only connector/launch losses remain). The difference, scaled to per kilometre, gives the attenuation.

## 1.4 Dispersion Measurement

**Definition:** Dispersion is the spreading of an optical pulse as it propagates through a fiber, leading to **inter-symbol interference (ISI)** in digital communication. It is measured as pulse broadening (ns/km) in time domain or as bandwidth (MHz·km) in frequency domain.

**Intuition:** Think of a sprinter's starting pistol pulse. If all runners (different wavelength components) start together but reach the finish at different times because each runs at a slightly different speed, the finishing crowd will be smeared – this is pulse dispersion.

## 1.5 Refractive Index Profile (RIP) Measurements

**Definition:** The Refractive Index Profile n(r) describes how the refractive index varies radially across the core of an optical fiber. It directly determines the fiber's **bandwidth, NA, mode-coupling characteristics**, and **bending sensitivity**.

> [!NOTE]
> Modern graded-index multimode fibers are specifically engineered with a near-parabolic n(r) profile to **minimize intermodal dispersion**, making RIP measurement a critical quality control step in fiber manufacture.

## 1.6 Optical Time Domain Reflectometer (OTDR)

**Definition:** The **OTDR** is a non-destructive, single-ended fiber characterization instrument that injects optical pulses into a fiber and analyses the **backscattered (Rayleigh) and reflected (Fresnel) light** as a function of time to determine attenuation, splice losses, connector locations, and fault positions along the fiber.

**Intuition:** The OTDR works like **SONAR for optical fibers** – a sound/light pulse is sent in, and the echoes returning from scatterers and breaks are timed and analysed to map the fiber.

## 1.7 Fiber Numerical Aperture (NA) Measurement

**Definition:** The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of an optical fiber is a dimensionless figure of merit that quantifies the *light-gathering ability* of the fiber. It is defined as the sine of the maximum acceptance angle (θₐ) at which light entering the fiber will be totally internally reflected and guided.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Constants / Metrics used in this module:**
> - Speed of light in vacuum: **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**
> - Standard test wavelength: **850 nm, 1300 nm, 1550 nm**
> - NA range for standard silica fibers: **0.10 to 0.40**
> - Attenuation unit: **dB/km**
> - Dispersion unit: **ps/(nm·km)** for chromatic dispersion; **ns/km** for intermodal

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Attenuation Measurement – Cut-Back Method

### Operating Principle
1. A **stabilized optical source** (LED or laser) with a known wavelength (λ) is launched into the *full-length fiber under test (FUT)* through a stable launch condition (mode scrambler/filter recommended).
2. The output power **P_out (L)** is measured at the far end using a calibrated photodetector.
3. *Without disturbing the launch conditions*, the fiber is cut back to a length of **L_ref ≈ 1–2 m** from the input end (this short length is assumed lossless apart from connector).
4. The output power from the short fiber **P_out (L_ref)** is measured.
5. Attenuation per unit length is then computed.

### Key Engineering Facts
- The method is **destructive** (it destroys the FUT), so it is used mainly in **laboratory/quality control** settings.
- Launch conditions must remain identical between the two measurements; otherwise the result is erroneous.
- A **mode filter** is used at the input to ensure **equilibrium mode distribution (EMD)**, eliminating dependence on launch conditions.
- It is the **reference (standard) method** against which all other attenuation methods are calibrated.

> [!NOTE]
> **Typical measured attenuation values** in commercial silica fibers: ~**3 dB/km at 850 nm**, ~**0.5 dB/km at 1300 nm**, ~**0.2 dB/km at 1550 nm**.

## 2.2 Dispersion Measurement

### (a) Time Domain Method – Pulse Delay Measurement
A narrow optical pulse (width ~ tens of ps) is launched into the FUT, and the output pulse is recorded on a high-speed sampling oscilloscope. The **pulse broadening Δτ** over a length L gives the dispersion.

### (b) Frequency Domain Method – Swept Frequency / Phase Shift
The fiber is excited with an intensity-modulated sinusoid. By sweeping the modulation frequency, the **baseband frequency response** H(f) is obtained. The **−3 dB bandwidth (f₃dB)** and total dispersion are extracted.

## 2.3 Refractive Index Profile – Measurement Techniques

| Method | Principle | Resolution | Destructive? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Interferometric Method** | Two-beam interference between sample and reference beam | High (~1 µm) | Yes (thin slice) |
| **Near-Field Scanning Method** | Scans the magnified near-field intensity at the output face | ~1 µm | No |
| **Refracted Ray Method** | Measures the deflection angle of a focused laser ray traversing the fiber cross-section | ~1–2 µm | No |
| **Reflected Ray Method** | Measures light reflected back from cleaved end at different radial positions | Moderate | No |

> [!NOTE]
> The **Refracted Near-Field (RNF) method** is the **most widely accepted** industrial method and is the **IEC standard** for measuring RIP.

## 2.4 Optical Time Domain Reflectometer (OTDR)

### Working Principle
- OTDR injects **high-power, narrow optical pulses** (λ = 850, 1300, 1550 nm) into one end of the fiber.
- As the pulse travels, **Rayleigh backscattering** continuously returns a small fraction of the light to the detector.
- **Fresnel reflections** occur at discrete discontinuities (connectors, splices, breaks, fiber ends).
- The OTDR plots **backscattered power (dB) vs. distance (km)** – this plot is called the **OTDR trace or signature**.

### Major Block Diagram Functional Sections
1. **Pulse generator & laser source**
2. **Directional coupler (beamsplitter)**
3. **Photodetector (APD)**
4. **Signal averager (signal processor)**
5. **Display (CRT/LCD) – Trace display**

## 2.5 Numerical Aperture Measurement

**Definition Equation:**
$$NA = n_0 \, \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

where:
- **n₀** = refractive index of the surrounding medium (≈ 1.0 for air)
- **θₐ** = maximum acceptance angle (half-angle of the acceptance cone)
- **n₁** = refractive index of core
- **n₂** = refractive index of cladding

### Measurement Methods
1. **Far-Field Scan Method:** The angular distribution of output power P(θ) is measured. NA = sin θₐ, where θₐ is the angle at which the power drops to 5% of maximum.
2. **Spot Size / Mode Field Diameter Method:** For single-mode fibers, NA is inferred from the mode field diameter.
3. **Refracted Power Method:** The power transmitted through a small numerical-aperture-matched sink is measured.

## 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula | Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Attenuation coefficient (Cut-Back) | $\alpha = \dfrac{10}{L - L_{ref}} \log_{10} \left(\dfrac{P_{ref}}{P_{out}}\right)$ | **dB/km** | $L$, $L_{ref}$ in km |
| 2 | Total fiber loss | $A = \alpha L$ | **dB** | |
| 3 | Power ratio – loss | $A_{dB} = -10 \log_{10}(P_{out}/P_{in})$ | **dB** | |
| 4 | Intermodal dispersion (MMF) | $\Delta\tau = \dfrac{n_1 L}{c} \left(\dfrac{n_1}{n_2} - 1\right) = \dfrac{n_1 L \Delta}{c}$ | **ns** | $\Delta = (n_1 - n_2)/n_1$ |
| 5 | RMS pulse broadening | $\sigma_{out} = \sqrt{\sigma_{in}^2 + \sigma_{fiber}^2}$ | **ps** | |
| 6 | Bandwidth × Length product | $B \cdot L = \dfrac{0.44}{\Delta\tau}$ | **MHz·km** | Gaussian approx. |
| 7 | Chromatic dispersion coefficient | $D(\lambda) = \dfrac{S_0}{4}(\lambda - \lambda_0^4/\lambda^3)$ | **ps/(nm·km)** | $S_0$ = slope |
| 8 | Refractive index profile (power-law) | $n(r) = n_1 \sqrt{1 - 2\Delta (r/a)^{\alpha}}$ | dimensionless | $\alpha$ = profile parameter |
| 9 | Numerical Aperture | $NA = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta}$ | dimensionless | |
| 10 | Acceptance angle | $\theta_a = \sin^{-1}(NA/n_0)$ | **radians / degrees** | $n_0 \approx 1$ for air |
| 11 | OTDR distance resolution | $\Delta z = \dfrac{c \, t_p}{2 n_1}$ | **m** | $t_p$ = pulse width |
| 12 | OTDR backscatter level | $P_{BS} = P_0 \cdot S \cdot \alpha_s \cdot \dfrac{c t_p}{2 n_1}$ | **W** | $S$ = capture fraction |
| 13 | Return loss | $RL = -10 \log_{10}(P_{ref}/P_{in})$ | **dB** | |
| 14 | Mode field diameter (Gaussian) | $MFD = 2\omega_0$ | **µm** | Single-mode fiber |
| 15 | V-number (cutoff) | $V = \dfrac{2\pi a}{\lambda} NA$ | dimensionless | $V < 2.405$ for SMF |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Utility:**
> - **OTDR** is the *single most-used* field instrument for **installation, commissioning, and maintenance** of fiber-optic links.
> - **Cut-Back Method** is preferred in **fiber manufacturing QA** because of its unmatched accuracy.
> - **NA measurement** dictates **splice loss estimation, source-fiber coupling efficiency**, and **connector design**.
> - **RIP measurement** is critical for **bandwidth grading** of graded-index multimode fibers (e.g., OM1–OM5).

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation of Attenuation Coefficient (Cut-Back Method)

### Starting Assumptions
- Light from a stable source of wavelength λ is launched into the fiber under test (FUT).
- The launch conditions are **constant and reproducible** for both the long fiber (length L) and the cut-back reference fiber (length L_ref).
- The reference fiber is short enough (1–2 m) that its internal attenuation is negligible.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1:** Write the output power from the long fiber (length L):

$$P_{out}(L) = P_{in} \cdot \eta_{launch} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L / 10}$$

where **η_launch** is the launch efficiency and **α** is the attenuation coefficient in dB/km.

**Step 2:** Write the output power from the cut-back fiber (length L_ref):

$$P_{out}(L_{ref}) = P_{in} \cdot \eta_{launch} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L_{ref}/10}$$

**Step 3:** Form the ratio to eliminate P_in and η_launch (since they are identical in both measurements):

$$\dfrac{P_{out}(L_{ref})}{P_{out}(L)} = \dfrac{P_{in} \cdot \eta_{launch} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L_{ref}/10}}{P_{in} \cdot \eta_{launch} \cdot 10^{-\alpha L/10}}$$

**Step 4:** Cancel the common terms:

$$\dfrac{P_{ref}}{P_{out}} = 10^{(-\alpha L_{ref} + \alpha L)/10} = 10^{\alpha(L - L_{ref})/10}$$

**Step 5:** Take the base-10 logarithm of both sides:

$$\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{ref}}{P_{out}}\right) = \dfrac{\alpha(L - L_{ref})}{10}$$

**Step 6:** Solve for α:

$$\boxed{\alpha = \dfrac{10}{L - L_{ref}} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{ref}}{P_{out}}\right) \;\; \text{dB/km}}$$

**Numerical Example (KTU-style):**
A 5 km fiber gives P_out = 100 µW. After cutting back to 2 m, the power is 980 µW. Find α.

$$L - L_{ref} = 5 - 0.002 = 4.998 \text{ km}$$

$$\alpha = \dfrac{10}{4.998}\log_{10}\left(\dfrac{980}{100}\right) = \dfrac{10}{4.998} \times \log_{10}(9.8)$$

$$\alpha = \dfrac{10 \times 0.9912}{4.998} = \dfrac{9.912}{4.998} \approx 1.983 \text{ dB/km}$$

> [!NOTE]
> This value (~2 dB/km) is consistent with **operating near 850 nm** in a typical silica multimode fiber.

---

## 3.2 Derivation of Intermodal Dispersion (Time Domain)

**Starting principle:** Different modes (rays) travel different geometrical path lengths inside the multimode fiber. The **fastest** ray is the **axial (meridional) ray** with path length = L. The **slowest** is the **highest-angle ray** that just undergoes total internal reflection, with effective path length = **L / cos θ_c**, where **sin θ_c = n₂/n₁**.

**Step 1:** Time taken by the axial ray:

$$t_{min} = \dfrac{n_1 L}{c}$$

**Step 2:** Time taken by the highest-angle ray:

$$t_{max} = \dfrac{n_1 L}{c \cos \theta_c}$$

**Step 3:** Pulse broadening = time difference:

$$\Delta\tau = t_{max} - t_{min} = \dfrac{n_1 L}{c}\left(\dfrac{1}{\cos \theta_c} - 1\right)$$

**Step 4:** Use cos θ_c = √(1 − sin²θ_c) = √(1 − n₂²/n₁²):

$$\Delta\tau = \dfrac{n_1 L}{c}\left(\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - n_2^2/n_1^2}} - 1\right)$$

**Step 5:** Apply the binomial expansion for small Δ = (n₁ − n₂)/n₁:

$$\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1 - n_2^2/n_1^2}} \approx 1 + \dfrac{n_2^2}{2 n_1^2} \approx 1 + \Delta$$

**Step 6:** Substituting:

$$\Delta\tau \approx \dfrac{n_1 L \Delta}{c}$$

$$\boxed{\Delta\tau = \dfrac{n_1 L \Delta}{c} = \dfrac{n_1^2 L}{n_1 c}\dfrac{n_1 - n_2}{n_1} = \dfrac{(n_1 - n_2) L}{c}}$$

**Numerical example:** For n₁ = 1.48, n₂ = 1.46, L = 1 km, c = 3×10⁸ m/s.

$$\Delta\tau = \dfrac{(1.48 - 1.46) \times 1000}{3 \times 10^8} = \dfrac{20}{3 \times 10^8} = 66.7 \text{ ns/km}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation of Frequency Domain – 3 dB Optical Bandwidth

**Step 1:** The baseband frequency response of a fiber is well approximated by a **Gaussian transfer function**:

$$H(f) = \exp\left[-\left(\dfrac{f}{f_3}\right)^2 \ln 2\right]$$

where **f₃** is the −3 dB optical bandwidth (frequency at which H(f) = ½).

**Step 2:** The corresponding impulse response (pulse) is also Gaussian with RMS width σ:

$$\sigma = \dfrac{0.44}{f_3}$$

**Step 3:** For a fiber of length L, the total RMS broadening is:

$$\sigma_{out}^2 = \sigma_{in}^2 + \sigma_{fiber}^2$$

**Step 4:** For a sufficiently long fiber, σ_in ≪ σ_fiber, so:

$$\sigma_{fiber} \approx \sigma_{out} = \dfrac{0.44}{B \cdot L}$$

**Step 5:** Therefore, the **bandwidth × length product** is independent of length (a key engineering figure of merit):

$$\boxed{B \cdot L = \dfrac{0.44}{\sigma_{fiber}} = \dfrac{0.44}{\Delta\tau} \;\; \text{MHz·km}}$$

---

## 3.4 Derivation of Numerical Aperture from Snell's Law

**Step 1:** Consider a ray entering the fiber end-face from air (n₀ = 1) at the maximum acceptance angle θₐ.

**Step 2:** At the air–core interface, apply **Snell's Law**:

$$n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \sin \theta_1$$

where **θ₁** is the angle of refraction inside the core.

**Step 3:** For the ray to be guided, it must strike the core–cladding interface at the **critical angle θ_c**:

$$n_1 \sin \theta_c = n_2 \sin 90° \quad \Rightarrow \quad \sin \theta_c = \dfrac{n_2}{n_1}$$

**Step 4:** From geometry, **θ₁ + θ_c = 90°**, hence **sin θ₁ = cos θ_c**:

$$\sin \theta_1 = \cos \theta_c = \sqrt{1 - \sin^2 \theta_c} = \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}}$$

**Step 5:** Substituting in Snell's law:

$$n_0 \sin \theta_a = n_1 \sqrt{1 - \dfrac{n_2^2}{n_1^2}} = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2}$$

**Step 6:** For air, n₀ = 1, giving the standard **NA equation**:

$$\boxed{NA = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta}}$$

---

## 3.5 Derivation of OTDR Distance Measurement

**Step 1:** The OTDR records the **round-trip time t** of the backscattered light from a position z in the fiber.

**Step 2:** Round-trip distance = 2z, and the light travels at velocity **c/n₁** inside the fiber:

$$2z = \dfrac{c \, t}{n_1}$$

**Step 3:** Therefore, the distance from the input end is:

$$\boxed{z = \dfrac{c \, t}{2 n_1} = \dfrac{v_g \, t}{2}}$$

**Step 4:** **Spatial resolution Δz** is set by the pulse width t_p (the shorter the pulse, the finer the resolution):

$$\Delta z = \dfrac{c \, t_p}{2 n_1}$$

**Example:** For t_p = 10 ns, n₁ = 1.5:

$$\Delta z = \dfrac{3 \times 10^8 \times 10 \times 10^{-9}}{2 \times 1.5} = 1 \text{ m}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Engineering Insight:** Shorter pulse → better resolution BUT lower signal-to-noise ratio (less backscattered energy). OTDR operators must balance these two competing requirements.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram – Cut-Back Method for Attenuation Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Stabilized Optical Source LED Laser] --> B[Mode Filter and Mode Scrambler]
    B --> C[Launch Coupler and Connector]
    C --> D[Fiber Under Test FUT Long Length L]
    D --> E[Photodetector 1 Power P out L]
    E --> F[Signal Processor and Power Meter]
    D -.Cut Back to 2 m.-> G[Short Reference Fiber L ref]
    G --> H[Photodetector 2 Power P ref]
    H --> F
    F --> I[Computation of Attenuation alpha dB per km]
```

**Description:** The light from the source is launched into the FUT via a mode scrambler (to ensure **Equilibrium Mode Distribution**). Power **P_out(L)** is measured. The fiber is then cut back to ~2 m, and **P_ref** is measured. The signal processor computes **α = (10/(L − L_ref)) log₁₀(P_ref/P_out)**.

---

## 4.2 Block Diagram – Time Domain Dispersion Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1[Pulsed Laser Source ps Pulse Width] --> A2[Launch Optics]
    A2 --> A3[Fiber Under Test]
    A3 --> A4[High Speed Photodetector]
    A4 --> A5[Sampling Oscilloscope]
    A5 --> A6[Signal Analyzer and Display]
    A5 -.Reference pulse.-> A7[Delay Line]
    A7 -.Trigger.-> A1
```

**Description:** Narrow pulses are injected; the broadened output pulse is captured and compared with the input. The temporal broadening **Δτ** is measured directly, and the **bandwidth × length product B·L = 0.44/Δτ** is computed.

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram – Frequency Domain Dispersion Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    B1[RF Signal Generator Sweep f] --> B2[Electro Optic Modulator EOM]
    B3[Laser Source CW] --> B2
    B2 --> B4[Fiber Under Test]
    B4 --> B5[Photodetector]
    B5 --> B6[RF Spectrum Analyzer or Vector Voltmeter]
    B6 --> B7[Compute f3dB and B times L]
```

**Description:** The CW laser is intensity-modulated by the EOM driven by the swept RF source. The detected RF amplitude vs. frequency gives the baseband response H(f); the −3 dB point yields the fiber bandwidth.

---

## 4.4 Block Diagram – Optical Time Domain Reflectometer (OTDR)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    C1[Pulse Generator] --> C2[Laser Diode High Power Pulsed]
    C2 --> C3[Directional Coupler 3 dB]
    C3 --> C4[Fiber Under Test]
    C4 -.Backscattered and Reflected Light.-> C3
    C3 --> C5[Avalanche Photodiode APD]
    C5 --> C6[Amplifier and Signal Averager]
    C6 --> C7[Time to Distance Converter]
    C7 --> C8[Display Unit OTDR Trace Power vs Distance]
    C1 -.Synchronization.-> C7
```

**Description:** The directional coupler separates the forward-propagating pulse from the much weaker returning backscatter. The APD detects it, and a signal averager improves SNR by integrating many pulses. The trace reveals attenuation slope, splice points, breaks, and end-of-fiber reflection.

---

## 4.5 Block Diagram – Numerical Aperture Measurement (Far-Field Method)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    D1[Lambertian Source] --> D2[Fiber Under Test]
    D2 --> D3[Rotating Photodetector Arm]
    D3 --> D4[Amplifier]
    D4 --> D5[X Y Recorder or Computer]
    D5 --> D6[Plot P theta vs theta]
    D6 --> D7[Find 5 percent Power Angle theta a]
    D7 --> D8[Compute NA equals sin theta a]
```

**Description:** Output power is measured as a function of angle θ. The angle at which the power falls to a defined fraction (typically 5%) of the peak is taken as the acceptance half-angle θₐ, from which **NA = sin θₐ** is computed.

---

## 4.6 Block Diagram – Refracted Near-Field (RNF) RIP Measurement

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    E1[He Ne or Laser Source 633 nm] --> E2[Beam Splitter]
    E2 --> E3[Lens Focusing On Fiber End Face]
    E3 --> E4[Fiber Under Test Index Matching Bath]
    E4 -.Refracted Rays.-> E5[Photodetector with Pinhole]
    E2 -.Reference Beam.-> E6[Reference Photodetector]
    E5 --> E7[Ratio Detection and Signal Processing]
    E6 --> E7
    E7 --> E8[Plot n r vs r Refractive Index Profile]
```

**Description:** A focused laser scans across the fiber end face. The refractive index at each radial position is determined from the ratio of refracted power to reference power. The output is the **complete refractive index profile n(r)**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Question 1 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Question]`

**Explain the principle of attenuation measurement in optical fibers using the cut-back method. State the formula for attenuation coefficient.**

#### Model Answer:
The **cut-back method** is a destructive technique used to measure the attenuation coefficient of an optical fiber, standardized under **FOTP-78 / EIA-455-78**.

**Principle:** A stabilized optical source of known wavelength is launched into a long length L of the fiber under test (FUT). The output power **P_out** is measured. The fiber is then cut back to a short reference length **L_ref** (≈ 1–2 m) and the output power **P_ref** is measured under identical launch conditions.
