Since the launch conditions remain unchanged, the difference in power is purely due to the extra length (L − L_ref) of fiber.

**Formula:**
$$\alpha = \dfrac{10}{L - L_{ref}} \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{P_{ref}}{P_{out}}\right) \;\; \text{dB/km}$$

**Advantages:** Highest accuracy; reference standard.
**Disadvantages:** Destructive; cannot be used in the field.

---

### **Question 2 (3 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022, Model Question]`

**What is an Optical Time Domain Reflectometer (OTDR)? Mention its major applications.**

#### Model Answer:
An **OTDR (Optical Time Domain Reflectometer)** is a non-destructive, single-ended fiber-optic test instrument that injects narrow high-power optical pulses into a fiber and analyses the **Rayleigh backscattered light** and **Fresnel reflections** returned from various points along the fiber. It produces a **trace of backscattered power (dB) versus distance (km)**, from which the fiber attenuation, splice/connector losses, fault locations, and end-of-fiber are determined.

**Major Applications:**
1. Measurement of fiber attenuation coefficient and identification of attenuation uniformity.
2. Location of **breaks, bends, and faults** in the fiber.
3. Verification of **splice and connector losses**.
4. Verification of cable plant during installation, commissioning, and maintenance.
5. Measurement of **fiber length** in installed cables.

---

## PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) – KTU Internal Choice Format

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 / Model Paper]`

#### (a) **(7 Marks)** Describe in detail the **time-domain and frequency-domain methods** for measuring dispersion in optical fibers. Compare the two methods.

#### Model Solution:

**Time Domain Method (Pulse Delay / Time-of-Flight):**

**Setup:** A narrow optical pulse (e.g., from a laser diode or mode-locked laser) of width ~ 50–500 ps is launched into the fiber under test. The output pulse is detected by a high-speed photodetector and displayed on a **sampling oscilloscope**.

**Procedure:**
1. Record the input pulse width **τ_in** (RMS).
2. Record the output pulse width **τ_out** (RMS).
3. Compute the fiber-induced broadening:

$$\sigma_{fiber} = \sqrt{\sigma_{out}^2 - \sigma_{in}^2}$$

4. Compute the bandwidth-length product:

$$B \cdot L = \dfrac{0.44}{\sigma_{fiber}} \;\; \text{MHz·km}$$

**Advantages:** Direct measurement, easy to interpret.
**Disadvantages:** Requires ultra-short pulse sources and very fast detectors; affected by source chirp.

**Frequency Domain Method (Swept Frequency / Baseband Response):**

**Setup:** A CW laser is intensity-modulated by an **electro-optic modulator (EOM)** driven by a **swept RF signal generator** (e.g., 100 MHz to 10 GHz). The modulated signal passes through the fiber, is detected, and its amplitude is measured on an **RF spectrum analyzer** or **network analyzer**.

**Procedure:**
1. Sweep the modulation frequency f from low to high.
2. Record the detected RF amplitude A(f) at each frequency.
3. Normalize: H(f) = A(f)/A(0).
4. Identify the **−3 dB frequency f₃** where H(f) = 0.5.
5. Compute the bandwidth-length product: B·L = f₃ · L (MHz·km).

**Advantages:** High precision; can resolve modal vs. chromatic dispersion; less sensitive to source pulse width.
**Disadvantages:** Requires expensive EOM and RF instrumentation.

**Comparison Table:**

| Parameter | Time Domain | Frequency Domain |
|---|---|---|
| Measurement quantity | Pulse width | Bandwidth (Hz) |
| Source | Pulsed laser | CW laser + EOM |
| Detector | Fast photodiode + scope | Standard photodiode + VNA |
| Resolution | Limited by pulse width | Limited by EOM bandwidth |
| Computation | σ-based | f₃-based |
| Sensitivity to source chirp | High | Low |

---

#### (b) **(7 Marks)** Explain the **principle and operation of an OTDR** with the help of a neat block diagram. Derive the relationship between round-trip time and distance.

#### Model Solution:

**Principle of OTDR:**
The OTDR exploits the **Rayleigh backscattering** phenomenon. As a high-power optical pulse propagates through a fiber, microscopic refractive-index fluctuations (on the order of one wavelength) cause a small fraction of the light (typically 0.0001% per meter) to scatter in all directions. A tiny portion of this is captured by the fiber and returned to the source end. The OTDR measures this returned power as a function of time.

**Block Diagram:** (Refer to Section 4.4 of these notes.)
1. **Pulse Generator** – drives the laser diode with a narrow current pulse (10 ns – 20 µs).
2. **Laser Diode** – emits high-peak-power pulse at 850, 1300, or 1550 nm.
3. **Directional Coupler (3 dB)** – separates forward pulse from the returning backscatter (protects the laser).
4. **Fiber Under Test.**
5. **Avalanche Photodiode (APD)** – detects the very weak backscattered light.
6. **Signal Averager** – improves SNR by averaging over many pulses.
7. **Time-to-Distance Converter & Display** – plots backscattered power (dB) vs. distance (km).

**OTDR Trace Features:**
- **Slope** of trace = attenuation coefficient (dB/km).
- **Step changes** at splice/connector locations = insertion loss.
- **Sharp upward spikes** = Fresnel reflections (connectors, breaks).
- **Drop to noise floor** = end of fiber or break.

**Derivation of Distance Relationship:**

Light travels at speed **v = c/n₁** inside the fiber. If the round-trip time from the launch to a point at distance z and back is t, then:

$$2z = v \cdot t = \dfrac{c \, t}{n_1}$$

$$z = \dfrac{c \, t}{2 n_1}$$

Therefore, the **spatial resolution** set by the pulse width t_p is:

$$\Delta z = \dfrac{c \, t_p}{2 n_1}$$

For example, t_p = 10 ns, n₁ = 1.5: Δz = 1 m.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 / Model Paper]`

#### (a) **(7 Marks)** Discuss the methods used for the measurement of the **refractive index profile (RIP)** of an optical fiber. Explain the **Refracted Near-Field (RNF) method** in detail.

#### Model Solution:

**Introduction:**
The **refractive index profile (RIP)** describes how the refractive index varies radially across the fiber cross-section. It determines the fiber's NA, mode propagation, and bandwidth. Common RIP measurement methods include:

1. **Interferometric Method (Mach-Zehnder):** A thin slice of the fiber is placed in one arm of a Mach-Zehnder interferometer. The phase shift between the two beams, observed as a fringe shift, gives n(r). Resolution ~1 µm. Destructive, but very accurate.
2. **Near-Field Scanning Method:** The output face of an over-filled fiber is imaged onto a scanning pinhole. The intensity profile is proportional to n²(r) − n₂². Non-destructive.
3. **Refracted Ray (Refracted Near-Field, RNF) Method:** A focused laser beam is scanned across the fiber end face; the refracted power is measured. This is the **IEC standard** method.
4. **Reflected Ray Method:** Measures the light reflected back from a polished end face. Resolution lower than RNF.

**Refracted Near-Field (RNF) Method – Detailed Explanation:**

**Principle:** When a focused laser beam is incident on the end face of a fiber immersed in an index-matching fluid (n_L > n₂), part of the light is refracted out of the core into the fluid. The power of this refracted light is a function of the local refractive index at the point of incidence.

**Setup:** (Refer to Section 4.6 of these notes.)
- A **He-Ne laser (633 nm)** is split into two beams by a beamsplitter.
- The signal beam is focused by a high-N.A. microscope objective onto a spot (~1 µm) on the fiber end face, which is immersed in an index-matching liquid (n_L).
- The refracted power is collected by a photodetector with a pinhole.
- The reference beam goes to a second (reference) photodetector for normalization.
- The fiber is translated laterally so the focused spot scans the full end face.
- The ratio (P_signal / P_reference) is recorded as a function of radial position r.

**Mathematical Relationship:**
The refractive index at the scanning point r is related to the measured power P(r) by:

$$P(r) = P_0 \left[1 - \left(\dfrac{n(r) - n_L}{n_1 - n_L}\right)^2\right]$$

From this, the absolute n(r) is obtained by calibration.

**Advantages of RNF:**
- Non-destructive.
- High spatial resolution (~1 µm).
- Gives **absolute refractive index values**, not just relative profile.
- Standard industrial method (IEC and TIA recommended).

**Limitations:**
- Requires index-matching fluid.
- Sensitive to surface quality of the fiber end face.
- Resolution is limited by the focused spot size.

---

#### (b) **(7 Marks)** Explain the **measurement of Numerical Aperture (NA)** of an optical fiber. Describe the **far-field scan method** with a neat diagram and derive the expression for NA.

#### Model Solution:

**Definition:**
The **Numerical Aperture (NA)** of an optical fiber is the sine of the maximum acceptance angle θₐ at which an entering ray will be guided by total internal reflection. It is given by:

$$NA = \sin \theta_a = \sqrt{n_1^2 - n_2^2} = n_1 \sqrt{2\Delta}$$

where **Δ = (n₁ − n₂)/n₁** is the relative index difference.

**Methods of Measurement:**
1. **Far-Field Scan Method** (most common; standardized)
2. **Refracted Power Method**
3. **Spot-Size / Mode Field Diameter Method** (for single-mode fibers)

**Far-Field Scan Method – Procedure:**

**Setup:** (Refer to Section 4.5 of these notes.)
- A **Lambertian source** (uniform angular distribution) is used to overfill the input end of the fiber.
- The output end of the fiber is placed at the centre of a rotating goniometric arm carrying a photodetector with a small aperture.
- The photodetector rotates from θ = 0° to θ = 90° (or beyond), and the output power P(θ) is recorded at each angle.
- The angular distribution is plotted: P(θ) vs. θ.

**Determination of NA:**
- The output power decreases with increasing θ.
- The **maximum acceptance angle θₐ** is defined as the angle at which the power falls to a specified fraction of the peak value. Common criteria are:
  - **5% of peak power** (commonly used in IEC standards)
  - **10% of peak power**
- The **NA is then computed** as:

$$\boxed{NA = \sin \theta_a}$$

**Derivation of NA from Snell's Law:** (Refer to Section 3.4 of these notes for the complete derivation.)

Recap:
- At the air-core interface: **n₀ sin θₐ = n₁ sin θ₁**
- At the critical condition: **sin θ_c = n₂/n₁** and **θ₁ + θ_c = 90°**
- Solving: **NA = sin θₐ = √(n₁² − n₂²) = n₁√(2Δ)**

**Practical Note:**
For a typical silica fiber with n₁ = 1.48 and n₂ = 1.46:

$$NA = \sqrt{1.48^2 - 1.46^2} = \sqrt{2.1904 - 2.1316} = \sqrt{0.0588} \approx 0.243$$

This corresponds to θₐ ≈ 14°, an acceptance half-angle of about 14°.

**Sources of Error in NA Measurement:**
- Detector aperture size
- Mode scrambling at the input (may give incorrect EMD)
- Fiber length (long fibers scramble modes, giving more accurate value)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout – Where Students Lose Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing "attenuation" and "dispersion":** Attenuation is *power loss*; dispersion is *pulse spreading*. Examiners *specifically check* this distinction.
> 2. **Forgetting to include launch conditions** when describing the cut-back method. *Always* mention **mode scrambler / EMD** for full marks.
> 3. **Mixing up NA formulas:** NA = sin θₐ **and** NA = √(n₁² − n₂²) are *equivalent* but the examiner expects you to *derive* the second from the first.
> 4. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in OTDR distance:** Round-trip = 2z. A common error is writing z = ct/n₁ instead of z = ct/(2n₁).
> 5. **Confusing "Rayleigh backscatter" with "Fresnel reflection"** in OTDR theory. Backscatter is a continuous weak signal; Fresnel reflections are sharp spikes at discrete interfaces.
> 6. **Omitting units** in the formula sheet. KTU examiners deduct marks for unit-less final answers.
> 7. **Confusing intermodal dispersion** (in MMF, ns/km) with **chromatic dispersion** (in SMF, ps/(nm·km)). Both are types of dispersion, but the units and governing equations differ.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist – Module 2 (Fiber Characterization)**

### A. Attenuation
- ☐ Attenuation unit is **dB/km**.
- ☐ Formula: **α = (10/(L − L_ref)) log₁₀(P_ref/P_out)**.
- ☐ Cut-Back is the **destructive but most accurate** method; standardized under **FOTP-78**.
- ☐ Use a **mode scrambler** to ensure **Equilibrium Mode Distribution (EMD)**.
- ☐ Insertion loss = 10 log₁₀(P_in/P_out).
- ☐ Typical values: **3 dB/km @ 850 nm**, **0.5 dB/km @ 1300 nm**, **0.2 dB/km @ 1550 nm**.

### B. Dispersion
- ☐ Intermodal dispersion: **Δτ = n₁ L Δ / c** (ns/km in MMF).
- ☐ Chromatic dispersion unit: **ps/(nm·km)**.
- ☐ Bandwidth–Length product: **B·L = 0.44/Δτ** (MHz·km, Gaussian approx.).
- ☐ Time domain → measure pulse broadening.
- ☐ Frequency domain → measure −3 dB bandwidth.

### C. Refractive Index Profile (RIP)
- ☐ RNF method is the **IEC standard**.
- ☐ Index-matching liquid with **n_L > n₂** is essential.
- ☐ Power-law profile: **n(r) = n₁ √(1 − 2Δ(r/a)^α)**.
- ☐ Graded-index (α ≈ 2) minimizes intermodal dispersion.

### D. OTDR
- ☐ **z = ct/(2n₁)** — note the factor of 2 (round-trip).
- ☐ Resolution: **Δz = ct_p/(2n₁)**.
- ☐ Detects **Rayleigh backscatter** (continuous) and **Fresnel reflections** (discrete spikes).
- ☐ Trace slope → attenuation; step → splice loss; spike → reflection.
- ☐ Non-destructive; single-ended; used for **installation and maintenance**.

### E. Numerical Aperture (NA)
- ☐ **NA = sin θₐ = √(n₁² − n₂²) = n₁√(2Δ)**.
- ☐ Far-field scan is the standard method.
- ☐ NA dictates **light-gathering ability, splice loss, and coupling efficiency**.
- ☐ Higher NA → more multimode propagation → higher intermodal dispersion.

### F. Engineering Quick-Reference Numbers
- ☐ **c = 3 × 10⁸ m/s**; n₁(SiO₂) ≈ 1.48; n₂ ≈ 1.46.
- ☐ **V = (2πa/λ) NA**; V < 2.405 for single-mode operation.
- ☐ Standard fiber diameters: core 8–10 µm (SMF), 50/62.5 µm (MMF); cladding 125 µm.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Measurement of Pressure, Temperature, Current, Voltage, Liquid Level and Strain
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEAET413 - OPTICAL INSTRUMENTATION > - **Module:** Module Module 2 > - **Topic:** Measurement of Pressure, Temperature, Current, Voltage, Liquid Level and Strain ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Introduction to Optical Measurement of Non-Optical Quantities Optical instrumentation is not limited to measuring light itself. The **transduction principle** allows optical techniques to measure a vast range of physical, electrical, and chemical quantities by converting the measurand into a change in an optical property — intensity, phase, polarization, wavelength, or coherence. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Core Definition (KTU 2024 Scheme):** *Optical Measurement* refers to a class of non-contact or minimally invasive measurement techniques where a physical quantity (pressure, temperature, strain, etc.) modulates an optical signal (intensity, phase, wavelength, or polarization state), and the modulation is decoded using photodetectors and signal processing to yield a quantitative reading of the original quantity. ### 1.2 The Six Measurands in This Module | Measurand | Primary Optical Mechanism | Typical Industrial Use | |---|---|---| | **Pressure** | Elastic deformation → optical path change / Fabry-Perot cavity shift | Oil wells, aerospace, biomedical | | **Temperature** | Blackbody radiation, fluorescence decay, refractive index change | Furnaces, transformers, hyperthermia | | **Current** | Magneto-optic Faraday rotation in fiber | High-voltage substations, power grids | | **Voltage** | Electro-optic Pockels/Kerr effect in crystals | EHV lines, pulsed power systems | | **Liquid Level** | Total Internal Reflection (TIR), refraction at liquid interface | Fuel tanks, chemical reactors | | **Strain** | Bragg wavelength shift in FBG, interferometric phase shift | Civil, aerospace, smart structures | ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Translator" > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — The Translator at a UN Conference:** > Imagine a diplomat who cannot speak French listening to a French speaker. He cannot hear the message directly, so he watches an interpreter translate the words into hand gestures. The *optical sensor* is like that interpreter: the measurand (pressure, temperature, etc.) "speaks" in its own physical language (force, heat, electric field). The optical sensor translates that invisible language into a visible signal — a change in light — which we can then measure with a photodetector. The detector "hears" the optical translation and gives us a number. ### 1.4 Why Optical Methods? — Key Advantages - **Intrinsic safety** in explosive/flammable environments (no electrical spark at the sensing point) - **Immunity to electromagnetic interference (EMI)** — critical near high-voltage lines - **Long-distance remote sensing** via optical fibers (kilometers) - **High sensitivity** — interferometric systems can detect displacements smaller than **1 nm** - **Multiplexing capability** — many FBG sensors share a single fiber - **Miniaturization** — fiber tips as small as **125 µm** ### 1.5 The General Block Architecture of an Optical Sensor Every optical measurement system has **three universal blocks**: 1. **Optical Source (Emitter):** LED, laser, broadband SLED, or blackbody (for pyrometry) 2. **Transducer / Sensing Head:** where light interacts with the measurand 3. **Photodetector + Signal Processor:** converts returning light into an electrical readout > [!TIP] > Always identify these three blocks whenever you draw or analyze an optical sensor in your KTU exam answers. Examiners award marks specifically for source → transducer → detector clarity. --- ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 Pressure Measurement — Optical Methods #### 2.1.1 Operating Principle A pressure-sensitive diaphragm (silicon, metal, or silica) deflects under differential pressure. The deflection is converted into an optical signal. #### 2.1.2 Fabry–Perot Pressure Sensor (Most Common) - A cavity is formed between the diaphragm and a fixed reference surface. - Pressure → diaphragm deflects → cavity length **L** changes → fringe pattern shifts. - The transmitted intensity is governed by the Airy function. **Airy Transmission Function:** $$T = \frac{1}{1 + F \sin^{2}(\delta/2)}$$ where the phase difference is: $$\delta = \frac{4\pi n L \cos\theta}{\lambda}$$ **Sensitivity (small-deflection regime):** $$\frac{\Delta \lambda}{\lambda} = \frac{\Delta L}{L}$$ #### 2.1.3 Fiber-Optic Pressure Probe - Light from an LED enters a multimode fiber, exits, reflects off a diaphragm, and re-enters the fiber. - Intensity modulates with pressure due to **microbending loss** or **reflectivity change**. --- ### 2.2 Temperature Measurement — Optical Methods #### 2.2.1 Radiation Pyrometry (Planck's Law Based) The spectral radiance of a blackbody at temperature **T** and wavelength **λ** is: $$B_{\lambda}(T) = \frac{2 h c^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{h c / (\lambda k_{B} T)} - 1}$$ where: - **h** = Planck's constant = **6.626 × 10⁻³⁴ J·s** - **k_B** = Boltzmann constant = **1.381 × 10⁻²³ J/K** - **c** = speed of light = **3 × 10⁸ m/s** #### 2.2.2 Fiber-Optic Fluorescent Thermometer A phosphor (e.g., ruby, alexandrite) is excited by a UV/green pulse. Its fluorescence **decay time τ** follows: $$\tau(T) = \frac{\tau_{0}}{1 + A \cdot e^{-E_{a}/k_{B} T}}$$ **Advantages:** immune to fiber attenuation, fiber bending losses, connector drift. #### 2.2.3 Fiber Bragg Grating (FBG) Temperature Sensor Bragg wavelength shifts with temperature via: $$\Delta \lambda_{B} = \lambda_{B}(\alpha + \xi) \Delta T$$ where **α** is thermal expansion coefficient and **ξ** is thermo-optic coefficient. --- ### 2.3 Current Measurement — Magneto-Optic (Faraday) Effect #### 2.3.1 Faraday Rotation Linearly polarized light traveling through a medium in a magnetic field **H** parallel to the propagation direction rotates its plane of polarization: $$\beta = V \int_{0}^{L} \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l}$$ For a uniform field: $$\beta = V \cdot H \cdot L$$ where **V** = Verdet constant of the material (rad/T/m). #### 2.3.2 Ampere's Law Linkage By Ampere's law, the line integral of H around a current-carrying conductor equals the enclosed current: $$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enclosed}$$ Therefore: $$\beta = V \cdot N \cdot I \quad \text{(for N turns of fiber around the conductor)}$$ This is the elegant basis of all-optical current transformers (OCT). --- ### 2.4 Voltage Measurement — Electro-Optic Effect (Pockels Effect) #### 2.4.1 Pockels Effect In a non-centrosymmetric crystal (e.g., **LiNbO₃, KDP, BSO**), the refractive index changes linearly with applied electric field: $$\Delta n = -\tfrac{1}{2} n^{3} r E$$ where **r** is the electro-optic coefficient. The induced phase retardation between two orthogonal polarization components is: $$\Gamma = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} n^{3} r V \cdot \frac{L}{d}$$ where: - **L** = crystal length - **d** = electrode spacing - **V** = applied voltage #### 2.4.2 Half-Wave Voltage $$\Gamma = \pi \quad \Rightarrow \quad V_{\pi} = \frac{\lambda d}{2 n^{3} r L}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Exam Keyword:** *Pockels effect = linear in E* (used for AC and DC voltages). *Kerr effect = quadratic in E* (used for high-voltage pulsed systems). --- ### 2.5 Liquid Level Measurement — Optical Methods #### 2.5.1 Total Internal Reflection (TIR) Sensor A prism or fiber tip is mounted at the level interface. Light undergoes TIR when the external medium is air (n_air < n_prism) and is refracted (lost) when submerged in liquid (n_liquid > n_air). **Critical angle condition:** $$\sin\theta_{c} = \frac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}$$ If **n₂ > n₁**, TIR is impossible — sensor reports "wet" via low return intensity. #### 2.5.2 Refractometric Level Sensor Measured refractive index change at the tip → direct conversion to liquid identity/level. --- ### 2.6 Strain Measurement — FBG and Interferometric #### 2.6.1 Fiber Bragg Grating Strain Sensor The Bragg wavelength is: $$\lambda_{B} = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$$ where **Λ** is the grating period and **n_eff** is the effective index. Strain response: $$\frac{\Delta \lambda_{B}}{\lambda_{B}} = (1 - p_{e}) \epsilon$$ where **p_e** = effective photoelastic coefficient ≈ **0.22** for silica. Combined strain + temperature: $$\frac{\Delta \lambda_{B}}{\lambda_{B}} = (1-p_{e})\epsilon + (\alpha + \xi)\Delta T$$ #### 2.6.2 Interferometric Strain Gauge (Michelson) Displacement ΔL between two arms produces phase: $$\Delta \phi = \frac{4\pi n}{\lambda} \Delta L$$ Resolution: ~ **10⁻⁶ rad → sub-nm displacement**. --- ### 2.7 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > This table is the **single most important revision artifact** before your KTU ESE. Cover each cell during last-mile preparation. | Measurand | Governing Optical Law | Key Equation | Constant / Symbol | |---|---|---|---| | Pressure | Fabry–Perot cavity | $T = \tfrac{1}{1+F\sin^{2}(\delta/2)}$ | F = finesse | | Temperature | Planck's Law | $B_{\lambda}(T)=\tfrac{2hc^{2}}{\lambda^{5}}\tfrac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T}-1}$ | h, k_B | | Current | Faraday Effect | $\beta = V \cdot H \cdot L$ | Verdet constant V | | Voltage | Pockels Effect | $\Gamma = \tfrac{2\pi}{\lambda} n^{3} r \cdot \tfrac{L}{d} \cdot V$ | EO coeff. r | | Liquid Level | TIR / Refraction | $\sin\theta_{c} = \tfrac{n_{2}}{n_{1}}$ | n_1, n_2 | | Strain | FBG / Interferometry | $\tfrac{\Delta\lambda_{B}}{\lambda_{B}} = (1-p_{e})\epsilon$ | p_e ≈ 0.22 | | Fluorescence Temp. | Decay time | $\tau(T)=\tfrac{\tau_{0}}{1+Ae^{-E_{a}/k_B T}}$ | E_a (activation) | --- ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation: Faraday Rotation → Current Measurement We want to derive the relationship between the **rotated polarization angle β** and the **current I** flowing through a conductor encircled by a single-turn optical fiber loop. **Starting Point — Faraday's Law of Magneto-Optics:** $$\beta = V \int_{0}^{L} \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l}$$ **Step 1 — Apply Ampere's Circuital Law:** For any closed path encircling a current-carrying conductor: $$\oint \vec{H} \cdot d\vec{l} = I_{enclosed}$$ **Step 2 — Substitute into Faraday's equation:** $$\beta = V \cdot I_{enclosed}$$ **Step 3 — Generalize to N fiber turns around the conductor:** Each turn encircles the current once. Summing over all turns: $$\beta = V \cdot N \cdot I$$ **Step 4 — Solve for current:** $$\boxed{I = \frac{\beta}{V \cdot N}}$$ **Step 5 — Sensitivity example:** For a silica fiber with **V = 4.5 × 10⁻⁶ rad/A** (at λ = 1310 nm) and **N = 50 turns**, a rotation of **0.01 rad** corresponds to: $$I = \frac{0.01}{4.5 \times 10^{-6} \times 50} \approx 44.4 \text{ A}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation: FBG Strain Sensitivity **Step 1 — Bragg condition:** $$\lambda_{B} = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$$ **Step 2 — Take total differential of both sides:** $$d\lambda_{B} = 2 \Lambda \, dn_{eff} + 2 n_{eff} \, d\Lambda$$ **Step 3 — Divide by λ_B:** $$\frac{d\lambda_{B}}{\lambda_{B}} = \frac{dn_{eff}}{n_{eff}} + \frac{d\Lambda}{\Lambda}$$ **Step 4 — Mechanical strain definition:** $$\epsilon = \frac{d\Lambda}{\Lambda}$$ **Step 5 — Photoelastic relation** (index change due to strain via Pockels effect of silica): $$\frac{dn_{eff}}{n_{eff}} = -p_{e} \cdot \epsilon$$ **Step 6 — Substitute:** $$\frac{d\lambda_{B}}{\lambda_{B}} = \epsilon - p_{e} \epsilon = (1 - p_{e}) \epsilon$$ **Step 7 — For silica at 1550 nm**, **p_e ≈ 0.22**, hence: $$\boxed{\frac{\Delta\lambda_{B}}{\lambda_{B}} \approx 0.78 \epsilon}$$ **Step 8 — Numerical example:** For a strain of **1000 microstrain (µε)** on a 1550 nm grating: $$\Delta\lambda_{B} = 1550 \times 10^{-9} \times 0.78 \times 1000 \times 10^{-6} = 1.209 \text{ nm}$$ This is readily measurable with an optical spectrum analyzer. --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Right Optical Technique > [!IMPORTANT] > The matrix below is a *frequently-asked* analytical question in KTU Module 2. Memorize the justification logic. | Application Scenario | Best Optical Method | Reason | |---|---|---| | High-voltage DC line current sensing | Faraday rotation in single-mode fiber | DC field measurement, no hysteresis | | 1000 °C furnace temperature | Disappearing-filament pyrometer / ratio pyrometer | Non-contact, wide range | | Cryogenic (–200 °C) temperature | Fluorescent decay thermometer | No electrical leads needed | | Submarine hull strain mapping | FBG array on single fiber | Wavelength-division multiplexing | | Fuel tank level in aircraft | TIR fiber tip | Intrinsically safe, no spark | | Pulsed high-voltage (MV) measurement | Kerr effect in nitrobenzene | Quadratic response handles large fields | | Differential pressure in oil well | Fabry–Perot diaphragm | High sensitivity, fiber-fed | --- ### 3.4 Worked Example: Pockels Voltage Sensor **Problem:** A LiNbO₃ crystal (n = 2.2, r = 30.8 pm/V) of length 10 mm and electrode gap 5 mm is used with light at λ = 633 nm. Find the half-wave voltage. **Step 1 — Phase retardation formula:** $$\Gamma = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} n^{3} r \cdot \frac{L}{d} \cdot V$$ **Step 2 — Substitute values:** - λ = 633 × 10⁻⁹ m - n³ = (2.2)³ = 10.648 - r = 30.8 × 10⁻¹² m/V - L = 10 × 10⁻³ m - d = 5 × 10⁻³ m $$\Gamma = \frac{2\pi (10.648)(30.8 \times 10^{-12})(10 \times 10^{-3})}{633 \times 10^{-9} \times 5 \times 10^{-3}} \cdot V$$ **Step 3 — Compute the constant term:** Numerator: $$2\pi \times 10.648 \times 30.8 \times 10^{-12} \times 10 \times 10^{-3} = 2.060 \times 10^{-11}$$ Denominator: $$633 \times 10^{-9} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 3.165 \times 10^{-9}$$ **Step 4 — Simplify:** $$\frac{\Gamma}{V} = \frac{2.060 \times 10^{-11}}{3.165 \times 10^{-9}} = 6.51 \times 10^{-3} \text{ rad/V}$$ **Step 5 — Half-wave condition: Γ = π:** $$V_{\pi} = \frac{\pi}{6.51 \times 10^{-3}} \approx 482.6 \text{ V}$$ > [!TIP] > Half-wave voltages in the 200–500 V range make LiNbO₃ excellent for medium-voltage instrumentation. Examiner loves this numerical — practice it. --- ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Universal Optical Sensor Functional Architecture ```mermaid flowchart LR srcNode[OPTICAL SOURCE
LED Laser SLED Blackbody] -->|light injected| trnNode[TRANSDUCER HEAD
interacts with measurand] trnNode -->|modulated light| detNode[PHOTODETECTOR
PD APD CCD] detNode -->|electrical signal| ampNode[SIGNAL PROCESSOR
amplifier filter DSP] ampNode -->|digital readout| outNode[DISPLAY
numeric graph chart] trnNode -.->|feedback| measNode[MEASURAND
P T I V level strain] measNode -.->|modulates| trnNode ``` ### 4.2 Fabry-Perot Pressure Sensor Architecture ```mermaid flowchart TB fiberIn[INPUT FIBER] --> couplerA[FIBER COUPLER] couplerA -->|guided to cavity| diaphragm[PRESSURE SENSITIVE
SILICON DIAPHRAGM] diaphragm -->|reflects with cavity phase| cavity[FABRY PEROT CAVITY
length L varies with P] cavity -->|returned spectrum| couplerA couplerA -->|decoded output| detector[SPECTRUM ANALYZER
or photodiode] pressureIn[APPLIED PRESSURE P] -->|mechanical deflection| diaphragm ``` ### 4.3 Fiber-Optic Current Sensor (Farctomery) ```mermaid flowchart LR laserSrc[STABILIZED LASER
1310 or 1550 nm] --> polarizer[INPUT POLARIZER
45 deg linear] polarizer --> coupler1[PORT 1 OF CIRCULATOR] coupler1 --> sensingLoop[FIBER LOOP
N turns around conductor] conductor[CONDUCTOR CARRYING
CURRENT I] -->|magnetic field H| sensingLoop sensingLoop -->|rotated polarization beta| coupler1 coupler1 -->|via port 2| analyzer[ANALYZER
at 45 deg] analyzer --> detectors[DUAL PHOTODIODE BRIDGE] detectors -->|ratio signal| processor[SIGNAL PROCESSOR] processor -->|value of I| displayOut[CURRENT READOUT] ``` ### 4.4 Pockels-Effect Voltage Sensor ```mermaid flowchart TB laser[LASER SOURCE] --> bs1[POLARIZING BEAMSPLITTER] bs1 -->|vertical| eoCrystal[ELECTRO OPTIC CRYSTAL
LiNbO3 or BSO] eoCrystal --> wp[QUARTER WAVE PLATE] wp --> mirror[RETROREFLECTING MIRROR] mirror -->|double pass| wp wp --> eoCrystal eoCrystal --> bs1 bs1 -->|horizontal component| anlz[ANALYZER] anlz --> pd[PHOTODIODE] pd --> amp[TRANSIMPEDANCE AMP] amp -->|voltage proportional| vout[VOLTAGE OUTPUT] hv[HIGH VOLTAGE LINE] -->|field E| eoCrystal ``` ### 4.5 FBG-Based Strain and Temperature Sensor Array ```mermaid flowchart LR bbSrc[BROADBAND SLED SOURCE] --> cir[CIRCULATOR] cir --> fbg1[FBG1 Lambda B1] cir --> fbg2[FBG2 Lambda B2] cir --> fbg3[FBG3 Lambda B3] cir --> fbgN[FBGN Lambda BN] fbg1 --> cir fbg2 --> cir fbg3 --> cir fbgN --> cir cir --> osa[OPTICAL SPECTRUM ANALYZER] osa --> proc[WAVELENGTH PEAK TRACKING DSP] proc -->|individual strain and temp| readout[DISPLAY ARRAY OF FBGS] struct[STRUCTURE UNDER TEST] -->|strain and temp applied| fbg1 struct --> fbg2 struct --> fbg3 struct --> fbgN ``` ### 4.6 TIR Liquid Level Sensor Functional Flow ```mermaid flowchart LR srcA[LED SOURCE] --> fiberArm[INPUT FIBER] fiberArm --> tip[POLISHED FIBER TIP
at angle theta] tip -->|air present TIR| returnA[RETURN FIBER] tip -->|liquid present refraction lost| returnA returnA --> detPD[PHOTODIODE] detPD -->|high intensity DRY| logicL[COMPARATOR] detPD -->|low intensity WET| logicL logicL -->|level threshold| relayOut[RELAY OUTPUT] ``` --- ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) #### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **State Faraday's magneto-optic effect. Mention two advantages of fiber-optic current sensors over conventional CTs.** **Model Answer:** *Faraday's magneto-optic effect states that the plane of polarization of linearly polarized light rotates when it propagates through a medium in the presence of a magnetic field parallel to the direction of propagation. The rotation angle is* $\beta = V \int \vec{H}\cdot d\vec{l}$ *where V is the Verdet constant.* *Advantages over conventional current transformers:* 1. *Intrinsic insulation — no copper secondary winding at high voltage.* 2. *Wide dynamic range and immunity to electromagnetic interference.* 3. *No saturation in ferromagnetic cores.* --- #### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – June 2024]** **What is the Pockels effect? Write the expression for the half-wave voltage of an electro-optic modulator.** **Model Answer:** *The Pockels effect is the linear change in the refractive index of a non-centrosymmetric crystal when an external electric field is applied:* $$\Delta n = -\tfrac{1}{2} n^{3} r E$$ *Half-wave voltage* — the voltage required to introduce a phase retardation of π between the two orthogonal polarization components: $$V_{\pi} = \frac{\lambda d}{2 n^{3} r L}$$ --- ### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** > 1. *Students frequently write the formula but forget to define each symbol.* Always define **n, r, L, d, λ, V, I, β** explicitly. Marks are deducted for undefined symbols. > 2. *Confusing Pockels (linear) with Kerr (quadratic) effect* — a classic 2-mark deduction. State "linear in E" vs "quadratic in E" explicitly. > 3. *In FBG problems, students forget the photoelastic coefficient p_e* and write only $\Delta\lambda/\lambda = \epsilon$. The correct form is $(1-p_e)\epsilon$. --- #### QUESTION A — Option 1 **[14 Marks]** ##### (a) [7 Marks] Explain the working of a fiber Bragg grating (FBG) sensor for measurement of strain. Derive the strain sensitivity expression. **Model Answer:** **Working Principle:** An FBG is a periodic modulation of the refractive index in the core of a photosensitive optical fiber. It acts as a wavelength-selective mirror, reflecting the Bragg wavelength $\lambda_B = 2 n_{eff} \Lambda$ and transmitting all other wavelengths. When the fiber is strained, two effects occur: 1. **Geometric change:** grating period Λ changes due to mechanical stretching. 2. **Photoelastic effect:** the effective index n_eff changes due to strain-induced birefringence in silica. **Derivation:** Taking the differential of the Bragg condition: $$d\lambda_{B} = 2\Lambda\, dn_{eff} + 2 n_{eff}\, d\Lambda$$ Dividing by $\lambda_B$: $$\frac{d\lambda_{B}}{\lambda_{B}} = \frac{dn_{eff}}{n_{eff}} + \frac{d\Lambda}{\Lambda}$$ Using $\epsilon = d\Lambda/\Lambda$ and $dn_{eff}/n_{eff} = -p_e \epsilon$: $$\boxed{\frac{\Delta\lambda_{B}}{\lambda_{B}} = (1 - p_{e})\epsilon}$$ For silica at 1550 nm, $p_e \approx 0.22$, giving sensitivity ≈ **0.78 pm/µε** per nm of Bragg wavelength. **Advantages of FBG strain sensors:** - Multiplexing on a single fiber (many FBGs at different λ_B). - Immune to EMI. - Absolute measurement (wavelength encoded). --- ##### (b) [7 Marks] With a block diagram, explain the working of a fiber-optic current sensor based on the Faraday effect. Derive the relation between current and rotation angle. **Model Answer:** **Block Diagram:** (Refer to SECTION 4.3 diagram) **Components:** - Laser source → polarizer → sensing fiber loop wrapped N times around the conductor → analyzer → dual photodetector → processor. **Working:** 1. Linearly polarized light enters the fiber. 2. Magnetic field **H** generated by current **I** rotates the polarization by: $$\beta = V H L$$ 3. By Ampere's circuital law: $$\oint \vec{H}\cdot d\vec{l} = I$$ 4. For N turns: $$\beta = V \cdot N \cdot I$$ 5. Analyzer + dual photodiode converts rotation into an electrical ratio signal. **Final Result:** $$\boxed{I = \frac{\beta}{V N}}$$ **Why fiber is preferred:** Standard silica fiber has well-characterized Verdet constant, is non-conductive (safe at HV), and the loop geometry provides excellent integration of H around the conductor. --- #### QUESTION B — Option 2 **[14 Marks]** ##### (a) [7 Marks] Describe the optical method of measuring temperature using a blackbody / radiation pyrometer. State Planck's law and explain the working of a two-color (ratio) pyrometer. **Model Answer:** **Planck's Law:** A blackbody at absolute temperature T emits spectral radiance: $$B_{\lambda}(T) = \frac{2hc^{2}}{\lambda^{5}} \cdot \frac{1}{e^{hc/\lambda k_B T} - 1}$$ As T increases, $B_{\lambda}(T)$ rises rapidly, and the peak wavelength shifts (Wien's displacement: $\lambda_{peak} \cdot T = 2.898 \times 10^{-3}$ m·K). **Single-Wavelength Pyrometer:** A narrow-band filter isolates a single wavelength. The detector measures intensity, which is compared with a reference internal lamp to determine T. **Two-Color (Ratio) Pyrometer:** Intensity is measured at two wavelengths λ₁ and λ₂. The ratio is: $$R = \frac{B_{\lambda_1}(T)}{B_{\lambda_2}(T)}$$ **Advantage:** The ratio is **independent of emissivity, smoke, dust, and partial obstructions**, making it ideal for harsh industrial environments (furnaces, molten metals). --- ##### (b) [7 Marks] Explain the principle of liquid-level measurement using total internal reflection. Derive the condition for TIR. **Model Answer:** **Principle:** A fiber tip or prism is positioned such that the light strikes the boundary between the prism (n₁) and the external medium (n₂) at an angle θ. **Snell's Law:** $$n_1 \sin\theta = n_2 \sin 90° = n_2$$ Critical angle: $$\sin\theta_c = \frac{n_2}{n_1}$$ **Total Internal Reflection occurs when:** $$\theta > \theta_c \quad \text{AND} \quad n_1 > n_2$$ **Application to Level Sensing:** - **Air present (n₂ ≈ 1.0):** n₁ > n₂ → TIR occurs → strong return signal at detector → output = "DRY". - **Liquid present (n₂ > 1.33):** n₁ may not exceed n₂ at the chosen θ → light escapes by refraction → weak return → output = "WET". **Design Choice:** Use a glass prism with n₁ ≈ 1.5 and operate near θ_c ≈ 42° for water detection. **Advantage:** Intrinsically safe — no electrical conduction at the sensing tip, suitable for flammable liquids (petrol, alcohol, LNG). --- ### 5.3 Topic Recap & Rapid Revision Checklist > [!IMPORTANT] > **Last-Mile Revision — Read This Twice Before ESE.** - ☐ **Pressure** → Fabry–Perot cavity length change → Airy function shift. - ☐ **Temperature** → Planck's law governs radiation pyrometry; fluorescence decay is alternative. - ☐ **Current** → Faraday rotation $\beta = VNI$; fiber loop around conductor. - ☐ **Voltage** → Pockels effect (linear in E) for AC/DC; Kerr effect (quadratic) for pulsed HV. - ☐ **Liquid Level** → TIR sensor; critical angle $\sin\theta_c = n_2/n_1$. - ☐ **Strain** → FBG Bragg wavelength shift $(1-p_e)\epsilon$; photoelastic coefficient 0.22. - ☐ **Fluorescence decay** formula: $\tau(T) = \tau_0/(1 + A e^{-E_a/k_B T})$. - ☐ **Half-wave voltage** for Pockels: $V_\pi = \lambda d/(2n^3 r L)$. - ☐ **Key constants:** h, k_B, c; Verdet constant of silica; p_e of silica; n of LiNbO₃ = 2.2. - ☐ **Universal sensor block:** Source → Transducer → Detector → Processor → Display. - ☐ **Advantage of optical sensors:** EMI immunity, intrinsic safety, multiplexing, remote sensing. - ☐ **Examiner trap:** Pockels ≠ Kerr; linear ≠ quadratic in E. - ☐ **Examiner trap:** FBG needs $(1-p_e)$ not just $\epsilon$. - ☐ **Examiner trap:** Always define symbols in formulas before substituting. ---

topics.title: Optical Modulators: Different Types of Modulators - Electro-Optic Modulators, Magneto-Optic Modulators and Acousto-Optic Modulators
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET413 - OPTICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Optical Modulators: Different Types of Modulators - Electro-Optic Modulators, Magneto-Optic Modulators and Acousto-Optic Modulators

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is an Optical Modulator?

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Standard):**
> An **Optical Modulator** is a device that is used to manipulate one or more properties of a light beam (such as amplitude, phase, polarization, frequency, or direction of propagation) in accordance with an external control signal (electrical, magnetic, or acoustic). Modulation converts information from a low-frequency electrical domain onto a high-frequency optical carrier.

The three principal types of modulators discussed in this module are:

| Sl. No. | Modulator Type | Controlling Signal | Physical Effect Exploited |
|:---:|:---|:---|:---|
| 1 | **Electro-Optic Modulator (EOM)** | Electric field/voltage | **Pockels Effect** (linear EO) / **Kerr Effect** (quadratic EO) |
| 2 | **Magneto-Optic Modulator (MOM)** | Magnetic field | **Faraday Effect** / **Cotton-Mouton Effect** |
| 3 | **Acousto-Optic Modulator (AOM)** | Acoustic (sound) waves | **Acousto-Optic (Photoelastic) Effect** |

> [!NOTE]
> In all three cases, the *external* control signal alters the **refractive index** of the medium. Since light propagation, polarization, and diffraction depend on the refractive index, modulating the index allows us to modulate the light itself.

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition

Imagine you are standing at the edge of a swimming pool holding a flashlight.

1. **Electro-Optic Modulator (EOM) — "The Stretchy Window"**
   Think of a special glass window whose *optical thickness* changes the instant you apply a voltage across it. Without voltage, light passes through normally. Apply +5 V, and the window becomes "optically fatter" for one polarization; apply −5 V, and it becomes "optically thinner." The voltage is the *steering wheel* controlling the light.

2. **Magneto-Optic Modulator (MOM) — "The Twisting Magnet"**
   Picture polarized sunglasses. Now imagine wrapping a coil around the glass and passing current through it. The magnetic field produced *twists* the plane of polarization of light passing through the glass. The stronger the current, the more the polarization rotates. This is exactly how a Faraday rotator works.

3. **Acousto-Optic Modulator (AOM) — "The Ripple Tank"**
   Drop a pebble in a pond — you see ripples (compressions and rarefactions). Light hitting these ripples behaves like light hitting a moving diffraction grating. By switching the sound on and off (or varying its frequency), you can *deflect* or *chop* the light beam at will.

> [!TIP]
> **Memory Hook for Exams:** **E**lectro-Optic → **E**lectric field changes index; **M**agneto-Optic → **M**agnetic field rotates polarization; **A**cousto-Optic → **A**coustic waves act as gratings.

---

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants & Materials

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following standard materials and constants are *high-yield* for KTU examinations:

| Material | Relevant Effect | Typical Constant (at 633 nm) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Lithium Niobate (LiNbO₃)** | Pockels (EO) | $r_{33} \approx 30.8 \text{ pm/V}$ |
| **KD*P (Potassium Dihydrogen Phosphate)** | Pockels (EO) | $r_{41} \approx 23 \text{ pm/V}$ |
| **Barium Titanate (BaTiO₃)** | Kerr (EO) | $R \approx 10^{-13} \text{ m/V}^2$ |
| **Lead Glass (SF-6)** | Faraday (MO) | $V \approx 0.030 \text{ rad/(T·m)}$ |
| **Terbium Gallium Garnet (TGG)** | Faraday (MO) | $V \approx 1.34 \text{ rad/(T·m)}$ |
| **Tellurium Dioxide (TeO₂)** | Acousto-Optic | $n \approx 2.26$, slow-shear mode used |
| **Fused Quartz (SiO₂)** | Acousto-Optic | $n \approx 1.46$, $p_{12} \approx 0.27$ |

The symbols used above:
- $r$ → **Electro-optic coefficient** (Pockels)
- $R$ → **Kerr coefficient**
- $V$ → **Verdet constant**
- $p$ → **Photoelastic (strain-optic) coefficient**
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Electro-Optic Modulators (EOM)

### 2.1.1 The Pockels Effect (Linear Electro-Optic Effect)

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Statement:**
> The Pockels Effect describes a *linear* change in the refractive index of a non-centrosymmetric crystal (i.e., one lacking inversion symmetry) in proportion to an applied electric field $E$.

The change in the refractive index is:

$$\Delta n \propto E$$

For a crystal with principal refractive index $n_0$, the index changes to:

$$n = n_0 \pm \frac{1}{2}\, n_0^{\,3}\, r\, E$$

where $r$ is the **Pockels (electro-optic) coefficient** (units: m/V).

**Why does it happen?** The applied electric field distorts the electron cloud of the crystal lattice, slightly shifting the charge distribution. This changes the *polarizability* of the medium, which (via the Lorentz–Lorenz relation) changes the *refractive index*. Because the distortion is linear in $E$ for non-centrosymmetric media, $\Delta n$ is also linear in $E$.

**Engineering utility:** Used in high-speed fiber-optic communication (10–100 GHz), laser Q-switching, optical switching, and Pockels cells for pulse picking.

### 2.1.2 The Kerr Effect (Quadratic Electro-Optic Effect)

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Statement:**
> The Kerr Effect is a *quadratic* change in refractive index with the applied electric field. Unlike the Pockels effect, it occurs in *all* materials (centrosymmetric and non-centrosymmetric).

$$\Delta n \propto E^2$$

Mathematically:

$$\Delta n = \lambda\, K\, E^2$$

where $K$ is the **Kerr constant** (units: m/V²) and $\lambda$ is the wavelength. Most materials have an extremely small $K$, so Kerr cells typically require very high voltages (kV range) or very short interaction lengths.

### 2.1.3 The Electro-Optic Retarder / Pockels Cell

When an input linearly polarized light of wavelength $\lambda$ is sent through a Pockels crystal of length $L$ with electric field $E = V/d$ (where $V$ is the applied voltage and $d$ is the electrode separation), the **phase retardation** $\Gamma$ between the fast and slow axes is:

$$\Gamma = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\, n_0^{\,3}\, r\, V\, \frac{L}{d}$$

The **half-wave voltage** $V_{\lambda/2}$ is the voltage required to produce $\Gamma = \pi$ (i.e., 180° phase shift):

$$V_{\lambda/2} = \frac{\lambda\, d}{2\, n_0^{\,3}\, r\, L}$$

For a **longitudinal configuration** ($L = d$):

$$V_{\lambda/2} = \frac{\lambda}{2\, n_0^{\,3}\, r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A common mistake is forgetting whether $L$ appears in the denominator (longitudinal) or not (transverse). In **longitudinal** geometry, the light travels *parallel* to the applied field, so $L = d$ and the half-wave voltage is *independent* of crystal length. In **transverse** geometry, the light travels *perpendicular* to the field, so a longer crystal gives a *lower* $V_{\lambda/2}$.

### 2.1.4 Polarization Modulation vs. Amplitude Modulation

By placing a Pockels cell between **crossed polarizers**, the transmitted intensity follows the **Malus-law-like** response:

$$I_{\text{out}} = I_{\text{in}}\, \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Gamma}{2}\right) = I_{\text{in}}\, \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\pi V}{2 V_{\lambda/2}}\right)$$

This converts the *phase* modulation (Pockels) into *amplitude* modulation (light → dark → light) when voltage is varied.

---

## 2.2 Magneto-Optic Modulators (MOM)

### 2.2.1 The Faraday Effect

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Statement:**
> The Faraday Effect is the *rotation of the plane of polarization* of linearly polarized light as it propagates through a transparent medium in the presence of a magnetic field parallel to the direction of propagation.

The angle of rotation $\theta$ is:

$$\theta = V\, B\, L$$

where:
- $V$ = **Verdet constant** (rad·T⁻¹·m⁻¹)
- $B$ = magnetic flux density (T)
- $L$ = path length through the medium (m)

**Key difference from natural optical activity:** In the Faraday effect, the rotation is *non-reciprocal* — if light travels in the opposite direction, the rotation occurs in the *same* (absolute) sense, not the opposite sense. This makes Faraday rotators ideal as **optical isolators**.

### 2.2.2 The Cotton–Mouton Effect (Magnetic Birefringence)

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Statement:**
> The Cotton–Mouton Effect is a *magnetic birefringence* that occurs when a magnetic field is applied *perpendicular* to the light propagation direction. The medium becomes birefringent, with refractive indices for polarizations parallel and perpendicular to the field differing by:

$$\Delta n = C\, B^2\, \lambda$$

where $C$ is the **Cotton–Mouton constant**.

### 2.2.3 Magneto-Optic Modulator Configuration

A typical MOM consists of:
1. A polarizer (input)
2. A Faraday-active medium inside a solenoid (the magnetic field source)
3. An analyzer (output, often crossed with the polarizer at $B = 0$)

The output intensity follows:

$$I_{\text{out}} = I_{\text{in}}\, \cos^2(\theta) = I_{\text{in}}\, \cos^2(V B L)$$

By modulating the solenoid current (and hence $B$), the polarization rotation angle is modulated, and the analyzer converts this to **intensity modulation**.

---

## 2.3 Acousto-Optic Modulators (AOM)

### 2.3.1 The Photoelastic / Acousto-Optic Effect

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Statement:**
> The Acousto-Optic Effect (also called the photoelastic effect in this context) describes the periodic variation of the refractive index of a medium caused by an acoustic wave. Compressions and rarefactions of the sound wave alternately raise and lower the local density (and hence the index), creating a *phase grating* that diffracts light.

The acoustic wavelength $\Lambda$ and the acoustic frequency $f_a$ are related by:

$$\Lambda = \frac{v_a}{f_a}$$

where $v_a$ is the velocity of the acoustic wave in the medium (typically 1–6 km/s for solids).

### 2.3.2 The Quality Factor $Q$ (Bragg vs. Raman–Nath)

The dimensionless **Klein–Cook parameter** $Q$ determines the regime of diffraction:

$$Q = \frac{2\pi\, \lambda\, L}{n\, \Lambda^{\,2}}$$

| Regime | Condition | Behavior |
|:---|:---|:---|
| **Raman–Nath (thin grating)** | $Q \ll 1$ | Multiple diffraction orders (like an optical grating) |
| **Bragg (thick grating)** | $Q \gg 1$ | Only two orders (0th and 1st) — efficient |

> [!TIP]
> **Exam Tip:** AOMs used as *modulators* almost always operate in the **Bragg regime** for high efficiency (> 70%).

### 2.3.3 Bragg Diffraction Condition

For an acoustic wave of wavelength $\Lambda$ acting as a thick phase grating, the **Bragg condition** for first-order diffraction is:

$$\sin \theta_B = \frac{\lambda\, f_a}{2\, n\, v_a}$$

where $\theta_B$ is the Bragg angle (measured from the grating planes). Equivalently:

$$2\, \Lambda\, \sin \theta_B = \frac{\lambda}{n}$$

**Engineering utility:** AOMs are used in laser printers, Q-switching of lasers, frequency shifting (heterodyne detection), and as beam deflectors in display systems.

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for Part A (3-mark) and Part B (14-mark) questions. NEVER use `|` in the answer — always write `modulus` or use $\vert \cdot \vert$.**

| Phenomenon | Key Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| Pockels Effect (linear EO) | $\Delta n = \tfrac{1}{2}\, n_0^{\,3}\, r\, E$ | $r$ = Pockels coefficient | m/V |
| Kerr Effect (quadratic EO) | $\Delta n = \lambda\, K\, E^2$ | $K$ = Kerr constant | m/V² |
| Phase retardation (Pockels cell) | $\Gamma = \tfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}\, n_0^{\,3}\, r\, V\, \tfrac{L}{d}$ | — | rad |
| Half-wave voltage (longitudinal) | $V_{\lambda/2} = \tfrac{\lambda}{2\, n_0^{\,3}\, r}$ | — | V |
| Half-wave voltage (transverse) | $V_{\lambda/2} = \tfrac{\lambda\, d}{2\, n_0^{\,3}\, r\, L}$ | — | V |
| Pockels output (crossed polarizers) | $I_{\text{out}} = I_{\text{in}}\, \sin^2\!\big(\tfrac{\pi V}{2 V_{\lambda/2}}\big)$ | — | W/m² |
| Faraday rotation | $\theta = V\, B\, L$ | $V$ = Verdet constant | rad |
| Cotton–Mouton birefringence | $\Delta n = C\, B^2\, \lambda$ | $C$ = Cotton–Mouton constant | m²/A² |
| MOM output intensity | $I_{\text{out}} = I_{\text{in}}\, \cos^2(V B L)$ | — | W/m² |
| Acoustic wavelength | $\Lambda = v_a / f_a$ | $v_a$ = acoustic velocity | m |
| Klein–Cook parameter | $Q = 2\pi \lambda L / (n \Lambda^2)$ | — | dimensionless |
| Bragg condition | $\sin \theta_B = \lambda f_a / (2 n v_a)$ | — | rad |
| Bragg efficiency (peak) | $\eta = \sin^2\!\big(\tfrac{\pi}{2} \sqrt{M_2 P_a L / (2 H)}\big)$ | $M_2$ = figure of merit | dimensionless |

> [!NOTE]
> The figure of merit $M_2$ for an AOM material is given by:
> $$M_2 = \frac{n^{\,6}\, p^{\,2}}{\rho\, v_a^{\,3}}$$
> where $p$ is the photoelastic coefficient, $\rho$ is the density. Materials with high $M_2$ (e.g., TeO₂, Ge, fused quartz) make efficient AOMs.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Half-Wave Voltage for a Longitudinal Pockels Cell

**Goal:** Show that the half-wave voltage in the *longitudinal* configuration is independent of the crystal length.

**Step 1 — Set up the geometry.**
In the longitudinal Pockels cell, light propagates along the optical axis (say, the $z$-axis), and the electric field $E$ is applied along the *same* axis using transparent ring electrodes. The crystal length $L$ and the inter-electrode spacing $d$ are both measured along $z$. Hence:

$$L = d$$

**Step 2 — Express the applied electric field.**

$$E = \frac{V}{d}$$

**Step 3 — Write the index change for the Pockels effect.**
For a uniaxial crystal like LiNbO₃ or KD*P, the extraordinary index changes by:

$$\Delta n = \frac{1}{2}\, n_0^{\,3}\, r\, E$$

**Step 4 — Compute the optical path difference (OPD) between the two induced principal axes.**
For the two orthogonal polarizations (ordinary and extraordinary), the OPD after traversing length $L$ is:

$$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\, \Delta n\, L = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\, \left(\frac{1}{2}\, n_0^{\,3}\, r\, E\right) L$$

**Step 5 — Substitute $E = V/d$ and $L = d$.**

$$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\, \left(\frac{1}{2}\, n_0^{\,3}\, r\, \frac{V}{d}\right) d = \frac{\pi\, n_0^{\,3}\, r\, V}{\lambda}$$

Notice that $d$ has cancelled — confirming the longitudinal $V_{\lambda/2}$ is geometry-independent.

**Step 6 — Apply the half-wave condition $\Delta \phi = \pi$.**

$$\pi = \frac{\pi\, n_0^{\,3}\, r\, V_{\lambda/2}}{\lambda}$$

**Step 7 — Solve for $V_{\lambda/2}$.**

$$V_{\lambda/2} = \frac{\lambda}{n_0^{\,3}\, r}$$

Since we defined $V_{\lambda/2}$ as the voltage that produces $\Gamma = \pi$ (i.e., a half-wave retardation), the *exact* formula is:

$$\boxed{V_{\lambda/2} = \frac{\lambda}{2\, n_0^{\,3}\, r}}$$

(The factor 2 appears depending on the convention of $r$ — whether $r$ is the "full" coefficient or the "half" coefficient. The KTU standard convention places the factor 2 in the formula as shown.)

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Faraday Rotation in a Magneto-Optic Medium

**Goal:** Show that the polarization rotation is linear in magnetic field and path length.

**Step 1 — Lorentz force on bound electrons.**
When a magnetic flux density $B$ is applied along the light propagation direction ($z$), bound electrons in the medium experience the Lorentz force $\vec{F} = -e(\vec{v} \times \vec{B})$ in addition to the restoring force from the binding potential.

**Step 2 — Resonant frequencies split.**
The equation of motion for an electron of natural frequency $\omega_0$ becomes (in the circular-polarization basis):

$$\omega_{\pm} = \omega_0 \pm \frac{e B}{2 m_e}$$

This is the **Zeeman-like splitting** of the resonant frequency for left- and right-circularly polarized light (LCP and RCP).

**Step 3 — Refractive indices split.**
The refractive index near a resonance is dispersion-shaped. For small splitting $\Delta \omega = eB/m_e$, the index difference between LCP ($n_+$) and RCP ($n_-$) is:

$$n_+ - n_- = \frac{e B}{m_e}\, \frac{\partial n}{\partial \omega}$$

**Step 4 — Phase accumulation difference.**
After traversing a length $L$:

$$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\, (n_+ - n_-)\, L = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\, \frac{e B L}{m_e}\, \frac{\partial n}{\partial \omega}$$

**Step 5 — Identify the Verdet constant.**

$$V = \frac{e\, \pi}{\lambda\, m_e}\, \frac{\partial n}{\partial \omega}$$

So the rotation angle (which is half the phase difference between the two circular components) is:

$$\theta = \frac{\Delta \phi}{2} = V\, B\, L$$

Hence the **Faraday rotation** law is derived:

$$\boxed{\theta = V\, B\, L}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key observation:** $V$ depends on $\lambda$. For a normal-dispersion medium, $\partial n / \partial \omega > 0$, so $V > 0$ — the rotation direction follows the *Ampère's rule* for the current producing the field.

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Bragg Diffraction Condition in an AOM

**Goal:** Derive the Bragg condition for light scattering from a moving acoustic grating.

**Step 1 — Treat the acoustic wave as a moving phase grating.**
The acoustic wave produces a periodic variation in refractive index:

$$n(z, t) = n_0 + \Delta n\, \cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi z}{\Lambda} - 2\pi f_a t\right)$$

The grating "moves" with the acoustic velocity $v_a = f_a \Lambda$.

**Step 2 — Apply the Doppler-shifted momentum/energy conservation.**
The acoustic phonon carries momentum $\hbar \vec{q}_a$ and energy $\hbar \omega_a$. Light scattering from this phonon obeys:

$$\vec{k}_{\text{out}} = \vec{k}_{\text{in}} \pm \vec{q}_a$$

$$\omega_{\text{out}} = \omega_{\text{in}} \pm \omega_a$$

**Step 3 — Geometry: equal-angle Bragg scattering.**
For maximum diffraction efficiency, the scattered and incident wavevectors make equal angles $\theta_B$ with the acoustic wavefronts. The angle between $\vec{k}_{\text{in}}$ and the wavefront normal is $\theta_B$. From the vector diagram:

$$2\, k\, \sin \theta_B = q_a$$

**Step 4 — Substitute magnitudes.**
$k = 2\pi n / \lambda$ and $q_a = 2\pi / \Lambda$:

$$2\, \left(\frac{2\pi n}{\lambda}\right) \sin \theta_B = \frac{2\pi}{\Lambda}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $\sin \theta_B$.**

$$\sin \theta_B = \frac{\lambda}{2\, n\, \Lambda} = \frac{\lambda\, f_a}{2\, n\, v_a}$$

Hence the **Bragg condition**:

$$\boxed{\sin \theta_B = \frac{\lambda\, f_a}{2\, n\, v_a}}$$

**Step 6 — Frequency shift of the diffracted beam.**
From the energy conservation, the +1 order is **up-shifted** in frequency by $f_a$:

$$f_{\text{out}} = f_{\text{in}} + f_a$$

This Doppler-like shift is exploited in **heterodyne detection** and **laser frequency shifting**.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of the Three Modulators

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-yield comparison table for KTU 14-mark questions:**

| Parameter | Electro-Optic (EOM) | Magneto-Optic (MOM) | Acousto-Optic (AOM) |
|:---|:---|:---|:---|
| **Control signal** | Voltage (V) | Magnetic field (B) or current | RF acoustic power & frequency |
| **Effect on light** | Phase / polarization / intensity | Polarization rotation | Direction (deflection) / frequency |
| **Key material** | LiNbO₃, KD*P, BaTiO₃ | TGG, SF-6 glass | TeO₂, fused quartz, Ge |
| **Modulation speed** | Very high (GHz–THz) | Moderate (kHz–MHz, coil limited) | High (ns switching) |
| **Insertion loss** | Low | Low | Moderate (diffraction efficiency < 100%) |
| **Reciprocity** | Reciprocal (Pockels) | **Non-reciprocal** (Faraday) | Reciprocal (Bragg) |
| **Typical application** | Telecom modulators, Q-switching | Optical isolators, current sensors | Laser Q-switching, beam steering, frequency shifting |
| **Main disadvantage** | High drive voltage | Coil inductance limits speed | Limited diffraction efficiency, frequency-dependent deflection |
| **KTU-favored formula** | $V_{\lambda/2} = \lambda d / (2 n_0^3 r L)$ | $\theta = V B L$ | $\sin \theta_B = \lambda f_a / (2 n v_a)$ |

    sigA[Control Signal Source<br>Voltage or Current or RF] -.-> modA
    modA --> anaA[Output Polarizer P2]

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The laser is linearly polarized by P1, then passes through the modulation medium whose optical properties are altered by the control signal. The output polarizer P2 (or analyzer) converts the phase/polarization change into an intensity change, which the photodetector measures. This is the canonical "polarizer–modulator–analyzer" (PMA) architecture.

> [!TIP]
> **Operation logic:** When $V = 0$, P1 and P2 are crossed → output is dark. When $V = V_{\lambda/2}$, the polarization is rotated 90° → output is maximum. Intermediate voltages give intermediate intensities. This is the *standard amplitude modulation* setup.

## 4.4 Functional Architecture: Acousto-Optic Modulator in Bragg Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    trA -.-> aoMed
    aoMed --> zerA[Zero Order Beam<br>undiffracted]

```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The RF signal drives a piezoelectric transducer, which launches an acoustic wave into the medium. The acoustic wave acts as a moving phase grating. Light incident at the Bragg angle $\theta_B$ is partially deflected into the +1 order (with frequency shift $+f_a$). The acoustic absorber prevents standing-wave formation.
