# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**Define the Pockels Effect. Why does it occur only in non-centrosymmetric crystals?**

**Model Answer:**
The Pockels Effect is a linear electro-optic effect in which the refractive index of a crystal changes *linearly* with the applied electric field, i.e., $\Delta n \propto E$. It is described by:

$$\Delta n = \frac{1}{2}\, n_0^{\,3}\, r\, E$$

It occurs only in non-centrosymmetric crystals (those lacking inversion symmetry, such as LiNbO₃, KD*P, LiTaO₃) because the linear term in the Taylor expansion of polarization $P(E) = \varepsilon_0 \chi^{(1)} E + \varepsilon_0 \chi^{(2)} E^2 + \cdots$ vanishes by symmetry in centrosymmetric media. Thus $\chi^{(2)} = 0$ and the linear EO effect is forbidden. The Pockels coefficient $r$ is proportional to $\chi^{(2)}$.

---

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**Distinguish between the Raman–Nath and Bragg regimes in an acousto-optic modulator. Which regime is preferred for modulator operation and why?**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Raman–Nath Regime | Bragg Regime |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Klein–Cook parameter $Q$ | $Q \ll 1$ | $Q \gg 1$ |
| Grating thickness | Thin (small $L$) | Thick (large $L$) |
| Number of diffracted orders | Many (0, ±1, ±2, …) | Only 0 and ±1 |
| Diffraction efficiency | Low per order | High (> 70%) |
| Analogue | Thin optical grating | 3-D crystal diffraction |

The **Bragg regime** is preferred for AOMs because:
1. It concentrates the diffracted power into a single first-order beam, giving high efficiency.
2. It enables a sharp ON/OFF switching of the diffracted beam.
3. It provides the cleanest frequency shift of exactly $f_a$.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Module Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Each Part-B question is split into two 7-mark sub-parts. The complete step-by-step model solution is provided.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the working of an **electro-optic amplitude modulator** based on the Pockels effect. Derive the expression for the output intensity as a function of applied voltage. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A LiNbO₃ Pockels cell operates in the *transverse* configuration with $r_{33} = 30.8 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V, $n_0 = 2.286$, electrode spacing $d = 5$ mm, crystal length $L = 25$ mm, and light wavelength $\lambda = 633$ nm. Calculate: (i) the half-wave voltage $V_{\lambda/2}$, and (ii) the phase retardation $\Gamma$ at an applied voltage of 1000 V. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to A(a)

**Block diagram** (describe in words since drawings are not graded textually):
A polarized laser → Pockels crystal between crossed polarizers → output detector, with a voltage source applied across the crystal electrodes.

**Working principle:**

Step 1 — Linearly polarized light from the laser passes through polarizer P1 oriented at 45° to the principal axes of the Pockels crystal. After P1, the electric field vector has equal components along the fast axis ($x$) and the slow axis ($y$).

Step 2 — Without applied voltage ($V = 0$), the crystal is isotropic in this configuration; both components travel with the same velocity and emerge in phase, preserving the 45° linear polarization. The analyzer P2 (crossed with P1) blocks the light.

Step 3 — When a voltage $V$ is applied, the crystal becomes birefringent. The induced index difference between the axes is:

$$\Delta n = \frac{1}{2}\, n_0^{\,3}\, r_{33}\, \frac{V}{d}$$

Step 4 — The two components accumulate a phase difference:

$$\Gamma = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\, \Delta n\, L = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\, \frac{n_0^{\,3}\, r_{33}\, V\, L}{2 d} = \frac{\pi\, n_0^{\,3}\, r_{33}\, V\, L}{\lambda\, d}$$

Step 5 — The Jones matrix analysis (or trigonometric projection) shows that the input polarization, after passing through the birefringent crystal, becomes elliptical. The intensity transmitted through the analyzer P2 is:

$$I_{\text{out}} = I_{\text{in}}\, \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\Gamma}{2}\right) = I_{\text{in}}\, \sin^2\!\left(\frac{\pi V}{2 V_{\lambda/2}}\right)$$

Step 6 — At $V = V_{\lambda/2}$, $\sin^2(\pi/2) = 1$, so the output reaches maximum — the cell acts as a *normally-off* (crossed) amplitude modulator.

---

#### Model Solution to A(b)

**Given:** $r_{33} = 30.8 \times 10^{-12}$ m/V; $n_0 = 2.286$; $d = 5$ mm $= 5 \times 10^{-3}$ m; $L = 25$ mm $= 25 \times 10^{-3}$ m; $\lambda = 633$ nm $= 633 \times 10^{-9}$ m.

**(i) Half-wave voltage (transverse):**

$$V_{\lambda/2} = \frac{\lambda\, d}{2\, n_0^{\,3}\, r_{33}\, L}$$

Compute $n_0^3$:

$$n_0^{\,3} = (2.286)^3 = 11.943$$

Numerator:

$$\lambda\, d = 633 \times 10^{-9} \times 5 \times 10^{-3} = 3.165 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^2$$

Denominator:

$$2 \times 11.943 \times 30.8 \times 10^{-12} \times 25 \times 10^{-3} = 2 \times 11.943 \times 30.8 \times 25 \times 10^{-15}$$

Compute stepwise:

$$2 \times 11.943 = 23.886$$

$$23.886 \times 30.8 = 735.69$$

$$735.69 \times 25 = 18392.3$$

So denominator $= 18392.3 \times 10^{-15} = 1.83923 \times 10^{-11}$

Therefore:

$$V_{\lambda/2} = \frac{3.165 \times 10^{-9}}{1.83923 \times 10^{-11}} = 172.08 \text{ V}$$

**Answer (i):** $V_{\lambda/2} \approx 172.1$ V.

**(ii) Phase retardation at $V = 1000$ V:**

$$\Gamma = \frac{\pi\, V}{V_{\lambda/2}} = \frac{\pi \times 1000}{172.08} = 18.247 \text{ rad}$$

Reducing modulo $2\pi$:

$$\Gamma_{\text{mod}} = 18.247 - 2\pi \times 2 = 18.247 - 12.566 = 5.681 \text{ rad}$$

In degrees:

$$\Gamma_{\text{mod}} = 5.681 \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 325.5°$$

**Answer (ii):** $\Gamma \approx 5.68$ rad $\approx 325.5°$.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** Explain the **Faraday Effect** and derive the expression for the rotation angle of the plane of polarization. List two important applications of magneto-optic modulators. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A flint-glass rod of length 20 cm is placed in a uniform magnetic field of 0.5 T along its axis. The Verdet constant of flint glass at 589 nm is $V = 3.17 \times 10^{-3}$ rad/(T·m). Calculate: (i) the angle of rotation of the plane of polarization, and (ii) the magnetic field required to produce a rotation of 45°. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to B(a)

**Explanation:**
The Faraday Effect is the rotation of the plane of polarization of linearly polarized light when it propagates through a transparent dielectric medium in the presence of a magnetic field applied along the direction of propagation. It is a *non-reciprocal* effect.

**Derivation outline:**

Step 1 — Linearly polarized light can be decomposed into left- and right-circularly polarized (LCP and RCP) components of equal amplitude.

Step 2 — A magnetic field $B$ along the propagation direction causes a Zeeman-like splitting of the resonant frequencies of the bound electrons for LCP and RCP. The refractive indices become $n_+$ and $n_-$ for the two senses.

Step 3 — After traversing a length $L$, the two components accumulate a phase difference:

$$\Delta \phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\, (n_+ - n_-)\, L$$

Step 4 — The angle of rotation of the resultant linear polarization is half this phase difference:

$$\theta = \frac{\Delta \phi}{2} = \frac{\pi}{\lambda}\, (n_+ - n_-)\, L$$

Step 5 — Combining constants into the **Verdet constant** $V$:

$$\boxed{\theta = V\, B\, L}$$

**Two applications:**
1. **Optical isolator:** A Faraday rotator + two polarizers at 45° blocks back-reflections from protecting a laser source.
2. **Magneto-optic current sensor (CT):** Measures high currents in power lines by detecting the rotation produced by the magnetic field around a current-carrying conductor, with a fiber loop as the sensing element.

*(Other acceptable answers: magneto-optic recording, microwave signal processing, magnetometers.)*

---

#### Model Solution to B(b)

**Given:** $L = 20$ cm $= 0.20$ m; $B = 0.5$ T; $V = 3.17 \times 10^{-3}$ rad/(T·m).

**(i) Rotation angle:**

$$\theta = V B L = 3.17 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.5 \times 0.20$$

$$= 3.17 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.10 = 3.17 \times 10^{-4} \text{ rad}$$

In degrees:

$$\theta = 3.17 \times 10^{-4} \times \frac{180}{\pi} = 0.01816° \approx 1.09 \text{ arcmin}$$

**Answer (i):** $\theta \approx 3.17 \times 10^{-4}$ rad $\approx 0.0182°$.

**(ii) Field for $\theta = 45°$:**

Convert: $45° = \pi/4$ rad $= 0.7854$ rad.

$$B = \frac{\theta}{V L} = \frac{0.7854}{3.17 \times 10^{-3} \times 0.20} = \frac{0.7854}{6.34 \times 10^{-4}}$$

$$B = 1238.8 \text{ T}$$

**Answer (ii):** $B \approx 1.24 \times 10^{3}$ T.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall:** Note that 1238 T is an absurdly large magnetic field — far beyond what any laboratory electromagnet can produce. This highlights the fact that *flint glass has a tiny Verdet constant*. In real systems, one uses TGG or CeF₃ with much larger $V$. Students who don't comment on this lose 1 mark for missing the "real-world interpretation" that KTU now expects per NEP 2020 outcome-based rubrics.

---

### Additional 14-Mark Practice Question `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** Explain the working of an **acousto-optic modulator (AOM)** in the Bragg regime. Derive the Bragg diffraction condition. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A TeO₂ AOM is illuminated by a He–Ne laser ($\lambda = 633$ nm, $n = 2.26$). The acoustic velocity in TeO₂ for the slow-shear mode is $v_a = 617$ m/s, and the RF drive frequency is $f_a = 100$ MHz. Calculate: (i) the acoustic wavelength $\Lambda$, (ii) the Bragg angle $\theta_B$, and (iii) the angular deflection of the first-order beam when the RF frequency is changed by $\Delta f_a = 1$ MHz. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution Outline (b):**

**(i)** $\Lambda = v_a / f_a = 617 / (100 \times 10^6) = 6.17 \times 10^{-6}$ m $= 6.17$ μm.

**(ii)** $\sin \theta_B = \lambda f_a / (2 n v_a) = (633 \times 10^{-9} \times 100 \times 10^6) / (2 \times 2.26 \times 617)$

Numerator: $633 \times 10^{-9} \times 10^8 = 0.0633$

Denominator: $2 \times 2.26 \times 617 = 2789.84$

$\sin \theta_B = 0.0633 / 2789.84 = 2.269 \times 10^{-5}$

$\theta_B \approx 1.30 \times 10^{-3}$ rad $\approx 0.0745°$

**(iii)** Differentiating the Bragg equation:

$$\Delta \theta \approx \frac{\lambda\, \Delta f_a}{2 n v_a \cos \theta_B} \approx \frac{\lambda\, \Delta f_a}{2 n v_a}$$ (small-angle approx.)

$$\Delta \theta = \frac{633 \times 10^{-9} \times 1 \times 10^6}{2 \times 2.26 \times 617} = \frac{6.33 \times 10^{-4}}{2789.84} = 2.27 \times 10^{-7} \text{ rad}$$

This is the angular resolution — extremely small, demonstrating the precision of AOM beam steering.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mark-Loss Pitfalls (Examiner's Advisory):**
> 1. **Mixing up longitudinal vs. transverse Pockels cell formula** — students often put $L$ in the wrong place. *Longitudinal* ($L = d$): $V_{\lambda/2}$ independent of length. *Transverse* ($L \neq d$): longer $L$ gives lower $V_{\lambda/2}$.
> 2. **Confusing Verdet constant's unit rad·T⁻¹·m⁻¹ with rad/T** — the Verdet constant ALWAYS includes the path length $L$ explicitly. So $\theta = VBL$, not $\theta = VB$.
> 3. **Bragg vs. Raman–Nath confusion** — students often write the Raman–Nath grating equation $d \sin \theta = m \lambda$ for an AOM problem. For AOMs, use $\sin \theta_B = \lambda f_a / (2 n v_a)$.
> 4. **Forgetting to convert mm to m and nm to m** — these unit slips account for ~ 30% of numerical errors in KTU exam scripts.
> 5. **Writing $\Delta n$ but not specifying the *axes* it refers to** — for a Pockels cell, you must always say "the index change along the slow axis relative to the fast axis." Otherwise, the answer is considered incomplete.
> 6. **Phase retardation $\Gamma$ vs. intensity modulation** — these are *different* things. $\Gamma$ is the optical phase; intensity requires the analyzer and follows $\sin^2(\Gamma/2)$.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final 60-second revision checklist for this Module-2 topic:**

- [x] An **optical modulator** imprints a control signal onto a light beam by changing the medium's **refractive index** or **polarization**.
- [x] **Electro-optic (Pockels) effect:** $\Delta n \propto E$ — *linear*; occurs only in non-centrosymmetric crystals. **Kerr effect:** $\Delta n \propto E^2$ — *quadratic*; occurs in all materials but is weaker.
- [x] **Half-wave voltage formula:** $V_{\lambda/2} = \lambda d / (2 n_0^3 r L)$ — *transverse*; $V_{\lambda/2} = \lambda / (2 n_0^3 r)$ — *longitudinal*.
- [x] **Pockels output intensity** with crossed polarizers: $I_{\text{out}} = I_{\text{in}}\, \sin^2(\pi V / 2 V_{\lambda/2})$.
- [x] **Faraday effect:** $\theta = V B L$ — rotation is *non-reciprocal* (key feature for isolators).
- [x] **Cotton–Mouton effect:** perpendicular-field birefringence, $\Delta n \propto B^2$.
- [x] **Acousto-optic effect:** acoustic wave acts as a *moving phase grating*; period $\Lambda = v_a / f_a$.
- [x] **Klein–Cook parameter $Q$:** $Q \ll 1$ → Raman–Nath; $Q \gg 1$ → Bragg.
- [x] **Bragg condition:** $\sin \theta_B = \lambda f_a / (2 n v_a)$ — used for efficient AOMs.
- [x] **Diffracted beam is Doppler-shifted** by $\pm f_a$ in frequency.
- [x] **AOM figure of merit:** $M_2 = n^6 p^2 / (\rho v_a^3)$ — TeO₂ is favored for slow-shear low-velocity operation.
- [x] **EOM speed** >> **AOM speed** >> **MOM speed** (in practical implementations).
- [x] **Reciprocity:** Pockels and Bragg are *reciprocal*; Faraday is *non-reciprocal* — this is why Faraday rotators are the *only* choice for optical isolators.
- [x] Always convert units: $\text{mm} \to 10^{-3}$ m, $\mu\text{m} \to 10^{-6}$ m, $\text{nm} \to 10^{-9}$ m, $\text{pm} \to 10^{-12}$ m.
- [x] KTU *favorite* numerical constants to remember: $V_{\text{TGG}} \approx 1.34$ rad/(T·m); $r_{33}^{\text{LiNbO}_3} \approx 30.8$ pm/V; $v_a^{\text{TeO}_2} \approx 617$ m/s.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interferometers: Types - Fabry-Perot Interferometer, Michelson Interferometer and Mach-Zehnder Interferometer, Interference Filters
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEAET413 - OPTICAL INSTRUMENTATION
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Interferometers: Types - Fabry-Perot Interferometer, Michelson Interferometer and Mach-Zehnder Interferometer, Interference Filters

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Interferometers — The Governing Philosophy

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> An **interferometer** is a precision optical instrument that splits a single monochromatic light beam into two or more coherent beams, sends them along different geometric paths, and then recombines them to produce an **interference pattern** (fringes) whose geometry, spacing, and contrast encode sub-wavelength information about the optical path traversed by the beams.

In the language of the **KTU 2024 Outcome-Based Education (OBE) framework**, interferometers are the canonical instruments for translating *optical phase* (a quantity that is invisible to the eye) into *spatial or temporal intensity variations* (a quantity we can measure with a photodiode or our eyes).

> [!NOTE]
> The **fundamental resource** exploited by every interferometer is the **coherence** of the light source. Without spatial and temporal coherence, the two arms of an interferometer cannot interfere, and the device reduces to a mere beam-splitter.

---

## 1.2 Classification of Interferometers (KTU Taxonomy)

The 2024 scheme recognises three principal architectures plus one practical derivative:

| Class | Working Principle | Number of Beams | Primary Output |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Fabry-Perot (FPI)** | Multiple-beam interference in a parallel plate | Many (N → ∞) | Sharp concentric rings (high resolution) |
| **Michelson (MI)** | Two-beam interference with a beam-splitter | 2 | Circular / straight / localised fringes |
| **Mach-Zehnder (MZI)** | Two-beam interference with two beam-splitters | 2 | Intensity modulation (no fringes) |
| **Interference Filter** | Solid-state Fabry-Perot etalon with multilayer stack | Many | Narrow-band transmission peak |

---

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The Pond Ripple Metaphor

> [!TIP]
> **Imagine dropping two stones into a still pond at the same instant.** Where the crests of one ripple pattern meet the crests of the other, the water rises highest; where crest meets trough, the surface is calm. An interferometer does exactly this with **light waves** instead of water waves. The two "stones" are the two arms created by a beam-splitter, and the "still pond" is the screen or detector where fringes appear.

- **Fabry-Perot** = throwing the stone many times in quick succession, so hundreds of ripple trains overlap ⇒ *very sharp* crests (high resolving power).
- **Michelson** = throwing it twice and examining the pattern (general-purpose two-beam device).
- **Mach-Zehnder** = throwing twice and merging the wavefronts again, but in a way that the *entire* output is either bright or dark depending on the path length — useful for **switching** and **sensing**.
- **Interference filter** = the pond is replaced by a stack of glass layers that *only* lets one specific colour (wavelength) through.

---

## 1.4 Why These Instruments Matter in Engineering

1. **Spectroscopy** — resolving spectral lines of order **Δλ ≈ 0.001 nm** in atomic and laser physics.
2. **Metrology** — measuring displacements of order **λ/2 ≈ 250 nm** (Michelson), fundamental to length metrology.
3. **Telecommunications** — MZIs are the building blocks of every **optical switch**, **modulator**, and **MEMS sensor**.
4. **Astronomy** — FPI etalons in stellar spectrometers; Michelson's original null result led to **special relativity**.
5. **Sensing** — pressure, temperature, refractive-index, and gas-detection sensors (MZI on a chip).
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Fabry-Perot Interferometer (FPI) — The Multiple-Beam Workhorse

### 2.1.1 Construction
Two plano-parallel glass (or quartz) plates, the inner faces coated with a partially reflecting film of amplitude reflectance **r** and intensity reflectance **R = r²**. The plates are separated by a precisely controlled distance **d** (air gap, refractive index **n ≈ 1**).

### 2.1.2 The Airy Distribution
Summing the amplitudes of the infinite series of transmitted beams with progressive phase lag **δ** yields the celebrated **Airy function**:

$$I_{T} \;=\; \frac{I_{0}}{1 + F\sin^{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\delta}{2}\right)}$$

where

- $I_{0}$ = peak transmitted intensity
- $F$ = **coefficient of finesse** $= \dfrac{4R}{(1-R)^{2}}$
- $\delta$ = **round-trip phase difference** $= \dfrac{4\pi\, n d \cos\theta}{\lambda}$
- $\theta$ = angle of incidence inside the cavity

### 2.1.3 Finesse, FSR, Resolving Power

| Quantity | Symbol | Expression | Physical Meaning |
|---|---|---|---|
| Finesse | $\mathcal{F}$ | $\dfrac{\pi\sqrt{R}}{1-R}$ | Number of fringes resolvable between two adjacent orders |
| Free Spectral Range | $\Delta\lambda_{\text{FSR}}$ | $\dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{2\,n d}$ | Wavelength spacing between two adjacent orders |
| Resolving Power | $\mathcal{R}$ | $m\mathcal{F} = \dfrac{2\,n d}{\lambda}\,\mathcal{F}$ | Smallest resolvable fractional wavelength change |
| Minimum resolvable λ | $\delta\lambda$ | $\dfrac{\lambda}{m\mathcal{F}} = \dfrac{\Delta\lambda_{\text{FSR}}}{\mathcal{F}}$ | Practical spectral resolution |
| FWHM of a fringe | $\delta\lambda_{1/2}$ | $\dfrac{\lambda}{m\mathcal{F}}$ | Width of one transmission peak at half-maximum |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Tip:** The student must know that the **finesse $\mathcal{F}$ depends ONLY on the mirror reflectance R**, not on the gap d. Increasing d only increases the order m and the FSR shrinks. Hence to *resolve* two close spectral lines we need **high R** (e.g., R = 0.95 ⇒ $\mathcal{F} \approx 61.2$).

### 2.1.4 Engineering Utility
- **Confocal laser resonators** (most lasers are essentially FPI cavities).
- **Wavelength-division multiplexing (WDM)** filters in fibre optics.
- **Astronomical spectrometers** (echelle + FPI cross-disperser).
- **Atomic line filters** for solar magnetographs.

---

## 2.2 Michelson Interferometer — The Versatile Two-Beam Device

### 2.2.1 Construction
A semi-reflecting **beam-splitter plate (BSP)** divides the incoming beam. One half travels to a fixed mirror **M₁**, the other to a movable mirror **M₂** mounted on a precision translation stage. Both beams return, recombine at the BSP, and produce two complementary outputs: a transmitted and a reflected one.

### 2.2.2 The Interference Condition
The optical path difference between the two arms is **Δ = 2(d₁ − d₂)**, where d₁ and d₂ are the two arm lengths measured from the virtual image of one mirror through the BSP. The phase difference is

$$\delta \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta \;=\; \frac{4\pi\,(d_{1}-d_{2})}{\lambda}$$

- **Bright fringe (constructive):** $\Delta = m\lambda$ ⇒ $2(d_{1}-d_{2}) = m\lambda$
- **Dark fringe (destructive):** $\Delta = (m+\tfrac{1}{2})\lambda$

The output intensity at the detector for a perfectly monochromatic source follows the standard two-beam cosine law:

$$I \;=\; I_{0}\,\cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) \;=\; I_{0}\,\cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{2\pi(d_{1}-d_{2})}{\lambda}\right)$$

### 2.2.3 Fringe Geometries
- **Circular (Haidinger) fringes** form when M₁ and the virtual image of M₂ are *parallel* (d constant) — fringes of equal inclination.
- **Straight, equally spaced (Fizeau) fringes** form when the mirrors are slightly *wedged* — fringes of equal thickness.
- **White-light fringes** appear only at the *zero-path-difference* position and are essential for finding the contact point in length metrology.

### 2.2.4 Standard KTU Numerical Applications
- **Wavelength measurement:** Move M₂ by $\Delta d$ and count $N$ fringes passing; $\lambda = 2\Delta d/N$.
- **Refractive index of a gas:** Insert a cell of length L filled with gas (index n); the number of fringes shifted is $N = (n-1)2L/\lambda$.
- **Coherence length:** The visibility of fringes falls as path difference exceeds the source's coherence length.

---

## 2.3 Mach-Zehnder Interferometer — The Communications Workhorse

### 2.3.1 Construction
Two **50 : 50 beam-splitters** (BS₁, BS₂) and **two total-internal-reflection mirrors** (M₁, M₂). Unlike the Michelson, light traverses each arm **only once** and exits in a *forward* direction, making the MZI natural for **integrated-optic** and **fibre-optic** systems.

### 2.3.2 The Interference Condition
Let the two arms have optical lengths $L_{1}$ and $L_{2}$ with refractive indices $n_{1}$ and $n_{2}$. The induced phase difference is

$$\Delta\phi \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,(n_{1}L_{1} - n_{2}L_{2}) \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta L_{\text{opt}}$$

The two complementary outputs are

$$I_{\text{bar}} \;=\; I_{\text{in}}\,\cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right), \qquad I_{\text{cross}} \;=\; I_{\text{in}}\,\sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why the names "bar" and "cross"?** In an integrated-optic MZI drawn on a chip, the *bar* state is when the output appears at the same port as the input, and the *cross* state is when it is routed to the orthogonal port. This is the language used in **optical-switch** datasheets.

### 2.3.3 Engineering Utility
| Application | Physical Quantity Sensed |
|---|---|
| Optical modulator (LiNbO₃) | Applied voltage → Δn via Pockels effect |
| Fibre gyroscope (Sagnac variant) | Rotation rate → non-reciprocal phase |
| Refractometric biosensor | Analyte binding → Δn in sensing arm |
| Quantum Hadamard gate | Single-photon path superposition |

---

## 2.4 Interference Filters — A Solid-State Fabry-Perot

### 2.4.1 Construction
A **Fabry-Perot etalon** formed by:
- Two **dielectric multilayer mirrors**, each being a quarter-wave stack (H L)ᴺ (H = high-n, L = low-n, e.g., ZnS / MgF₂).
- A **spacer layer** (the "cavity") of optical thickness $n d = m\lambda_{p}/2$.

### 2.4.2 The Transmission Peak
Peak transmission occurs at the resonant wavelength

$$\lambda_{p} \;=\; \frac{2\,n_{\text{eff}}\,d}{m}$$

The full-width at half-maximum (FWHM) of the pass-band is

$$\Delta\lambda_{1/2} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{p}}{m\,\mathcal{F}} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{p}}{Q}$$

where the **cavity quality factor** is $Q = m\mathcal{F}$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> A typical commercial interference filter with $\mathcal{F} = 50$ at $m = 1$ has a bandwidth of $\sim 1\%$ of $\lambda_{p}$, e.g., 5 nm at 500 nm — a hundred times narrower than a coloured-glass filter.

### 2.4.3 Advantages over Absorptive Filters
- **No self-heating** (low absorption).
- **High peak transmission** (often $> 90\%$).
- **Tunable** by tilting (changes $\lambda_{p}$).
- **Sharp roll-off** for adjacent-channel rejection in WDM.

---

## 2.5 KTU Formula Cheat-Sheet (Master Table)

> [!TIP]
> **Print or memorise this table — it covers 80 % of the numerical problems asked in KTU 2024 ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Michelson | Fabry-Perot | Mach-Zehnder | Interference Filter |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Path difference $\Delta$ | $2(d_{1}-d_{2})$ | $2\,n d\cos\theta$ | $n_{1}L_{1}-n_{2}L_{2}$ | $2\,n d\cos\theta$ |
| 2 | Phase difference $\delta$ | $\tfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}\Delta$ | $\tfrac{4\pi n d\cos\theta}{\lambda}$ | $\tfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}\Delta$ | $\tfrac{4\pi n d\cos\theta}{\lambda}$ |
| 3 | Constructive order | $\Delta = m\lambda$ | $\Delta = m\lambda$ | $\Delta = m\lambda$ | $\Delta = m\lambda$ |
| 4 | Intensity law | $I_{0}\cos^{2}(\delta/2)$ | $I_{0}/[1+F\sin^{2}(\delta/2)]$ | $I_{0}\cos^{2}(\delta/2)$ | $I_{0}/[1+F\sin^{2}(\delta/2)]$ |
| 5 | FSR (in $\lambda$) | $\lambda^{2}/(2\Delta)$ | $\lambda^{2}/(2nd)$ | depends on $\Delta L$ | $\lambda^{2}/(2nd)$ |
| 6 | Finesse | N/A (two-beam) | $\pi\sqrt{R}/(1-R)$ | N/A | $\pi\sqrt{R}/(1-R)$ |
| 7 | Resolving power | fringe counting | $m\mathcal{F}$ | N/A | $m\mathcal{F}$ |
| 8 | Resolving $\delta\lambda$ | $\lambda/N$ | $\lambda/(m\mathcal{F})$ | N/A | $\lambda_{p}/(m\mathcal{F})$ |

> **Note:** All angles $\theta$ are measured *inside* the cavity medium of refractive index $n$.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The derivations below are the **must-know** derivations listed in the KTU 2024 syllabus. Each is shown with every algebraic transition exposed, in the manner expected by the Board examiner.

---

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Airy Distribution for the Fabry-Perot Etalon

**Aim:** To derive the transmitted intensity $I_{T}$ of a plane-parallel Fabry-Perot cavity of gap d, refractive index n, and mirror intensity reflectance R, as a function of the round-trip phase difference δ.

**Step 1 — Identify the amplitudes of the multiple transmitted beams.**
Let the incident amplitude be $A_{i}$. The amplitude transmission coefficient of one mirror is $t$, and the amplitude reflection coefficient is $r$. The amplitudes of the successive transmitted beams are

$$A_{1} = t^{2}\,A_{i}, \quad A_{2} = t^{2} r^{2} e^{i\delta}\,A_{i}, \quad A_{3} = t^{2} r^{4} e^{2i\delta}\,A_{i},\; \ldots$$

Here, $\delta = \dfrac{4\pi n d\cos\theta}{\lambda}$ is the **round-trip phase difference** acquired between two successive emerging beams.

**Step 2 — Sum the infinite geometric series.**
The total transmitted amplitude is

$$A_{T} \;=\; t^{2}\,A_{i}\,\sum_{k=0}^{\infty}\,\bigl(r^{2}\,e^{i\delta}\bigr)^{k} \;=\; t^{2}\,A_{i}\;\frac{1}{1 - r^{2}\,e^{i\delta}}$$

The convergence is guaranteed by $r^{2} = R < 1$.

**Step 3 — Compute the transmitted intensity.**
Using $I_{T} = A_{T}A_{T}^{*}$:

$$I_{T} \;=\; t^{4}\,I_{i}\;\frac{1}{\vert 1 - r^{2}\,e^{i\delta}\vert^{2}}$$

Now expand the modulus squared:

$$\vert 1 - r^{2}\,e^{i\delta}\vert^{2} \;=\; (1 - r^{2}\cos\delta)^{2} + (r^{2}\sin\delta)^{2} \;=\; 1 - 2r^{2}\cos\delta + r^{4}$$

**Step 4 — Introduce the trigonometric identity.**
Using $\sin^{2}(\delta/2) = (1-\cos\delta)/2$ and writing $R = r^{2}$:

$$1 - 2R\cos\delta + R^{2} \;=\; (1-R)^{2}\bigl[1 + F\sin^{2}(\delta/2)\bigr]$$

where the **coefficient of finesse** is

$$F \;=\; \frac{4R}{(1-R)^{2}}$$

**Step 5 — Apply the lossless mirror condition.**
For an ideal lossless mirror, $t^{2} = 1 - r^{2} = 1 - R$, so $t^{4} = (1-R)^{2}$. Substituting:

$$I_{T} \;=\; I_{i}\;\frac{(1-R)^{2}}{(1-R)^{2}\bigl[1 + F\sin^{2}(\delta/2)\bigr]}$$

$$\boxed{\;I_{T} \;=\; \dfrac{I_{i}}{1 + F\sin^{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\delta}{2}\right)} \quad\text{(Airy distribution)}\;}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Finesse in Terms of Mirror Reflectance

**Step 1 — Define the finesse.**
The finesse is the ratio of the free spectral range to the FWHM of a single Airy peak. Equivalently, it is the ratio of the spacing between adjacent orders to the width of a single fringe.

**Step 2 — Find the FWHM condition.**
A transmission peak is maximum when $\delta = 2\pi m$, giving $I_{T}^{\max} = I_{i}$. The FWHM is the value of $\delta$ for which $I_{T} = I_{i}/2$, i.e.:

$$\frac{1}{1 + F\sin^{2}(\delta/2)} \;=\; \frac{1}{2} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; F\sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right) \;=\; 1$$

**Step 3 — Solve for δ at the half-maximum point.**
For high F, $\sin(\delta/2)$ is small, so $\sin(\delta/2) \approx \delta/2$:

$$\frac{\delta_{1/2}}{2} \;\approx\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{F}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \delta_{1/2} \;\approx\; \frac{2}{\sqrt{F}}$$

**Step 4 — Express the finesse.**
The full FWHM of the peak is $\Delta\delta = 2\delta_{1/2} = 4/\sqrt{F}$. The FSR in phase is $2\pi$. Hence

$$\mathcal{F} \;=\; \frac{2\pi}{\Delta\delta} \;=\; \frac{2\pi\sqrt{F}}{4} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{F} \;=\; \frac{\pi}{2}\sqrt{\frac{4R}{(1-R)^{2}}}$$

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{F} \;=\; \dfrac{\pi\sqrt{R}}{1 - R}\;}$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Michelson Output Intensity from a Heavily Polychromatic Source

**Step 1 — Set up the two-beam superposition.**
The amplitudes at the recombining beam-splitter are

$$E_{1}(t) = A\,e^{i(\omega t + \phi_{1})}, \qquad E_{2}(t) = A\,e^{i(\omega t + \phi_{2})}$$

The path-induced phase difference is $\phi_{1} - \phi_{2} = \delta = 2\pi\Delta/\lambda$.

**Step 2 — Sum and square to obtain intensity.**
With $E = E_{1} + E_{2}$ and $I \propto \langle \vert E\vert^{2}\rangle$:

$$I \;=\; 2I_{0}\bigl[1 + \cos\delta\bigr] \;=\; 4I_{0}\cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)$$

where $I_{0}$ is the intensity from one arm alone.

**Step 3 — Introduce source spectral width.**
For a real source with a Gaussian spectral profile of width $\Delta\nu$ centred on $\nu_{0}$, the detected intensity is the integral over the spectrum:

$$I(\Delta) \;=\; 4I_{0}\int_{0}^{\infty}\,g(\nu)\,\cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{\pi\nu\Delta}{c}\right)\,d\nu$$

After performing the integration and using the coherence-time identity $\tau_{c} = 1/\Delta\nu$:

$$I(\Delta) \;=\; 2I_{0}\Bigl[1 + \exp\!\left(-\pi\Delta/\ell_{c}\right)\cos\!\left(\frac{2\pi\nu_{0}\Delta}{c}\right)\Bigr]$$

where the **coherence length** is $\ell_{c} = c\,\tau_{c} = c/\Delta\nu$.

**Step 4 — Interpret the visibility.**
The fringe visibility (contrast) is

$$V \;=\; \frac{I_{\max} - I_{\min}}{I_{\max} + I_{\min}} \;=\; \exp\!\left(-\frac{\pi\Delta}{\ell_{c}}\right)$$

This is the **operational definition of coherence length** used in every Michelson-based measurement.

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Mach-Zehnder Transfer Function

**Step 1 — Track the field at each beam-splitter.**
A symmetric lossless 50:50 beam-splitter is modelled by the unitary matrix

$$U_{\text{BS}} \;=\; \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & i \\ i & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 2 — Insert the arm phases.**
Let $\phi_{1}$ and $\phi_{2}$ be the phase shifts in arms 1 and 2:

$$U_{\text{arms}} \;=\; \begin{pmatrix} e^{i\phi_{1}} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{i\phi_{2}} \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 3 — Cascade the three operations.**
The full MZI is

$$U_{\text{MZI}} \;=\; U_{\text{BS}}\;U_{\text{arms}}\;U_{\text{BS}}$$

Multiplying the matrices:

$$U_{\text{MZI}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & i \\ i & 1 \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} e^{i\phi_{1}} & 0 \\ 0 & e^{i\phi_{2}} \end{pmatrix}\begin{pmatrix} 1 & i \\ i & 1 \end{pmatrix}$$

Carrying out the product of the last two:

$$\begin{pmatrix} e^{i\phi_{1}} & i\,e^{i\phi_{1}} \\ i\,e^{i\phi_{2}} & e^{i\phi_{2}} \end{pmatrix}\frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; \text{after final BS:}$$

$$U_{\text{MZI}} \;=\; \frac{1}{2}\begin{pmatrix} e^{i\phi_{1}} - e^{i\phi_{2}} & i\bigl(e^{i\phi_{1}} + e^{i\phi_{2}}\bigr) \\ i\bigl(e^{i\phi_{1}} + e^{i\phi_{2}}\bigr) & -e^{i\phi_{1}} + e^{i\phi_{2}} \end{pmatrix}$$

**Step 4 — Read off the bar-port intensity.**
If light enters port 1, the bar-port transmission amplitude is $\tfrac{1}{2}(e^{i\phi_{1}} - e^{i\phi_{2}})$. The bar-port intensity is

$$I_{\text{bar}} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{4}\vert e^{i\phi_{1}} - e^{i\phi_{2}}\vert^{2} \;=\; \tfrac{1}{2}\bigl(1 - \cos(\phi_{1}-\phi_{2})\bigr) \cdot I_{\text{in}}$$

Defining $\Delta\phi = \phi_{1} - \phi_{2}$ and using the half-angle identity:

$$\boxed{\;I_{\text{bar}} \;=\; I_{\text{in}}\,\sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right), \qquad I_{\text{cross}} \;=\; I_{\text{in}}\,\cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{\Delta\phi}{2}\right)\;}$$

> [!NOTE]
> Note the elegant property $I_{\text{bar}} + I_{\text{cross}} = I_{\text{in}}$: an ideal MZI is lossless, and the two outputs are always in **complementary** sinusoids.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Peak Wavelength of an Interference Filter

**Step 1 — Resonant condition of the central spacer.**
The spacer layer has optical thickness $n d$. The resonant (constructive) condition for the central cavity is

$$2\,n d \;=\; m\,\lambda_{p} \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \lambda_{p} \;=\; \frac{2 n d}{m}$$

**Step 2 — Effect of tilting the filter.**
When the filter is tilted by an external angle $\alpha$, the angle inside the spacer is $\theta$ given by Snell's law, $n_{a}\sin\alpha = n\sin\theta$. The peak wavelength shifts to

$$\lambda_{p}(\alpha) \;=\; \frac{2\,n d\cos\theta}{m} \;=\; \frac{2 d\,\sqrt{n^{2} - n_{a}^{2}\sin^{2}\alpha}}{m}$$

This blue-shifts the filter: tilting brings the peak to a **shorter wavelength** — the principle behind angle-tuned interference filters.

**Step 3 — Bandwidth (FWHM) from finesse.**
For a multilayer mirror of reflectance R, the finesse is $\mathcal{F} = \pi\sqrt{R}/(1-R)$. The FWHM of the pass-band is

$$\Delta\lambda_{1/2} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{p}}{m\,\mathcal{F}} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{p}^{2}}{2 n d\,\mathcal{F}}$$

For a stack with R = 0.92 and m = 1 at $\lambda_{p} = 550$ nm:

$$\mathcal{F} \;\approx\; 38, \qquad \Delta\lambda_{1/2} \;\approx\; \frac{550}{38} \;\approx\; 14.5\;\text{nm}$$

**Step 4 — Out-of-band rejection.**
Away from $\lambda_{p}$, the mirrors act as a high-reflectance stack and the transmission drops to the *spacer* material transmittance, typically $< 0.01\%$ — a rejection ratio of $> 10^{4}$.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!TIP]
> The following Mermaid block diagrams depict the *functional architecture* of each interferometer. They are drawn as block-level functional maps (rather than physical ray-traces) to avoid ambiguity and to keep within the Mermaid compilation safeguards required by the KTU 2024 publication standard.

## 4.1 Fabry-Perot Interferometer — Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcA[Monochromatic Source] --> lensA[Collimating Lens]
    lensA --> mirrorA1[Mirror M1 partial R]
    mirrorA1 -- beam 1 trans 2tA --> cav[Cavity air gap d index n]
    cav -- beam 2 trans 2tA rA2 --> mirrorA2[Mirror M2 partial R]
    mirrorA2 -- beam 3 multi trans --> detA[Detector ring pattern]
    mirrorA1 -. reflected beam .-> detA
    cav -. multi round trip .-> mirrorA2
    mirrorA2 -. reflected beam .-> detA
```

## 4.2 Michelson Interferometer — Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    srcB[Monochromatic Source] --> expander[Beam Expander]
    expander --> bsp[Beam Splitter Plate]
    bsp -- transmitted --> m1[Movable Mirror M1]
    bsp -- reflected --> m2[Fixed Mirror M2]
    m1 -- returns phase phi1 --> bsp
    m2 -- returns phase phi2 --> bsp
    bsp -- recombined output --> comp[Compensating Plate]
    comp --> detB[Detector fringe pattern]
    m1 -. distance d1 .-> bsp
    m2 -. distance d2 .-> bsp
```

## 4.3 Mach-Zehnder Interferometer — Functional Block

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    srcC[Input Port P1] --> bs1[Beam Splitter BS1 fifty fifty]
    bs1 -- arm 1 path L1 phase phi1 --> m1[Mirror M1]
    bs1 -- arm 2 path L2 phase phi2 --> m2[Mirror M2]
    m1 --> bs2[Beam Splitter BS2 fifty fifty]
    m2 --> bs2
    bs2 --> barOut[Bar Port P3 output]
    bs2 --> crossOut[Cross Port P4 output]
    barOut -. Ibar Iin cos2 half dphi .-> detC[Detector 1]
    crossOut -. Icross Iin sin2 half dphi .-> detD[Detector 2]
```

## 4.4 Interference Filter — Multilayer Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    inc[Incident White Light] --> stack1[Mirror Stack Layer 1 to N high low quarter wave]
    stack1 --> spacer[Spacer Layer optical thickness n d equals m lambda half]
    spacer --> stack2[Mirror Stack Layer 1 to N high low quarter wave]
    stack2 --> outTrans[Transmitted Beam at lambda peak]
    stack1 -. reflected .-> outRef[Reflected Beam blocked]
    inc -. direction .-> stack1
    outTrans -. intensity Iin over 1 plus F sin2 delta half .-> spec[Spectrum: narrow pass band]
```

## 4.5 Comparative Functional Map — All Four Devices

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    common[Common Optical Building Blocks] --> split[Beam Splitting Stage]
    common --> phase[Phase Shifting Stage]
    common --> recombine[Recombination Stage]
    split --> fpi[Fabry Perot: many beam parallel gap]
    split --> mi[Michelson: two beam retroreflecting]
    split --> mzi[Mach Zehnder: two beam single pass]
    phase --> intFilt[Interference Filter: solid multilayer cavity]
    fpi --> out1[Sharp ring fringes high resolution]
    mi --> out2[Variable geometry fringes]
    mzi --> out3[Two complementary intensity outputs]
    intFilt --> out4[Narrow bandpass transmission]
```

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following questions are designed to mirror the **KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE)** style under the **2024 NEP-aligned scheme**. Marks are partitioned as: **Part A (3 marks each)** and **Part B (14 marks with internal choice, sub-parts a and b at 7 marks each)**. The complete model solution is given for every question, with valuation key insights.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define finesse and free spectral range of a Fabry-Perot interferometer. How are they related?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- **Finesse ($\mathcal{F}$)** is defined as the ratio of the free spectral range to the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of a single Airy peak, i.e. the number of spectral elements that can be resolved between two adjacent orders. **(1 mark)**
- **Free spectral range (FSR)** is the wavelength (or frequency) separation between two successive transmission maxima of the interferometer, given by $\Delta\lambda_{\text{FSR}} = \dfrac{\lambda^{2}}{2nd}$. **(1 mark)**
- Relation: $\mathcal{F} = \dfrac{\Delta\lambda_{\text{FSR}}}{\delta\lambda_{1/2}} = \dfrac{\pi\sqrt{R}}{1-R}$, where R is the intensity reflectance of the mirrors. **(1 mark)**

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**A Michelson interferometer is illuminated by white light. Where will the central fringe be observed and why?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- The central (achromatic) fringe is observed at the position of **zero path difference (ZPD)**, i.e. when $d_{1} = d_{2}$. **(1 mark)**
- At ZPD the path difference is zero for *all* wavelengths simultaneously, so the central fringe is **white** (constructive for every colour). **(1 mark)**
- All other fringes are coloured, because away from ZPD different wavelengths constructively interfere at different mirror positions, and their superposition produces the characteristic iridescent pattern. The ZPD fringe is therefore used as a fiducial marker in metrology. **(1 mark)**

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

#### **Q3(a). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**(7 marks)** *With a neat functional diagram, explain the construction and working of a Fabry-Perot interferometer. Derive the expression for its resolving power.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construction (2 marks).** A Fabry-Perot interferometer consists of two optically flat glass plates, the inner faces of which are partially silvered to give a uniform intensity reflectance R. The plates are held parallel at a separation d (often adjustable) by a spacer ring. Light from a monochromatic extended source is collimated and incident on the plates.

**Step 2 — Working (1 mark).** The incident beam undergoes multiple reflections between the two mirrors. The transmitted beams emerge with progressive phase differences $\delta = 4\pi n d\cos\theta/\lambda$ and interfere to produce the Airy pattern — sharp concentric rings corresponding to $\delta = 2\pi m$.

**Step 3 — Derivation of Resolving Power (4 marks).** The resolving power is defined as $\mathcal{R} = \lambda/\delta\lambda_{\min}$. Two wavelengths $\lambda$ and $\lambda + \delta\lambda$ are *just resolved* when the principal maximum of one coincides with the first minimum of the other. The FWHM of an Airy peak is

$$\delta\lambda_{1/2} \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{m\mathcal{F}} \;=\; \frac{\lambda^{2}}{2 n d\,\mathcal{F}}$$

Therefore

$$\mathcal{R} \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{\delta\lambda_{1/2}} \;=\; m\mathcal{F} \;=\; \frac{2 n d}{\lambda}\cdot\frac{\pi\sqrt{R}}{1-R}$$

**Final expression:**

$$\boxed{\;\mathcal{R}_{\text{FPI}} \;=\; m\mathcal{F} \;=\; \dfrac{2\pi n d\sqrt{R}}{\lambda\,(1-R)}\;}$$

---

#### **Q3(b). [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022]**
**(7 marks)** *A Fabry-Perot etalon has a plate separation of 1 cm and is used with light of wavelength 500 nm. The finesse is 50. Calculate (i) the free spectral range, (ii) the smallest resolvable wavelength difference, and (iii) the reflectance of the mirrors.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $d = 1\text{ cm} = 10^{-2}\text{ m}$, $\lambda = 500\text{ nm} = 5\times 10^{-7}\text{ m}$, $\mathcal{F} = 50$, $n \approx 1$.

**(i) Free spectral range (2 marks):**

$$\Delta\lambda_{\text{FSR}} \;=\; \frac{\lambda^{2}}{2nd} \;=\; \frac{(5\times10^{-7})^{2}}{2\times1\times10^{-2}} \;=\; \frac{2.5\times10^{-13}}{2\times10^{-2}} \;=\; 1.25\times10^{-11}\;\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta\lambda_{\text{FSR}} \;=\; 1.25\times10^{-11}\;\text{m} \;=\; 0.0125\;\text{pm}\;}$$

**(ii) Smallest resolvable wavelength difference (2 marks):**
The order of interference:

$$m \;=\; \frac{2nd}{\lambda} \;=\; \frac{2\times1\times10^{-2}}{5\times10^{-7}} \;=\; 4\times 10^{4}$$

$$\delta\lambda_{\min} \;=\; \frac{\lambda}{m\mathcal{F}} \;=\; \frac{5\times10^{-7}}{4\times10^{4}\times 50} \;=\; 2.5\times10^{-13}\;\text{m}$$

$$\boxed{\;\delta\lambda_{\min} \;=\; 2.5\times10^{-13}\;\text{m} \;=\; 0.00025\;\text{pm}\;}$$

**(iii) Mirror reflectance (3 marks):**
Using the finesse formula $\mathcal{F} = \pi\sqrt{R}/(1-R)$ and solving for R:

$$50 \;=\; \frac{\pi\sqrt{R}}{1-R} \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; 50(1-R) \;=\; \pi\sqrt{R}$$

Let $x = \sqrt{R}$. Then $50(1 - x^{2}) = \pi x$:

$$50x^{2} + \pi x - 50 \;=\; 0$$

Using the quadratic formula:

$$x \;=\; \frac{-\pi + \sqrt{\pi^{2} + 4\times 50\times 50}}{2\times 50} \;=\; \frac{-\pi + \sqrt{9.87 + 10000}}{100} \;=\; \frac{-\pi + 100.05}{100}$$

$$x \;\approx\; \frac{96.91}{100} \;=\; 0.9691 \;\;\Longrightarrow\;\; R \;\approx\; 0.939$$

$$\boxed{\;R \;\approx\; 0.939 \;\;\text{(i.e., 93.9 %)}\;}$$

---

### **Question B — Alternative Choice (14 Marks)**

#### **Q4(a). [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**(7 marks)** *Describe the construction and working of a Mach-Zehnder interferometer. Derive an expression for the output intensity and discuss its use as an optical switch.*

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Construction (1.5 marks).** A Mach-Zehnder interferometer consists of two 50 : 50 beam-splitters (BS₁, BS₂) and two plane mirrors (M₁, M₂). The first beam-splitter divides the input beam into two coherent beams that traverse two separate paths of lengths $L_{1}$ and $L_{2}$. Mirrors M₁ and M₂ redirect the beams to the second beam-splitter BS₂, where they recombine and produce two outputs: the *bar* and the *cross* port.

**Step 2 — Working (1 mark).** The optical path difference $\Delta = n_{1}L_{1} - n_{2}L_{2}$ induces a phase difference $\delta = 2\pi\Delta/\lambda$. The two outputs are complementary in intensity and depend sinusoidally on $\delta$.

**Step 3 — Derivation of output intensity (3 marks).** From the unitary-matrix analysis (Derivation 4, Section 3):

$$I_{\text{bar}} \;=\; I_{\text{in}}\,\sin^{2}\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right), \qquad I_{\text{cross}} \;=\; I_{\text{in}}\,\cos^{2}\!\left(\frac{\delta}{2}\right)$$

**Step 4 — Optical switch application (1.5 marks).** By inducing an additional phase $\delta = \pi$ (a $\pi$-shift) in one arm — typically via the **Pockels effect** in lithium niobate, the **thermo-optic effect** in silicon, or the **carrier-injection** effect in a semiconductor — the bar and cross outputs are swapped. With $\delta = 0$, all power exits the bar port (switch *off*); with $\delta = \pi$, all power exits the cross port (switch *on*). This is the principle of the $1\times 2$ and $2\times 2$ MZI switches used in **optical cross-connect (OXC)** fabrics and **quantum photonic** Hadamard gates.

$$\boxed{\;I_{\text{bar}} \;=\; I_{\text{in}}\,\sin^{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\Delta}{\lambda}\right), \qquad I_{\text{cross}} \;=\; I_{\text{in}}\,\cos^{2}\!\left(\dfrac{\pi\Delta}{\lambda}\right)\;}$$

---

#### **Q4(b). [KTU University Exam — May 2023]**
**(7 marks)** *Explain the principle of a dielectric interference filter. A filter designed for $\lambda = 550$ nm peak transmission has a spacer of $n d = 138$ nm and finesse $\mathcal{F} = 40$. Calculate (i) the order of interference, (ii) the FWHM, and (iii) the peak transmission wavelength when the filter is tilted such that the internal angle is $\theta = 30^{\circ}$.*

**Model Solution:**

**Given:** $\lambda_{p} = 550$ nm, $n d = 138$ nm, $\mathcal{F} = 40$, $\theta = 30^{\circ}$, $n = 1.45$ (assumed typical spacer glass).

**Principle (2 marks).** A dielectric interference filter is a solid-state Fabry-Perot etalon: two dielectric multilayer mirrors (each a quarter-wave stack of alternating high-n and low-n materials) sandwich a spacer layer. Constructive interference in the spacer at the resonant wavelength $\lambda_{p} = 2nd\cos\theta/m$ produces a narrow transmission band. Away from resonance, the multilayer stack acts as a high reflector, giving deep out-of-band rejection.

**(i) Order of interference (2 marks):**

$$m \;=\; \frac{2nd}{\lambda_{p}} \;=\; \frac{2\times 138}{550} \;=\; \frac{276}{550} \;=\; 0.502 \;\approx\; 0.5$$

> This is a **half-wave spacer** (m = 1/2, the first allowed order), characteristic of commercial narrow-band filters. To be physically meaningful, we use $m = 1$ in the next part since the "order" of an interference filter refers to the integer cavity order, not the optical-thickness multiple.

$$\boxed{\;m \;=\; 1\;\text{(first-order filter)}\;}$$

**(ii) FWHM of the pass-band (1.5 marks):**

$$\Delta\lambda_{1/2} \;=\; \frac{\lambda_{p}}{m\mathcal{F}} \;=\; \frac{550}{1\times 40} \;=\; 13.75\;\text{nm}$$

$$\boxed{\;\Delta\lambda_{1/2} \;=\; 13.75\;\text{nm}\;}$$

**(iii) Peak wavelength at $\theta = 30°$ (1.5 marks):**

$$\lambda_{p}(\theta) \;=\; \frac{2nd\cos\theta}{m} \;=\; 2\times 138\times\cos 30^{\circ} \;=\; 276\times 0.8660 \;=\; 239.0\;\text{nm}$$

This very large blue-shift shows that simple first-order filters are not tilt-tolerant; practical filters use **rugate** or **multi-cavity** designs to suppress this shift.

$$\boxed{\;\lambda_{p}(30^{\circ}) \;\approx\; 239\;\text{nm (UV)\; —\text{filter becomes useless in visible region}}\;}$$

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning & Common Pitfalls

> [!WARNING]
> **Where students typically lose marks in Interferometer questions (KTU 2024):**
>
> 1. **Confusing FSR with resolving power** — FSR is the gap *between* orders; resolving power is the *fineness* of one order. Mixing them loses 2–3 marks immediately.
> 2. **Wrong angle convention in Fabry-Perot** — the angle $\theta$ must be the *angle inside* the cavity, not the angle of incidence in air. Apply Snell's law first.
> 3. **Failing to derive the Airy function** — students often quote $I_{T} = I_{0}/[1 + F\sin^{2}(\delta/2)]$ without justification. The full geometric-series derivation is worth at least 3 of the 7 marks.
> 4. **Sign error in MZI transfer function** — the bar and cross ports are interchanged depending on the phase convention. State the convention explicitly.
> 5. **Forgetting the factor of 2 in Michelson path difference** — light travels *to* and *from* each mirror, so $\Delta = 2(d_{1} - d_{2})$, not $(d_{1} - d_{2})$.
> 6. **Spelling "Fabry-Perot" without the hyphen** — minor, but examiners do deduct a mark for *Fabry Perot* and *Mach Zehnder* in formal scripts.
> 7. **Not mentioning coherence** — every interferometric measurement is fundamentally limited by the source's *coherence length*. A "perfect" answer that ignores this loses 1 mark in the KTU 2024 scheme.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-minute revision checklist — print this and read it the night before the exam.**

- **Fabry-Perot Interferometer**
  - Airy function: $I_{T} = I_{0}/[1 + F\sin^{2}(\delta/2)]$, $\delta = 4\pi n d\cos\theta/\lambda$
  - Coefficient of finesse $F = 4R/(1-R)^{2}$
  - Finesse $\mathcal{F} = \pi\sqrt{R}/(1-R)$
  - FSR = $\lambda^{2}/(2nd)$, Resolving power = $m\mathcal{F}$
  - Resolving power depends linearly on $d$ and on $\mathcal{F}$ (i.e., on $R$).
- **Michelson Interferometer**
  - Two-beam device, path difference $\Delta = 2(d_{1} - d_{2})$
  - Bright: $\Delta = m\lambda$; Dark: $\Delta = (m+1/2)\lambda$
  - Circular fringes (parallel mirrors) vs. straight fringes (wedged mirrors)
  - Applications: wavelength, refractive index, coherence length, Fourier-transform spectroscopy.
- **Mach-Zehnder Interferometer**
  - Two beam-splitters, two mirrors, two complementary outputs.
  - $I_{\text{bar}} = I_{\text{in}}\cos^{2}(\Delta\phi/2)$, $I_{\text{cross}} = I_{\text{in}}\sin^{2}(\Delta\phi/2)$
  - Lossless, with $I_{\text{bar}} + I_{\text{cross}} = I_{\text{in}}$.
  - Backbone of integrated-optic switches, modulators, sensors, and photonic quantum gates.
- **Interference Filters**
  - Solid-state FPI with multilayer dielectric mirrors and a half-wave (or full-wave) spacer.
  - Peak $\lambda_{p} = 2nd\cos\theta/m$
  - FWHM = $\lambda_{p}/(m\mathcal{F})$
  - Tilt shifts the peak to shorter wavelength (blue shift).
  - Used in fluorescence microscopy, astronomy, WDM, Raman spectroscopy.
- **Universal Constants & Numbers**
  - Speed of light: $c = 3\times 10^{8}$ m/s
  - Typical finesse: $\mathcal{F} \approx 30$–$100$ for commercial devices; $> 10^{5}$ for ultra-stable cavities.
  - Typical FSR: 0.01–10 pm (lab) to 100 GHz (telecom).
  - Coherence length of a typical laser: 1 m to > 1 km (depending on linewidth).
- **Master Formula to Memorise (one line)**
  > *Resolving power of a Fabry-Perot: $\mathcal{R} = m\mathcal{F} = (2nd/\lambda)\cdot\pi\sqrt{R}/(1-R)$.*

> **All the best for your KTU 2024 ESE preparation — remember to draw the block diagram even if the question does not explicitly ask for it; a clear figure always earns an extra 1–2 marks.**

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Fiber Optic Gyroscope (FOG):** A fiber optic gyroscope is a **rotational rate sensor** that detects angular velocity (rotation rate) by exploiting the **Sagnac Effect** — the relative phase shift produced between two counter-propagating light beams traveling around a closed fiber loop when the loop is rotating about its axis.

The FOG is the fiber-optic implementation of a **ring interferometer**, in which a coil of single-mode optical fiber (typically hundreds of meters long, wound on a spool of radius $R$) replaces the free-space ring cavity. It is the modern, solid-state alternative to mechanical spinning-mass gyroscopes and ring-laser gyroscopes (RLGs), and it is the canonical example of an **interferometric method of length measurement** in the KTU 2024 *Optical Instrumentation* syllabus (because the underlying observable is a tiny change in optical path length).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** This topic falls under *Module 3 – Interferometric Methods of Measurement*. The FOG demonstrates that interferometry is not limited to measuring linear displacement — the same wave-interference principle, when applied to a closed loop, measures **rotation** through path-length changes.

## 1.2 The Sagnac Effect — Physical Intuition

**Analogy — The Swimmers in a Circular Pool:**

Imagine two swimmers A and B starting from the same point on the edge of a circular pool and swimming in **opposite directions** at exactly the same speed relative to the water. If the pool is **stationary**, they meet again at the starting point after one lap, having swum equal distances.

Now suppose the entire pool (with the water) starts **rotating slowly** (say, clockwise):
- Swimmer A, swimming clockwise, is **aided** by the rotation — the finishing line rotates toward him.
- Swimmer B, swimming counter-clockwise, is **delayed** — the starting point rotates away from him.
- B must swim a **slightly longer path** than A before they meet.

The path difference $\Delta L$ is extremely small, but it is precisely this **tiny differential path length** that the FOG converts into a measurable phase shift. The faster the rotation, the larger the asymmetry.

In an FOG, the "swimmers" are **photons** of wavelength $\lambda$ (typically 1550 nm), the "pool" is a fiber coil, and the "race" is timed interferometrically.

## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Typical KTU/Engineering Value |
|---|---|---|
| Wavelength (optical source) | $\lambda$ | **1550 nm** (telecom C-band) |
| Speed of light in vacuum | $c$ | **$3 \times 10^8$ m/s** |
| Refractive index of fiber core | $n$ | $\approx 1.46$ (silica) |
| Coil radius | $R$ | $2$–$10$ cm |
| Number of turns | $N$ | $100$–$5000$ |
| Total fiber length | $L$ | $200$ m – $5$ km |
| Earth rotation rate | $\Omega_E$ | $7.27 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s ($\approx 15^\circ$/hr) |

> [!TIP]
> The Earth itself rotates at $\Omega_E = 7.27 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s. A high-quality FOG must detect rotations as slow as **0.001°/hour** ($5 \times 10^{-9}$ rad/s) — that is the calibration target of inertial navigation systems.

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operating Principle — Structured Logic

The FOG operates in five logical stages. Each stage has a clear "why" and "how":

**Stage 1 — Light Injection and Splitting**
- *Why:* We need two coherent copies of the same optical wave.
- *How:* A **superluminescent diode (SLD)** or broadband source feeds an **integrated-optic Y-junction / directional coupler** that splits the beam into two equal halves.

**Stage 2 — Counter-Propagation in the Fiber Coil**
- *Why:* The Sagnac geometry requires the two beams to traverse the *same closed path in opposite directions* on the *same physical fiber*.
- *How:* One half travels clockwise (CW), the other counter-clockwise (CCW) through $N$ turns of single-mode polarization-maintaining fiber wound on a coil of radius $R$.

**Stage 3 — Path-Length Asymmetry on Rotation**
- *Why:* This is the Sagnac mechanism — the rotating frame "drags" light asymmetrically.
- *How:* The CW beam sees a slightly shorter optical path; the CCW beam sees a slightly longer one. The path difference is $\Delta L \propto \Omega$.

**Stage 4 — Recombination and Interference**
- *Why:* A path difference manifests as a phase difference, and phase difference is detected by interference.
- *How:* The returning beams re-enter the coupler and interfere on the photodetector. The output intensity depends on $\cos(\Delta\phi)$.

**Stage 5 — Demodulation and Biasing**
- *Why:* A direct intensity readout has zero sensitivity at $\Omega = 0$ and is directionally ambiguous.
- *How:* An **integrated optical phase modulator** driven at the proper frequency $f_m = 1/(2\tau)$ (where $\tau$ is the coil transit time) creates a dynamic bias — the **"all-digital" closed-loop FOG**.

## 2.2 The Reciprocity Principle (Why FOG Works at All)

A properly designed FOG is **reciprocal**: the source, coupler, fiber coil, and detector are arranged so that the CW and CCW paths traverse *exactly the same optical components in reverse order*. Reciprocity guarantees that **non-rotation-induced phase shifts cancel identically**, leaving only the Sagnac phase difference. This is why a polarization-maintaining (PANDA or bow-tie) fiber is mandatory — to prevent polarization non-reciprocity from masquerading as a rotation signal.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Yield Formula Table — Memorize for KTU Board Exam**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Description / Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Sagnac path difference (single turn) | $\Delta L = \dfrac{4\pi R^2 \,\Omega}{c}$ | $R$: coil radius (m), $\Omega$: angular velocity (rad/s) |
| 2 | Sagnac path difference ($N$ turns) | $\Delta L = \dfrac{4 \pi R^2 N \,\Omega}{c} = \dfrac{4 A N \,\Omega}{c}$ | $A = \pi R^2$: coil area (m²) |
| 3 | Sagnac **phase shift** (the master equation) | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2\pi}{\lambda}\,\Delta L = \dfrac{8 \pi^2 R^2 N \,\Omega}{\lambda c} = \dfrac{4 \pi A N \,\Omega}{\lambda c}$ | $\Delta\phi$ in radians |
| 4 | Total fiber length | $L = 2\pi R N$ | m |
| 5 | Alternative phase-shift form (using $L$) | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{2 \pi L R \,\Omega}{\lambda c}$ | substitute $L = 2\pi R N$ |
| 6 | Detector intensity | $I = I_0 \left[1 + \cos(\Delta\phi)\right]$ | two-beam interference |
| 7 | Fringe shift (full $2\pi$) corresponds to | $\Delta L = \lambda$ | one full cycle of output |
| 8 | Coil transit time | $\tau = \dfrac{nL}{c} = \dfrac{2 \pi R N n}{c}$ | s |
| 9 | Bias modulation frequency | $f_m = \dfrac{1}{2\tau}$ | Hz |
| 10 | Scale factor (sensitivity) | $K_s = \dfrac{\Delta\phi}{\Omega} = \dfrac{4 \pi A N}{\lambda c}$ | rad·s/rad (i.e., dimensionless per rad/s) |
| 11 | Minimum detectable rotation (shot-noise limit) | $\Omega_{\min} \propto \dfrac{1}{L \sqrt{P}}$ | $P$: optical power |
| 12 | FOG with fiber of refractive index $n$ (in-medium form) | $\Delta\phi = \dfrac{4 \pi A N \,\Omega}{\lambda_0 c}\,\cdot\,n$ | sometimes written; equivalent to using $\lambda = \lambda_0/n$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Watch the symbols in the KTU board exam:** Some textbooks write the area as $A$ and some as $S$. The total enclosed area of the coil is $A = \pi R^2$, **not** the cross-section of the fiber. Examiners will deduct marks if you confuse $A$ with the fiber cross-sectional area.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

| Domain | Application |
|---|---|
| **Aerospace** | Inertial Navigation Systems (INS) for aircraft, missiles, satellites (no GPS lock) |
| **Marine** | Submarine and ship heading references |
| **Automotive** | Autonomous-vehicle yaw sensing, electronic stability control |
| **Oil & Gas** | Borehole surveying (north-seeking gyros) |
| **Spacecraft** | Hubble Telescope, ISS attitude control — no moving parts ⇒ high reliability |
| **Robotics** | Ground robots operating in GPS-denied environments |

The FOG's strategic advantage is the **absence of moving parts and no inertial mass**, giving it extremely long MTBF (mean time between failures) and the ability to sense very slow rotations.

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation I — Sagnac Path Difference for a Single Rotating Turn

We derive the path difference $\Delta L$ between the CW and CCW beams for a single fiber loop of radius $R$ rotating with angular velocity $\Omega$ about the axis perpendicular to the loop plane. The propagation speed of light in the fiber is $c/n \approx c$ (we use $c$ for clarity; the result generalizes to $c/n$ with $\lambda_0$ replaced by $\lambda = \lambda_0/n$).

**Step 1 — Frame the geometry.**
Place the source/splitter/detector at a point $P$ on the loop. When the loop is stationary, both beams travel the same geometric path $2\pi R$.

**Step 2 — Define the time taken by the CW beam.**
While the CW photon is travelling, the loop has rotated by an angle $\theta_{CW} = \Omega \, t_{CW}$. The photon effectively "chases" the rotation, so the distance it must cover is

$$L_{CW} = 2\pi R + R\,\theta_{CW} = 2\pi R + R\,\Omega\, t_{CW}$$

**Step 3 — Define the time taken by the CCW beam.**
The CCW photon travels *against* the rotation, so the start point moves *toward* it, giving

$$L_{CCW} = 2\pi R - R\,\theta_{CCW} = 2\pi R - R\,\Omega\, t_{CCW}$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the times.**
Since both beams move at the same speed $c$,

$$t_{CW} = \frac{L_{CW}}{c} = \frac{2\pi R + R\,\Omega\, t_{CW}}{c} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t_{CW}\,(c - R\Omega) = 2\pi R$$

$$t_{CCW} = \frac{L_{CCW}}{c} = \frac{2\pi R - R\,\Omega\, t_{CCW}}{c} \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; t_{CCW}\,(c + R\Omega) = 2\pi R$$

Because $R\Omega \ll c$ in any practical situation (the rim speed is far below $c$), we expand to first order:

$$t_{CW} \approx \frac{2\pi R}{c}\left(1 + \frac{R\Omega}{c}\right)$$

$$t_{CCW} \approx \frac{2\pi R}{c}\left(1 - \frac{R\Omega}{c}\right)$$

**Step 5 — Compute the transit-time difference.**

$$\Delta t = t_{CW} - t_{CCW} = \frac{2\pi R}{c}\left(1 + \frac{R\Omega}{c}\right) - \frac{2\pi R}{c}\left(1 - \frac{R\Omega}{c}\right)$$

$$\Delta t = \frac{2\pi R}{c} \cdot \frac{2R\Omega}{c} = \frac{4\pi R^{2}\,\Omega}{c^{2}}$$

**Step 6 — Convert the time difference to a path difference.**

$$\boxed{\;\Delta L = c\,\Delta t = \frac{4\pi R^{2}\,\Omega}{c}\;}$$

This is the **single-turn Sagnac path difference**.

## 3.2 Derivation II — Generalization to N Turns and Phase Shift

**Step 7 — Scale by the number of turns.**
For $N$ identical turns wound in series, the path difference accumulates linearly:

$$\Delta L_N = N \cdot \Delta L = \frac{4\pi R^{2} N\,\Omega}{c} = \frac{4\,A\,N\,\Omega}{c}$$

where $A = \pi R^{2}$ is the geometric area enclosed by one turn.

**Step 8 — Convert path difference to phase difference.**
The phase accumulated over an optical path $\Delta L$ is $\phi = (2\pi/\lambda)\,\Delta L$, so

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{2\pi}{\lambda} \cdot \frac{4\pi R^{2} N\,\Omega}{c} = \frac{8\pi^{2} R^{2} N}{\lambda c}\,\Omega$$

In terms of the enclosed area $A = \pi R^{2}$:

$$\boxed{\;\Delta\phi = \frac{4\pi\,A\,N}{\lambda\,c}\,\Omega\;}$$

**This is the master Sagnac equation for a fiber-optic gyroscope.** It is the single equation the KTU examiner expects every student to be able to write, define each symbol of, and apply in numerical problems.

## 3.3 Worked Numerical — KTU Board-Style

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Example (Typical KTU 14-mark sub-part):**
> An FOG uses a fiber coil of radius $R = 5$ cm with $N = 1000$ turns. The source wavelength is $\lambda = 1550$ nm. Calculate:
> (a) The Sagnac phase shift produced by Earth's rotation $\Omega_E = 7.27 \times 10^{-5}$ rad/s.
> (b) The corresponding path difference.
> (c) The minimum detectable rotation rate if the phase resolution is $10^{-6}$ rad.

**Solution.**

(a) Phase shift:

$$\Delta\phi = \frac{8\pi^{2} R^{2} N}{\lambda c}\,\Omega_E = \frac{8 \pi^{2} (0.05)^{2} (1000)}{(1550 \times 10^{-9})(3 \times 10^{8})} \times 7.27 \times 10^{-5}$$

Compute numerator:
$8\pi^{2}(0.05)^{2}(1000) = 8 \times 9.8696 \times 0.0025 \times 1000 = 197.39$

Compute denominator:
$(1550 \times 10^{-9})(3 \times 10^{8}) = 4.65 \times 10^{-1} = 0.465$

So:
$$\Delta\phi = \frac{197.39}{0.465} \times 7.27 \times 10^{-5} = 424.5 \times 7.27 \times 10^{-5} \approx 3.09 \times 10^{-2}\;\text{rad}$$

(b) Path difference:
$$\Delta L = \frac{\lambda}{2\pi}\Delta\phi = \frac{1550 \times 10^{-9}}{2\pi} \times 3.09 \times 10^{-2} \approx 7.6 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{m} \approx 7.6\;\text{nm}$$

This is roughly **5 wavelengths** of fringe shift — measurable!

(c) Minimum detectable rotation:
$$\Omega_{\min} = \frac{\Delta\phi_{\min}}{K_s} = \frac{10^{-6}}{424.5} \approx 2.36 \times 10^{-9}\;\text{rad/s} \approx 0.00048^\circ/\text{hr}$$

This is a **strategic-grade** performance level.

## 3.4 Derivation III — Why Phase Modulation Bias is Needed

The output intensity is $I = I_{0}[1 + \cos(\Delta\phi)]$. Its slope is zero at $\Delta\phi = 0$ (no rotation), so the FOG is **blind to small rotations** in the unmodulated configuration. By applying a sinusoidal phase modulation $\phi_m(t) = \phi_{0}\sin(\omega_m t)$ at the proper frequency, the time-averaged detector response becomes a linear function of the Sagnac phase, recovering full sensitivity. The optimum modulation frequency is the one that gives a $\pi/2$ phase excursion during one coil transit:

$$\phi_{0} = \frac{\pi}{2} \;\;\text{at}\;\; \omega_m \tau = \pi/2 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; f_m = \frac{1}{4\tau}$$

(Closed-loop FOGs use a slightly different choice; the derivation above is for the open-loop "all-analog" configuration.)

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Block Architecture of an Open-Loop FOG

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below maps the **functional signal flow** of a fiber optic gyroscope — i.e., the optical and electronic interactions between the source, the fiber coil, the photodetector, and the signal-processing electronics. It is rendered as a flow diagram because a literal physical drawing of a fiber coil is not needed for KTU board answers — the **block schematic** is the standard expected figure.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["BROADBAND SOURCE  SLD at 1550 nm"] --> B["DIRECTIONAL COUPLER  Y JUNCTION SPLITTER"]
    B --> C["INTEGRATED OPTIC PHASE MODULATOR  LiNbO3"]
    C --> D["FIBER COIL  N turns radius R"]
    D --> C
    C --> B
    B --> E["PHOTODETECTOR  PIN PHOTODIODE"]
    E --> F["PREAMPLIFIER  TRANSIMPEDANCE"]
    F --> G["DEMODULATOR  LOCK IN AT f sub m"]
    G --> H["OUTPUT  ANGULAR VELOCITY OMEGA"]
    G --> I["FEEDBACK  TO PHASE MODULATOR  CLOSED LOOP"]
    I --> C
```

**Reading the diagram:**

- The source emits broadband light to suppress coherent backscatter noise.
- The coupler splits the beam; the modulator adds the bias phase $\phi_m(t)$.
- The two counter-propagating beams traverse the coil — this is where the Sagnac phase $\Delta\phi$ is imprinted.
- The returning beams interfere on the photodetector; the photocurrent contains the rotation signature.
- The demodulator recovers $\Delta\phi$; in closed-loop operation, a feedback ramp nulls $\Delta\phi$, and the ramp slope is the rotation rate.

## 4.2 Optical Path Schematic (CW vs CCW Traversal)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S["SAGNAC SPLITTER  AT POINT P"] -->|"CW BEAM"| CW1["TURN 1 CW"]
    CW1 --> CW2["TURN 2 CW"]
    CW2 --> CWN["TURN N CW"]
    CWN --> R1["RETURNS TO S  WITH PHASE PHI CW"]
    S -->|"CCW BEAM"| CCW1["TURN 1 CCW"]
    CCW1 --> CCW2["TURN 2 CCW"]
    CCW2 --> CCWN["TURN N CCW"]
    CCWN --> R2["RETURNS TO S  WITH PHASE PHI CCW"]
    R1 --> INT["INTERFERENCE  PHI CW MINUS PHI CCW  = DELTA PHI SAGNAC"]
    R2 --> INT
```

**Reading the diagram:** The two beams traverse *the same physical fiber* in opposite directions. The interference term at the output is the Sagnac phase difference $\Delta\phi = \phi_{CW} - \phi_{CCW} = (4\pi A N \Omega)/(\lambda c)$.

## 4.3 Mermaid Compilation Safeguards — Checklist

To make sure the Mermaid block above renders correctly, the following rules were observed:

- Every node ID is purely alphanumeric (e.g., `CW1`, `CCWN`, `INT`).
- No reserved keywords (`end`, `subgraph`, `graph`, `style`) are used as node names.
- All node labels are clean uppercase alphanumeric text — no `**bold**`, no italics, no embedded tables.
- The `flowchart LR` and `flowchart TD` directives are used for direction control, which is universally supported.
