# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Energy of a Photon and the Bond-Dissociation Criterion for Ablation

**Goal:** Show that 193 nm UV photons carry enough energy to directly break covalent bonds.

**Step 1 — Start from Planck–Einstein relation.**

$$ E_{\text{photon}} = h \nu = \frac{h c}{\lambda} $$

**Step 2 — Substitute numerical values.**

$$ h = 6.626 \times 10^{-34} \text{ J·s}, \quad c = 3.00 \times 10^8 \text{ m/s} $$

For λ = 193 nm = 193 × 10⁻⁹ m:

$$ E = \frac{(6.626 \times 10^{-34})(3.00 \times 10^8)}{193 \times 10^{-9}} $$

$$ E = \frac{1.9878 \times 10^{-25}}{1.93 \times 10^{-7}} $$

$$ E = 1.030 \times 10^{-18} \text{ J} $$

**Step 3 — Convert to electron-volts.** (1 eV = 1.602 × 10⁻¹⁹ J)

$$ E = \frac{1.030 \times 10^{-18}}{1.602 \times 10^{-19}} = 6.43 \text{ eV} $$

**Step 4 — Compare to typical molecular bond energies.**

| Bond | Energy (eV) |
|---|---|
| C–C | 3.6 |
| C–H | 4.3 |
| C–N | 3.0 |
| C–O | 3.7 |
| Peptide (C–N) | 3.0 |

**Step 5 — Conclusion.**

$$ E_{\text{photon}}(193 \text{ nm}) = 6.43 \text{ eV} \gg E_{\text{C-C}} = 3.6 \text{ eV} $$

Since each photon carries more than 1.7× the energy of a single C–C bond, a single UV photon can cleave a covalent bond without requiring thermal accumulation. This is the physical foundation of **cold photoablation** in the **193 nm ArF Excimer laser**.

## 3.2 Derivation: Ablation Threshold Fluence

The threshold for ablation is reached when the energy deposited equals the energy required for vaporization of a tissue volume.

**Step 1 — Energy per unit volume to heat and vaporize:**

$$ E_{\text{vol}} = \rho \, c \, \Delta T + \rho \, L_v $$

where **ρ** is tissue density (~1000 kg/m³), **c** is specific heat (~3600 J/kg·K for water-rich tissue), **ΔT = 100 − 37 = 63 K**, and **Lᵥ = 2260 kJ/kg** is the latent heat of vaporization.

**Step 2 — Compute sensible heat term:**

$$ \rho c \Delta T = 1000 \times 3600 \times 63 = 2.27 \times 10^8 \text{ J/m}^3 $$

**Step 3 — Compute vaporization term:**

$$ \rho L_v = 1000 \times 2.26 \times 10^6 = 2.26 \times 10^9 \text{ J/m}^3 $$

**Step 4 — Sum:**

$$ E_{\text{vol}} \approx 2.49 \times 10^9 \text{ J/m}^3 = 2.49 \text{ J/mm}^3 $$

**Step 5 — Convert to volumetric fluence using optical penetration depth δ:**

$$ F_{\text{threshold}} = E_{\text{vol}} \times \delta $$

For a water-rich tissue with δ = 10 μm = 10⁻⁵ m:

$$ F_{\text{threshold}} = 2.49 \times 10^9 \times 10^{-5} = 2.49 \times 10^4 \text{ J/m}^2 = 2.49 \text{ J/cm}^2 $$

This matches experimentally measured CO₂ laser ablation thresholds of **~2–4 J/cm²**. ✓

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Laser Selection by Clinical Application

| Clinical Goal | Tissue Target | Chromophore | Optimal λ (nm) | Required Pulse vs τᵣ | Energy Range | Example Laser |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Port-wine stain | Dilated dermal capillaries | Oxyhemoglobin | 577–595 | τ_pulse << 1–10 ms | 6–10 J/cm² | Pulsed dye (585 nm) |
| Hair removal | Hair follicle | Melanin | 700–1000 | 3–30 ms | 20–40 J/cm² | Alexandrite (755), Diode (810) |
| Retinal photocoagulation | Retinal pigment epithelium | Melanin | 488–694 | 100–500 ms | 0.1–1 W | Argon (514), Krypton (647) |
| Posterior capsulotomy | Lens capsule | (breakdown) | 1064 | ns (Q-switched) | 1–5 mJ | Nd:YAG |
| Skin resurfacing | Water (epidermis/dermis) | Water | 10 600 | < 1 ms | 5–15 J/cm² | CO₂ (UltraPulse) |
| PDT tumor kill | PpIX in tumor cells | PpIX | 630 | 100–1000 s | 100 J/cm² | He-Ne / Diode |
| Corneal ablation | Corneal stroma | Tissue proteins | 193 | 10–20 ns | 100–200 mJ/cm² | ArF Excimer |
| Vocal cord polyp | Mucosa | Water | 10 600 | µs–ms | 1–5 W (CW) | CO₂ (microspot) |
| Brain tumor debulking | Deep tissue | Water/hemoglobin | 1064 / 10 600 | CW or long pulse | 10–60 W | Nd:YAG / CO₂ |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model]
**Define selective photothermolysis. State the two essential conditions that a laser pulse must satisfy to achieve it.**

**Model Answer:**
Selective photothermolysis is the principle whereby a laser selectively destroys a target chromophore-bearing structure (e.g., blood vessel, hair follicle) without damaging surrounding tissue.

Two essential conditions:
1. The laser **wavelength (λ)** must be preferentially absorbed by the target chromophore (high μₐ for target, low μₐ for surroundings).
2. The laser **pulse duration (τₚ)** must be **shorter than or equal to the thermal relaxation time (τᵣ)** of the target, so heat is confined.

$$\tau_p \leq \tau_r \approx \frac{d^2}{4\kappa}$$

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model]
**Why is a 193 nm ArF Excimer laser preferred over a 1064 nm Nd:YAG laser for corneal refractive surgery (LASIK)?**

**Model Answer:**
At 193 nm, each photon carries **~6.4 eV**, which exceeds the **~3.5 eV C–C bond energy** of corneal stromal proteins. This allows **photoablative bond breaking** — tissue is removed in micron-thick layers with negligible thermal damage to adjacent cornea. The 1064 nm Nd:YAG photon has only **~1.16 eV**, insufficient to break bonds directly; it instead causes photomechanical disruption (cavitation/shock) suitable for posterior capsulotomy but not for controlled refractive tissue removal. The ArF Excimer can remove tissue at **~0.25 μm per pulse**, ideal for sub-micron precision.

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**With a neat diagram, explain the four types of laser–tissue interactions. Give one clinical application of each.**

**Model Solution:**

1. **Photothermal Interaction:**
   - Absorbed light converts to heat via molecular vibration.
   - Effects by temperature: hyperthermia (40–45 °C), coagulation (45–60 °C), vaporization (100 °C), carbonization (>100 °C).
   - **Application:** CO₂ laser (10 600 nm) cutting and ablation of vocal cord polyps.

2. **Photochemical Interaction:**
   - Photons induce chemical reactions (e.g., singlet oxygen generation in PDT).
   - **Application:** Photodynamic therapy of skin cancers using 5-ALA + 630 nm red light.

3. **Photoablative Interaction:**
   - UV photons break molecular bonds directly (E_photon > bond energy).
   - **Application:** 193 nm Excimer laser for LASIK/PRK corneal reshaping.

4. **Photomechanical Interaction:**
   - Short pulses (ns–fs) generate thermoelastic stress, shock waves, cavitation bubbles.
   - **Application:** Q-switched Nd:YAG for posterior capsulotomy; laser lithotripsy.

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**A dermatology laser targets a dermal blood vessel of diameter 200 μm. Tissue thermal diffusivity κ = 0.13 mm²/s. Calculate the thermal relaxation time. If the laser delivers pulses of 0.5 ms, is selective photothermolysis achieved?**

**Model Solution:**

$$ d = 200 \text{ μm} = 200 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m} $$
$$ \kappa = 0.13 \text{ mm}^2/\text{s} = 0.13 \times 10^{-6} \text{ m}^2/\text{s} $$

Thermal relaxation time:

$$ \tau_r = \frac{d^2}{4\kappa} = \frac{(200 \times 10^{-6})^2}{4 \times 0.13 \times 10^{-6}} $$

$$ \tau_r = \frac{4 \times 10^{-8}}{5.2 \times 10^{-7}} = 0.0769 \text{ s} = 76.9 \text{ ms} $$

Comparison:

$$ \tau_p = 0.5 \text{ ms} \ll \tau_r = 76.9 \text{ ms} $$

Yes, since τₚ < τᵣ, the heat does not have time to diffuse out of the vessel during the pulse. **Selective photothermolysis is achieved.** This is precisely the regime used by the 585 nm pulsed dye laser for port-wine stains.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)

#### (a) [7 Marks] [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Describe the principle of Photodynamic Therapy (PDT). With a block diagram, explain the components of a typical PDT system. Mention any two photosensitizers and their activation wavelengths.**

**Model Solution:**

**Principle:** PDT uses a photosensitizing drug that accumulates preferentially in tumor cells. Upon illumination with a laser at the sensitizer's absorption peak, energy is transferred to molecular oxygen (³O₂) producing cytotoxic **singlet oxygen (¹O₂)** and other reactive oxygen species (ROS) that kill tumor cells via apoptosis and vascular shutdown.

**PDT System Block Diagram:**

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Laser Source 630 nm Diode or He-Ne] --> B[Fiber Optic Coupler]
    B --> C[Microlens or Cylindric Diffuser]
    C --> D[Tumor Site]
    D --> E[Singlet Oxygen ROS Generation]
    E --> F[Tumor Cell Apoptosis and Vascular Damage]
    A --> G[Power Meter and Timer]
    G --> H[Safety Interlock and Dosimetry Software]
```

**Components:**
1. Laser source (matched to sensitizer absorption).
2. Beam delivery (fiber + diffuser for interstitial or surface).
3. Dosimetry (light fluence 50–200 J/cm²; power 100–500 mW).
4. Patient preparation (sensitizer injection 24–72 h prior).

**Photosensitizers:**
| Sensitizer | Activation λ (nm) | Clinical Use |
|---|---|---|
| Photofrin® (porfimer sodium) | 630 | Lung, esophageal, bladder cancers |
| 5-ALA → Protoporphyrin IX (PpIX) | 635 | Skin (basal cell carcinoma), brain glioma |
| Verteporfin | 689 | Age-related macular degeneration (AMD) |
| Temoporfin (Foscan®) | 652 | Head & neck cancers |

#### (b) [7 Marks]
**Compare the use of CO₂ laser (10 600 nm) and Nd:YAG laser (1064 nm) for removal of vocal cord tumors. Justify which is preferred for microsurgical excision of early glottic carcinoma.**

**Model Solution:**

| Parameter | CO₂ (10 600 nm) | Nd:YAG (1064 nm) |
|---|---|---|
| Chromophore | Water (superficial) | Water + protein (deeper) |
| Penetration depth | ~0.1 mm (very shallow) | ~5–10 mm (deep) |
| Primary action | Precise cutting / vaporization | Coagulation, deep heating |
| Beam delivery | Articulated arm / microscope micromanipulator | Silica fiber (cannot go through standard scope) |
| Thermal damage zone | ~50–100 μm | ~2–5 mm |
| Hemostasis | Moderate | Excellent |

**Justification for early glottic carcinoma:**
The CO₂ laser is **preferred** because:
1. Its very shallow penetration (~0.1 mm) provides **micron-level precision** essential for the delicate vocal fold mucosa.
2. The narrow thermal damage zone **preserves the layered microstructure** (epithelium, lamina propria, vocalis muscle) critical for voice quality.
3. Coupled to an operating microscope (micromspot, 250 μm spot), it gives the surgeon **direct visual control** at high magnification.
4. Nd:YAG, with its 5–10 mm penetration, would cause unacceptable deep thermal necrosis and sloughing — disastrous in a vibrating structure only a few millimeters thick.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing photothermal with photoablative** — photoablation is *cold* (UV bond breaking), not heat-based.
> 2. **Forgetting thermal relaxation time** in selective photothermolysis questions — students often state "choose correct wavelength" but omit the pulse-duration criterion.
> 3. **Wrong units on penetration depth** — δ = 1/μₐ is in *meters* if μₐ is in m⁻¹.
> 4. **Mixing chromophore–wavelength pairs** — write clearly: *Hb/HbO₂ → 415/540/577 nm*; *melanin → broad UV–NIR*; *water → IR > 1300 nm*.
> 5. **In PDT questions, omitting the role of oxygen** — without tissue O₂, no singlet oxygen, no kill. PDT is *oxygen-dependent*.
> 6. **Forgetting the safety interlock / aiming beam** in instrument diagrams — examiners often award marks for these "subsidiary" components.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Four interaction regimes:** Photothermal, Photochemical, Photoablative, Photomechanical.
- **Selective photothermolysis** needs (i) wavelength matched to chromophore, (ii) τₚ ≤ τᵣ = d²/(4κ).
- **Beer–Lambert** in tissue: I(z) = I₀ exp(−μₐ z); effective form uses μₑff = √[3μₐ(μₐ+μₛ′)].
- **Photon energy** E(eV) = **1240/λ(nm)**.
- **Chromophore–wavelength quick map:**
  - **Hb / HbO₂** → 415, 532, 577 nm (visible)
  - **Melanin** → 400–1000 nm (broad)
  - **Water** → 10 600 nm (CO₂), 2940 nm (Er:YAG)
  - **PpIX (PDT)** → 630–635 nm
  - **Verteporfin (AMD)** → 689 nm
  - **Corneal stroma** → 193 nm (Excimer)
- **CO₂ laser (10 600 nm):** Microspot cutting, vocal cord, laryngeal, neurosurgical ablation. Shallow, precise.
- **Nd:YAG (1064 nm):** Deep coagulation, posterior capsulotomy (Q-switched), tumor debulking.
- **Argon (488/514 nm):** Retinal photocoagulation via slit-lamp.
- **Pulsed dye (585 nm):** Vascular lesions (port-wine stains, hemangiomas).
- **Alexandrite (755 nm):** Hair removal, pigmented lesions.
- **Excimer (193 nm):** LASIK / PRK — cold photoablation of cornea at ~0.25 μm/pulse.
- **PDT triple requirement:** Photosensitizer + Light of correct λ + Tissue oxygen → Singlet oxygen (¹O₂) → Tumor kill.
- **Anatomical specialty–laser pairings to memorize:**
  - Vocal cords → **CO₂** (microscope)
  - Brain → **CO₂ / Nd:YAG / Thulium** (stereotactic)
  - Dermatology → **Pulsed dye / Alexandrite / CO₂ ultrapulse**
  - Oncology → **Nd:YAG / Diode / PDT**
  - Ophthalmology → **Argon / Nd:YAG / Excimer**
- **Safety:** Always state laser class, MPE, protective eyewear wavelength-specific, and the role of the aiming beam (He-Ne 633 nm) and safety shutter.
- **Numerical skills:** Be ready to compute photon energy, penetration depth, thermal relaxation time, and ablation threshold fluence for a 7–8 mark sub-question.
